EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved across Erie County, Pennsylvania as a cold front moved across the area. The thunderstorm caused many downed trees and was accompanied by quarter size hail on Old Frisbeetown Rd east of Mill Village.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported quarter size hail on Old Frisbeetown Road east of Mill Village.  Numerous trees and power lines were also brought down from strong thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance combined with a low pressure system that rode along an offshore cold front to bring the first measurable snow of the season for most of Eastern Pennsylvania (except right along the Delaware River) during the evening and overnight of the 13th. While many accumulations were less than an inch, one to four inches of snow fell over the higher terrain. Since the ground was warm, roadway accumulations were limited to lesser traveled roads and the snow started to melt once it stopped falling. Less traveled roadways in Berks County were described as slippery.||Over the Poconos, precipitation fell as snow from the late afternoon on the 13th until shortly after midnight EST on the 14th. Elsewhere, precipitation started as rain during the middle afternoon and changed over to snow during the late afternoon in Berks County, around 6 p.m. EST in the Lehigh Valley and the northwest suburbs of Philadelphia and during the middle of the evening in and near the local Philadelphia area. The snow ended during the middle to late evening on the 13th. ||Representative snowfall included 3.7 inches in Williams Township (Northampton County), 3.3 inches in Salisbury Township (Lehigh County), 2.5 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County) and Fleetwood and Huffs Church (Berks County), 2.1 inches at West Caln Township (Chester County), 2.0 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 1.8 inches in Elverson (Chester County), 1.6 inches in Mohnton (Berks County), 1.5 inches in Souderton (Montgomery County) and 0.5 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. ||The snow was caused by a low pressure system that formed on a cold front over the Carolinas on the 12th. The same cold front moved through Pennsylvania that day and stalled offshore. This low pressure system initially moved slowly and only reached the North Carolina coastal waters mid day on the 13th. From there it progressed northeast faster and passed east of New Jersey early on the 14th. An upper level jet streak also passed across the region overnight on the 13th and both combined to bring the snow into Eastern Pennsylvania.

An intense low pressure that passed east of New Jersey on the 2nd combined with an approaching high pressure system to bring strong northwest winds into Eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the late morning and early afternoon of the 2nd. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph. The strong winds knocked down weak trees, tree limbs and wires and caused scattered power outages. This was particularly true in southeast Pennsylvania where many trees still had leaves on them as October was unseasonably warm.  In Delaware County, a shed was tossed in Media and two vehicles were damaged by a downed tree in Havertown.  In Philadelphia, newspaper dispensers were knocked over. About 22,600 homes and businesses lost power, 20,000 in PECO Energy's service area in southeastern Pennsylvania.  Most of the outages were in Delaware and Montgomery Counties. The majority of customers had service restored the evening of the 2nd. The strong winds also caused arrival delays of about 90 minutes at Philadelphia International Airport. ||Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in Bushkill Township (Northampton County),  49 mph in Lenhartsville (Berks County), 48 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport,  47 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 45 mph in West Chester (Chester County), 44 mph in Perkasie (Bucks County), 43 mph in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and 41 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.  ||A developing low pressure over the Carolinas moved offshore on the morning of November 1st. As it passed east of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina during the early evening of November 1st, the pressure gradient (difference) between it and a high pressure system in the Mississippi Valley started to increase and winds became stronger. This pressure difference over Eastern Pennsylvania maximized during the late morning of the 2nd. At 7 a.m. EST on November 2nd, the low pressure system was well east of the New Jersey coast and as it reached the coastal waters east of Massachusetts that afternoon, strong winds ceased across Eastern Pennsylvania.

Another surge of frigid air impacted Central Pennsylvania with extreme cold and dangerous wind chills between 28-29 January 2014. The winds were not quite as strong as the cold outbreak that occurred a few weeks earlier on 6-8 January 2014, but wind chills still managed to drop to around -30��F. Minimum temperatures averaged between 0�� and -20��F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures generally varied between -0�� and -20��F with wind chills around -30��F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of sleet and freezing rain and even some snow in the Poconos occurred at the onset of precipitation associated with a strong nor'easter during the morning of the 9th.  Snow and sleet accumulations in the Poconos averaged around an inch or less while ice accumulations from the Philadelphia northern suburbs through the Poconos averaged between one-tenth and two-tenths of an inch.  Untreated roadways and walkways were hazardous.  Icy roadways were reported in Marshalls Creek (Monroe County). ||In the Poconos, precipitation started as snow between 4 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST on the 9th. It changed over to freezing rain in all areas between 6 a.m. EST and 730 a.m. EST and to plain rain by 9 a.m. EST on the 9th.  In the Philadelphia northern suburbs, Berks County and the Lehigh Valley, precipitation started briefly as sleet from south to north between 1 a.m. EST and 4 a.m. EST on the 9th. Precipitation then changed to freezing rain within an hour of its starting time and then to plain rain between 4 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST on the 9th.||Snowfall included 1.5 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 1.0 inch in Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 0.8 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County) and 0.2 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County).  Ice accumulations included .15 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County) and .05 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County).||The cold air, especially near the surface, was supplied by a high pressure system that moved across the southern tier of Canada and reached the Canadian Maritimes overnight on the 8th. Meanwhile, a low pressure system formed off the Carolina coastal waters on the 8th. It tracked northward and deepened as it reached the Delmarva coastal waters at 7 a.m. EST on the 9th. The low pressure system deepened 12 millibars in six hours and was off the New Jersey coastal waters at 1 p.m. EST on the 9th. The track and rapid intensification helped bring warmer air near the surface into Eastern Pennsylvania and changed precipitation to plain rain. The low pressure system stopped intensifying and drifted off to the northeast reaching the Rhode Island coastal waters during the evening of the 9th and eastern Massachusetts around Midnight EST on the 10th.

A low pressure system developed over the northern Gulf of Mexico and intensified as it headed toward the northeast U.S. A coastal low developed along the Atlantic seaboard on November 26th. This system spread snow, heavy at times, into northeast Pennsylvania during the late morning and afternoon hours of Wednesday, November 26th. The highest snowfall total of 10.2 inches was reported in Wyoming County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5-9 inches across the county. The highest amount of 9 inches fell 5 miles north-northeast of Towanda.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of Tuesday, September 2nd. This front acted on a moist and unstable air mass, resulting in the development of a severe thunderstorm in northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through the Poconos during the evening hours of Saturday, September 6th. This front resulted in an isolated severe thunderstorm over Luzerne County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist onshore and upslope flow along a developing coastal front produced an icy mixture of sleet and freezing rain over the higher terrain of the Poconos during the first half of the day on the 17th. Precipitation started as sleet shortly after Midnight EST on the 17th. The precipitation changed to plain rain in the valleys, but freezing rain over the higher terrain around 3 a.m. EST on the 17th. The precipitation slowly changed to rain as warmer air moved into the Poconos and the freezing rain was over by 10 a.m. EST.  Ice accumulations averaged less than one-tenth of an inch and mostly were reported on exposed surfaces: vehicles, decks and railings.

A very narrow lake effect snow band moved through Lehigh County during the evening of the 23rd. This narrow band produced localized snowfall amounts of up to around 2 inches in the county and caused roadways to become snow covered and hazardous again. Snowfall totals included 2.1 inches in New Tripoli and 1.2 inches in Schnecksville.

Snow and sleet accumulations in the Poconos averaged around an inch or less while ice accumulations from the Philadelphia northern suburbs through the Poconos averaged between one-tenth and two-tenths of an inch.  Untreated roadways and walkways were hazardous.  Icy roadways were reported in Marshalls Creek (Monroe County). ||In the Poconos, precipitation started as snow between 4 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST on the 9th. It changed over to freezing rain in all areas between 6 a.m. EST and 730 a.m. EST and to plain rain by 9 a.m. EST on the 9th.  In the Philadelphia northern suburbs, Berks County and the Lehigh Valley, precipitation started briefly as sleet from south to north between 1 a.m. EST and 4 a.m. EST on the 9th. Precipitation then changed to freezing rain within an hour of its starting time and then to plain rain between 4 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST on the 9th.||Snowfall included 1.5 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 1.0 inch in Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 0.8 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County) and 0.2 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County).  Ice accumulations included .15 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County) and .05 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County).||The cold air, especially near the surface, was supplied by a high pressure system that moved across the southern tier of Canada and reached the Canadian Maritimes overnight on the 8th. Meanwhile, a low pressure system formed off the Carolina coastal waters on the 8th. It tracked northward and deepened as it reached the Delmarva coastal waters at 7 a.m. EST on the 9th. The low pressure system deepened 12 millibars in six hours and was off the New Jersey coastal waters at 1 p.m. EST on the 9th. The track and rapid intensification helped bring warmer air near the surface into Eastern Pennsylvania and changed precipitation to plain rain. The low pressure system stopped intensifying and drifted off to the northeast reaching the Rhode Island coastal waters during the evening of the 9th and eastern Massachusetts around Midnight EST on the 10th.

A wintry mix of precipitation affected the Poconos, Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the far northern Philadelphia suburbs from the late morning into the evening of the 2nd. A combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred and caused slippery traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Snow and sleet accumulations were less than an inch and mainly confined to the Poconos while ice accumulations were around one tenth of an inch or less.||Precipitation started as snow in nearly all areas from south to north during the late morning and early afternoon of the 2nd. It changed over to sleet from south to north during the first half of the afternoon and then to freezing rain during the middle of the afternoon.  Precipitation changed to plain rain during the first half of the evening, except in the Poconos where the transition took the entire evening into the very early morning of the 3rd.  ||Representative snow and sleet accumulations included 0.5 inches in Tannersville (Monroe County), 0.4 inches in Bowmanstown (Carbon County) and 0.3 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County) and Palmerton (Carbon County). ||The wintry mix was caused by double barrel low pressure systems. During the afternoon of the 2nd, one low pressure system was located in the Tennessee Valley, while the second was off the Delmarva Peninsula. The Delmarva low pressure system became the stronger low overnight and managed to bring in warmer air at the surface into Eastern Pennsylvania. As the low pressure system passed south of the Twin Forks of Long Island, the rain ended before sunrise on the 3rd.

Snow wrapping around the nor'easter dropped 1 to 4 inches of snow across the Poconos mainly during the morning of the 10th and 1 to 3 inches in parts of Chester County overnight on the 10th. While snow did also fall elsewhere in Eastern Pennsylvania, because most of it fell during the daytime hours, temperatures were higher (and above freezing), many accumulations were less than an inch. Untreated roadways, mainly over the higher terrain became slippery.||Representative snowfall included 3.8 inches in Long Pond (Monroe County), 3.2 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 3.1 inches in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County), 1.9 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 1.8 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 1.6 inches in Elverson (Chester County), 1.5 inches in Reeders (Monroe County) and 1.3 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County). ||The nor'easter remained nearly stationary over southeastern Massachusetts during the daytime hours of the 10th, before slowly drifting offshore to the northeast that night.

An intense low pressure that passed east of New Jersey on the 2nd combined with an approaching high pressure system to bring strong northwest winds into Eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the late morning and early afternoon of the 2nd. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph. The strong winds knocked down weak trees, tree limbs and wires and caused scattered power outages. This was particularly true in southeast Pennsylvania where many trees still had leaves on them as October was unseasonably warm.  In Delaware County, a shed was tossed in Media and two vehicles were damaged by a downed tree in Havertown.  In Philadelphia, newspaper dispensers were knocked over. About 22,600 homes and businesses lost power, 20,000 in PECO Energy's service area in southeastern Pennsylvania.  Most of the outages were in Delaware and Montgomery Counties. The majority of customers had service restored the evening of the 2nd. The strong winds also caused arrival delays of about 90 minutes at Philadelphia International Airport. ||Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in Bushkill Township (Northampton County),  49 mph in Lenhartsville (Berks County), 48 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport,  47 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 45 mph in West Chester (Chester County), 44 mph in Perkasie (Bucks County), 43 mph in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and 41 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.  ||A developing low pressure over the Carolinas moved offshore on the morning of November 1st. As it passed east of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina during the early evening of November 1st, the pressure gradient (difference) between it and a high pressure system in the Mississippi Valley started to increase and winds became stronger. This pressure difference over Eastern Pennsylvania maximized during the late morning of the 2nd. At 7 a.m. EST on November 2nd, the low pressure system was well east of the New Jersey coast and as it reached the coastal waters east of Massachusetts that afternoon, strong winds ceased across Eastern Pennsylvania.

A low pressure system developed over the northern Gulf of Mexico and intensified as it headed toward the northeast U.S. A coastal low developed along the Atlantic seaboard on November 26th. This system spread snow, heavy at times, into northeast Pennsylvania during the late morning and afternoon hours of Wednesday, November 26th. The highest snowfall total of 10.2 inches was reported in Wyoming County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 8-10 inches across the county. The highest amount of 10.2 inches fell in Falls Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across southern Canada dragging a cold front across western Pennsylvania. Showers and thunderstorms developed in advance of the front. A couple of the stronger storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed a tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching low pressure system from the Ohio Valley combined with a departing high pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes to give the Poconos an icy mixture of sleet and freezing rain during the afternoon and into the evening of the 5th. Precipitation started as sleet during the early afternoon and transitioned to freezing rain by the middle of the afternoon of the 5th.  As the high pressure system moved farther east and the low pressure system neared, the freezing rain changed over to rain by the middle of the evening on the 5th.  Prior to the transition, one to two tenths of an inch of ice accumulated on exposed surfaces. This made traveling and walking on untreated surfaces hazardous. Rain continued overnight on the 5th and through the day on the 6th. ||In Monroe County, a ten vehicle chain reaction accident on eastbound Interstate 80 in Tunkhannock Township resulted in four injuries.  Other accidents were reported in Tobyhanna (Monroe County), Chestnuthill Township (Monroe County) and in Nesquehoning (Carbon County). The latter accident resulted in another injury.

In Delaware County, a shed was tossed in Media and two vehicles were damaged by a downed tree in Havertown.  In Philadelphia, newspaper dispensers were knocked over. About 22,600 homes and businesses lost power, 20,000 in PECO Energy's service area in southeastern Pennsylvania.  Most of the outages were in Delaware and Montgomery Counties. The majority of customers had service restored the evening of the 2nd. The strong winds also caused arrival delays of about 90 minutes at Philadelphia International Airport. ||Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in Bushkill Township (Northampton County),  49 mph in Lenhartsville (Berks County), 48 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport,  47 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 45 mph in West Chester (Chester County), 44 mph in Perkasie (Bucks County), 43 mph in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and 41 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.  ||A developing low pressure over the Carolinas moved offshore on the morning of November 1st. As it passed east of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina during the early evening of November 1st, the pressure gradient (difference) between it and a high pressure system in the Mississippi Valley started to increase and winds became stronger. This pressure difference over Eastern Pennsylvania maximized during the late morning of the 2nd. At 7 a.m. EST on November 2nd, the low pressure system was well east of the New Jersey coast and as it reached the coastal waters east of Massachusetts that afternoon, strong winds ceased across Eastern Pennsylvania.

A cold front and an upper level disturbance moving across northeast Pennsylvania acted on an unstable airmass to produce a severe thunderstorm during the late afternoon.

A low pressure system that developed along a frontal boundary helped produce clusters of showers and some strong to locally severe thunderstorms that moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning of the 19th. Several homes were also struck by lightning.  Approximately 4,500 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes occurred in the Mount Holly warning and forecast area as the thunderstorms moved through. Bucks County suffered the most outages in the area from the combined lightning and wind from the thunderstorms. About 30,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county. This ended the brief hot spell across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and subsequent fire totally consumed an abandoned home along Martin Road in Highland Township. The house was fully consumed by flames when fire crews arrived and it took two companies more than an hour to get the fire under control.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system located over southern Ontario along with surface boundaries in the vicinity of northeast Pennsylvania resulted in severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening hours.  These storms developed in an unstable airmass with severe thunderstorms producing large hail and wind damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another surge of frigid air impacted Central Pennsylvania with extreme cold and dangerous wind chills between 28-29 January 2014. The winds were not quite as strong as the cold outbreak that occurred a few weeks earlier on 6-8 January 2014, but wind chills still managed to drop to around -30��F. Minimum temperatures averaged between 0�� and -20��F.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures generally varied between -0�� and -20��F with wind chills around -30��F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving low pressure system streaked across the Central Appalachians and southern Mid-Atlantic states on 2-3 February 2014, producing a stripe of moderate to heavy snow on the northwest side of the low track over southern Pennsylvania. The heavy snowfall was aided by the placement of a coupled upper-level jet and strong low-mid level warm advection/isentropic lift. A general 4 to 8 inches of snow was common along and south of the US22/I-81 corridors, with 2-4 inches to the north from US22 to I-80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The interaction between a strong arctic cold front and coastal low pressure system produced heavy snow across portions of Central Pennsylvania on 2 January 2014. The longer-duration snow across the Northern Mountains, influenced primarily by the arctic front, fell mostly during the morning and afternoon hours. The bulk of the heavy snow in the Lower Susquehanna Valley fell during the late evening with occasional 1 inch/hour snowfall rates, as the surface low pressure system strengthened off the Mid-Atlantic coast. Very cold air surged into the region behind the arctic front, causing blowing snow and frigid wind chills over the northern tier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of light to moderate snow produced general accumulations between 4 and 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over New England spread cold air and precipitation west into northwestern Pennsylvania.  The relatively warm waters of Lake Erie helped enhance the snowfall.  The snow began a couple of hours before daybreak and increased in intensity during the morning hours.   Periods of heavy snow then continued through the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east during the evening hours.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  North to northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph during this event causing near whiteout conditions at times and considerable drifting.  Snowfall totals for this event were the greatest on the higher terrain inland from Lake Erie.  Snowfall totals south of Interstate 90 ranged from 6 to 8 inches in Erie County with a peak of 8.7 inches at Colt Station at the eastern end of the county.  Around 6 inches of snow was reported in northern and eastern Crawford County.  Travel was severely hampered during this event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of snow on the ground, a moist southerly flow and a slowly northward moving warm front all helped produce dense fog across most of Eastern Pennsylvania from the evening of the 5th into the overnight.    Some overnight improvement occurred around the local Philadelphia area as the warm front advanced farther to the north. The dense fog dispersed on the morning of the 6th as a strong cold front moved across the state.

A high pressure system over Pennsylvania combined with moist conditions at the surface from the rain earlier in the day on the 14th to produce widespread dense fog and even some black ice from the middle of the evening on the 14th through the morning on the 15th. Dense fog persisted longer in Upper Bucks County and the Lehigh Valley, into the afternoon of the 15th  A weak cold front that passed through the area during the early evening of the 15th prevented a second night of widespread dense fog from occurring. Black ice occurred from the far northwest Philadelphia suburbs northwestward through Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. Untreated roadways were hazardous.

A steady light to moderate rain event over approximately twenty-four hours coupled with some snow melt caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some creeks in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos on the 11th. Rain began during the evening of the 10th, fell at its heaviest during the morning and early afternoon on the 11th and ended during the evening of the 11th. Storm totals averaged around 1.5 inches, except reached up to 2.5 inches in the Poconos. The rain was caused by a milder and moist southwest flow of air preceding a cold front. The cold front's low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes and the Saint Lawrence River Valley on the 11th and dragged its cold front through Pennsylvania during the middle of the evening on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of snow melt and rain caused poor drainage and some creek flooding in southern Monroe County. In Stroudsburg, flooding was reported along McMichael's Creek in the vicinity of the 7th Street Bridge. In Hamilton Township, flooding was reported along Metzgar Road in the vicinity of the Lake Creek and Mackes Street. Event precipitation totals included 2.50 inches in East Stroudsburg, 2.32 inches in Saylorsburg and 1.69 inches in Mount Pocono.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds behind the departing strong low pressure system coupled with another arctic air mass dropped low temperatures on the morning of the 22nd around zero (well below zero in the Poconos) and produced wind chill factors as low as around 15 degrees below zero, except around 25 degrees below zero in the Poconos.  In some places, low temperatures were as cold as January 7th and wind chill factors came close to matching that morning. The unseasonably cold weather contributed to four deaths in the local Philadelphia area.  In Philadelphia, a 30 year-old homeless man was found dead in a snow drift at the intersection of 9th Street and Ridge Avenue.  In Delaware County, a 89-year-old man and 92-year-old woman in Ridley Park were found unresponsive outdoors as was a 67-year-old man in Swarthmore. ||Lowest hourly wind chills included 29 degrees below zero in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 17 degrees below zero at the Philadelphia International Airport and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 16 degrees below zero at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 15 degrees below zero in Reading (Berks County) and Doylestown (Bucks County).||Actual low temperatures included 4 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport. This matched January 7th for the lowest temperature of the winter season. The low temperature of 9 degrees below zero in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) also matched the low on January 7th. The lowest reading of the winter season there occurred on February 12th (10 below zero). Other low temperatures that morning included 5 degrees below zero in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 1 degree below zero at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, zero in Doylestown (Bucks County) and Glenmoore (Chester County), 1 degree above zero in Pottstown (Montgomery County) and Kennett Square (Chester County), 2 degrees above zero in Reading (Berks County), Horsham (Montgomery County) and Nazareth (Northampton County), 3 degrees above zero in Langhorne (Bucks County) and 4 degrees above zero in Aston (Delaware County).

A period of freezing rain affected Eastern Pennsylvania on the 5th and caused hazardous traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Ice accumulations averaged one to two tenths. Precipitation started briefly as sleet in most areas before quickly transitioning to freezing rain and then eventually to some spotty light rain. Freezing rain occurred during the morning of the 5th in Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, in Berks County and the far northwestern Philadelphia suburbs from the mid morning into the afternoon, in the Lehigh Valley from the late morning through the early evening and in the valleys of the Poconos from the afternoon through the evening of the 5th. The air mass was so cold and dense that higher terrain locations in the Poconos just experienced plain rain.  ||Representative ice accumulations included two tenths of an inch in Shillington (Berks County) and East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), one tenth of an inch in East Coventry Township (Chester County), Doylestown (Bucks County), the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Forks Township (Northampton County) and one twentieth of an inch at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The freezing rain was caused by the return flow off the Atlantic Ocean assisted by a weak stationary front over the Middle Atlantic States. While the air mass aloft warmed, the arctic air mass in place near the surface kept temperatures below freezing as precipitation moved through.

A high pressure system that moved over Pennsylvania coupled with fresh snow cover from the winter storm on the 2nd and 3rd gave the area one of its coldest winter mornings in years. This was the first of three arctic blasts in the state during the month.  While this was the coldest morning of the winter for more rural areas that are normally colder on calm wind nights, it was not the harshest.  Because the high pressure system was over the region, wind chill factors and actual air temperatures were nearly the same. This was not the case a few days later and again around the 22nd of January. ||The low temperature reached 8 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, the coldest morning in Philadelphia since January 24, 2011.  The low temperature at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was a daily record breaking 4 degrees below zero, the coldest morning of the winter and the coldest since the same minimum temperature on January 24, 2011.  The low temperature reached 1 degree above zero in Reading (Berks County), the coldest morning since it was zero degrees on January 24, 2011.  The low temperature reached 9 degrees below zero in Mount Pocono, this tied January 22nd as the coldest morning of January and the coldest day since January 17, 2009 when the low temperature reached 14 degrees below zero.||Other low temperatures on the morning of the 4th included 9 degrees below zero in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 5 degrees below zero in Martins Creek (Northampton County) and Glenmoore (Chester County), 4 degrees below zero in West Chester (Chester County), 3 degrees below zero in Lehighton (Carbon County) and Boyertown (Berks County), 2 degrees below zero in Doylestown (Bucks County) and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 1 degree in Warrington (Bucks County), 2 degrees in Horsham (Montgomery County) and 4 degrees in Havertown (Delaware County).

A period of freezing rain affected Eastern Pennsylvania on the 5th and caused hazardous traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Ice accumulations averaged one to two tenths. Precipitation started briefly as sleet in most areas before quickly transitioning to freezing rain and then eventually to some spotty light rain. Freezing rain occurred during the morning of the 5th in Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, in Berks County and the far northwestern Philadelphia suburbs from the mid morning into the afternoon, in the Lehigh Valley from the late morning through the early evening and in the valleys of the Poconos from the afternoon through the evening of the 5th. The air mass was so cold and dense that higher terrain locations in the Poconos just experienced plain rain.  ||Representative ice accumulations included two tenths of an inch in Shillington (Berks County) and East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), one tenth of an inch in East Coventry Township (Chester County), Doylestown (Bucks County), the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Forks Township (Northampton County) and one twentieth of an inch at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The freezing rain was caused by the return flow off the Atlantic Ocean assisted by a weak stationary front over the Middle Atlantic States. While the air mass aloft warmed, the arctic air mass in place near the surface kept temperatures below freezing as precipitation moved through.

The very persistent and strong west winds that started during the day on the 6th caused blowout tide conditions during the daytime low tide cycle on the 7th along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries in Pennsylvania. In Philadelphia, the low tide reached 2.03 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tide conditions start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water. The west wind decreased overnight and the subsequent low tide cycle fell just short of reaching blowout tide conditions again.

A deformation band of moderate to heavy snow persisted across the Middle Susquehanna Valley from the morning hours of December 11th through the mid afternoon hours. The band of snow was associated with deep low pressure off the Mid-Atlantic coast, which pulled moisture back into Central Pennsylvania. In addition, lake effect and orographic enhancement caused heavy snow in Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The persistent band of moderate to heavy snow produced a stripe of 6 to 12 inches of snow by the early afternoon on 12/11. Lake effect and terrain enchantment both contributed to the locally heavy amounts. Gusty winds caused significant blowing and drifting and reduced visibility.

The unseasonably cold arctic air mass resulted in ice jam flooding along the Delaware River north of the jam in Bucks County and along the Lehigh River in Northampton County before its confluence with the Delaware River on January 8th and 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The unseasonably cold arctic air mass resulted in ice jam flooding along the Delaware River north of the jam in Bucks County on January 8th and 9th. The ice jam started to form near the U.S. Route 1 Bridge on the night of the 7th and flooding began on the 8th. The flooding caused the closure of River Road (State Route 32) from Black Rock Road in Lower Makefield Township south through to East Trenton Avenue/Calhoun Street Bridge in Morrisville Borough. The road was re-opened at Noon EST on January 9th. The flooding caused a dual problem because of the low temperatures. As roadways flooded, they also became icy. Islands in the middle of the Delaware River were completely submerged. The Delaware River in Trenton was above its 20 foot flood stage at times from 550 p.m. EST on the 8th through 800 a.m. EST on the 9th. It crested at 20.76 feet at 730 p.m. EST on the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across east central and southeast Pennsylvania on the 21st and caused scores of accidents and tremendous traffic tie-ups on the afternoon of the 21st as well as least one storm related death.  Hundreds of flights were cancelled at the Philadelphia International Airport and many civic events were cancelled. An 87-year-old man in Wallingford (Delaware County) died after shoveling snow.||The heavy snow was caused by a quick moving clipper system tracked out of the Great Lakes region and began transferring its energy to the developing coastal low, resulting in rapid intensification. This strengthening low pressure moved northeast along the frontal boundary through the afternoon of the 21st, allowing multiple bands of heavy snow to traverse across portions of southeastern Pennsylvania. By late that day, the coastal low emerged east of the Delmarva Peninsula and quickly moved off towards New England, bringing an end to the snow from west to east across the region during the evening to overnight of the 21st to 22nd. ||Along and near the Interstate 95 corridor, temperatures were cold enough for rapid accumulation, and a narrow band of heavy snow developed in this vicinity.  Within this general area, snowfall rates reached 2 to 3 inches per hour for a time during the midday hours of the 21st. Another band of heavy snow developed during the evening of the 21st over the northwestern Philadelphia suburbs. Snowfall totals ranged from around 12 inches  in the local Philadelphia area, 6 to 10 inches in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs, around 6 inches in the Lehigh Valley and Berks County and 1 to 4 inches in the Poconos. ||In the greater Philadelphia area, the heavy banding snow led to the early dismissal of businesses, closing of malls and government offices and any schools that were open. This led to tremendous delays on roadways as traffic congestion complicated snow removal activities. Two hour or long commutes were common. The snow caused delays on SEPTA commuter rail service and forced the suspension or re-routing of about 30 bus routes.  Philadelphia International Airport managed to keep one runway open, but hundreds of flights were cancelled nonetheless. About 155 passengers spent the night at the airport. The Philadelphia Flyers-Carolina Hurricanes NHL hockey game was postponed. The effects of the heavy snow in southeast Pennsylvania continued the next day. Schools were closed. About 100 flights were cancelled at the Philadelphia International Airport. Major tourist attractions in Philadelphia were closed.  ||Representative snowfall totals included 13.9 inches in Clifton Heights (Delaware County), 13.5 inches at the Philadelphia International An arctic cold front crossed the Upper Ohio Valley on the 6th, bringing record low temperatures and extreme wind chills the morning of the 7th. It was the coldest January 7th on record in Pittsburgh with a low temperature of 9 below zero, and a high temperature of 4 above zero. Across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland, low temperatures ranged from 5 to 15 degrees below zero the morning of the 7th, with the lowest wind chill readings from 25 to 55 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low temperature of 9 degrees below zero with a wind chill of 31 degrees below zero was reported at the Pittsburgh International Airport, with a low temperature of 12 degrees below zero and a wind chill of 40 degrees below zero near Jefferson. Both occurred on the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked across the northern Great Lakes on January 26 and 27th, pulling an arctic cold front east across the area during the early morning hours of January 28th. Temperatures ahead of the front were in the upper 30s and lower 40s, falling through the day on the 27th with dangerously cold wind chill values developing during the early morning hours of the 28th. West to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with temperatures as low as -11 degrees to produce the wind chill conditions. Meadville recorded wind chill values as low as -26 degrees around 7 am on the 28th. Temperatures only recovered into the single digits during the day on the 28th and then fell to sub zero values again overnight. Nearly all schools were closed on the 28th with most school also closed on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dangerously cold air mass settled over northwest Pennsylvania from January 28th with wind chill values of -25 to -30 degrees during the early morning hours of January 28th. The wind chill dropped to -26 at Meadville around 7 am with west to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph. Conditions started to improve by late morning as temperatures gradually recovered into the single digits but dropped below zero again overnight. All area school were closed on January 28th and most were closed on January 29th.

Heavy snow fell across east central and southeast Pennsylvania on the 21st and caused scores of accidents and tremendous traffic tie-ups on the afternoon of the 21st as well as least one storm related death.  Hundreds of flights were cancelled at the Philadelphia International Airport and many civic events were cancelled. An 87-year-old man in Wallingford (Delaware County) died after shoveling snow.||The heavy snow was caused by a quick moving clipper system tracked out of the Great Lakes region and began transferring its energy to the developing coastal low, resulting in rapid intensification. This strengthening low pressure moved northeast along the frontal boundary through the afternoon of the 21st, allowing multiple bands of heavy snow to traverse across portions of southeastern Pennsylvania. One of the harshest arctic outbreaks in years occurred in Eastern Pennsylvania on the 7th.  Record calendar day low temperatures occurred and combined with strong northwest winds produced wind chill factors as low as around 30 degrees below zero in the Poconos and around 20 degrees below zero elsewhere. High temperatures struggled to get above zero in the Poconos and reach double digit figures elsewhere. The excessive cold  caused many schools and universities to either cancel classes or have delayed openings.||The severe cold led to Pennsylvania Governor Tom Corbett signed a Proclamation of Propane and Heating Oil Emergency��� to waive federal and state motor carrier regulations related to hours of service for drivers of commercial motor vehicles transporting propane gas and heating oil in Pennsylvania. AAA Mid-Atlantic reported an 81 percent increase in service calls, mainly for dead batteries. Amtrak reported extensive delays in its rail service. The cold weather also affected SEPTA Regional Rail service in the Philadelphia area as there were delays on the Paoli/Thorndale line. The cold weather strained power supplies. PJM Interconnection, the agency that oversees the electric grid supplying the region, said electricity suppliers were struggling to keep up with surging demand as the cold forced some power plants to shut. An all-time winter record usage was recorded at 8 a.m. EST on the 7th, 138,600 megawatts surpassing the previous record from 2007. Utilities asked their customers where possible to switch to diesel or fuel oil.  Three thousand homeless people in Philadelphia were sheltered.| |The low temperature of 4 degrees above zero at the Philadelphia International Airport was the coldest of the winter and the coldest air temperature day since January 24, 2005 when the low reached 3 degrees above zero. The low temperature of zero at Reading (Berks County) was the coldest of the winter season and matched the low last set on January 24, 2011. The low temperatures of 8 degrees below zero in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and one degree below zero at the Lehigh Valley International Airport were higher than the morning of January 4th. ||Other actual low temperatures included 3 degrees below zero in Lehighton (Carbon County) and Fleetwood (Berks County), 2 degrees below zero in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), Glenmoore (Chester County) and Macungie (Lehigh County), 1 degree below zero in Easton (Northampton County), zero in West Chester (Chester County) and Perkasie (Bucks County), 1 degree above zero in Newtown Square (Delaware County) and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 2 degrees above zero in Horsham (Montgomery County) and 3 degrees above zero in Langhorne (Bucks County). ||Lowest hourly wind chill factors included (they all occurred during the morning of the 7th) 32 degrees below zero in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 23 degrees below zero at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 22 degrees below zero in Doylestown (Bucks County) 21 degrees below zero in Reading (Berks County) and Coatesville (Chester County) and 18 degrees below zero at the Philadelphia International Airport.

A period of freezing rain affected Eastern Pennsylvania on the 5th and caused hazardous traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Ice accumulations averaged one to two tenths. Precipitation started briefly as sleet in most areas before quickly transitioning to freezing rain and then eventually to some spotty light rain. Freezing rain occurred during the morning of the 5th in Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, in Berks County and the far northwestern Philadelphia suburbs from the mid morning into the afternoon, in the Lehigh Valley from the late morning through the early evening and in the valleys of the Poconos from the afternoon through the evening of the 5th. The air mass was so cold and dense that higher terrain locations in the Poconos just experienced plain rain.  ||Representative ice accumulations included two tenths of an inch in Shillington (Berks County) and East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), one tenth of an inch in East Coventry Township (Chester County), Doylestown (Bucks County), the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Forks Township (Northampton County) and one twentieth of an inch at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The freezing rain was caused by the return flow off the Atlantic Ocean assisted by a weak stationary front over the Middle Atlantic States. While the air mass aloft warmed, the arctic air mass in place near the surface kept temperatures below freezing as precipitation moved through.

A high pressure system that moved over Pennsylvania coupled with fresh snow cover from the winter storm on the 2nd and 3rd gave the area one of its coldest winter mornings in years. This was the first of three arctic blasts in the state during the month.  While this was the coldest morning of the winter for more rural areas that are normally colder on calm wind nights, it was not the harshest.  Because the high pressure system was over the region, wind chill factors and actual air temperatures were nearly the same. This was not the case a few days later and again around the 22nd of January. ||The low temperature reached 8 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, the coldest morning in Philadelphia since January 24, 2011.  The low temperature at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was a daily record breaking 4 degrees below zero, the coldest morning of the winter and the coldest since the same minimum temperature on January 24, 2011.  The low temperature reached 1 degree above zero in Reading (Berks County), the coldest morning since it was zero degrees on January 24, 2011.  The low temperature reached 9 degrees below zero in Mount Pocono, this tied January 22nd as the coldest morning of January and the coldest day since January 17, 2009 when the low temperature reached 14 degrees below zero.||Other low temperatures on the morning of the 4th included 9 degrees below zero in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 5 degrees below zero in Martins Creek (Northampton County) and Glenmoore (Chester County), 4 degrees below zero in West Chester (Chester County), 3 degrees below zero in Lehighton (Carbon County) and Boyertown (Berks County), 2 degrees below zero in Doylestown (Bucks County) and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 1 degree in Warrington (Bucks County), 2 degrees in Horsham (Montgomery County) and 4 degrees in Havertown (Delaware County).

An arctic cold front crossed the Upper Ohio Valley on the 6th, bringing record low temperatures and extreme wind chills the morning of the 7th. It was the coldest January 7th on record in Pittsburgh with a low temperature of 9 below zero, and a high temperature of 4 above zero. Across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland, low temperatures ranged from 5 to 15 degrees below zero the morning of the 7th, with the lowest wind chill readings from 25 to 55 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low temperature of 14 degrees below zero with a wind chill of 39 degrees below zero was recorded near Rural Valley the morning of the 7th.

A high pressure system over Pennsylvania combined with moist conditions at the surface from the rain earlier in the day on the 14th to produce widespread dense fog and even some black ice from the middle of the evening on the 14th through the morning on the 15th. Black ice occurred from the far northwest Philadelphia suburbs northwestward through Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. Untreated roadways were hazardous.

A significant winter storm impacted Central Pennsylvania from late on 4 February into the afternoon on Wednesday 5 February 2014. A wintry mix of heavy snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain occurred across the region from a dynamic low pressure system and upper-level energy that shifted from the Ohio Valley to the Mid-Atlantic coast. The snow started during the late evening on 2/4 across the western Alleghenies before changing to sleet and freezing rain during the pre-dawn and early morning hours on 2/5. Sleet quickly changed to all freezing rain over the southern tier counties, where widespread heavy icing (0.25-0.50 inch) was observed.  The greatest ice accumulations were focused over the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation ended by the early afternoon on 2/5. Total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches over a relatively narrow sw-ne stripe through the zones located just north of I-80. Locally higher amounts close to a foot were observed in Tioga County and into the southern Poconos. Snow amounts of 1 to 5 inches were common in the south-central mountains, tempered by sleet and freezing rain. Over the far southeast, storm total (liquid) precipitation amounts averaged between 1 and 2 inches. ||Impacts: The heavy snow and significant icing prompted many schools to close on 2/5. Road travel varied from hazardous to extremely dangerous across the area with severe traffic impacts on the morning commute. The wintry precipitation caused numerous vehicle accidents and road closures.  A fatal vehicle accident involving four tractor trailers and a passenger car closed Interstate 76 (PA Turnpike) in Lower Allen Township, Cumberland County, around 1130 PM EST on 2/4 until about 1 PM EST on 2/5. The storm also downed several trees and utility lines creating widespread power outages. At the height of the storm nearly 850,000 customers statewide were without power (primarily in the southeastern PA). The State EOC maintained activation throughout the storm. Shelters and warming centers were opened throughout the southeast region. Governor Tom Corbett declared a disaster emergency for York and Lancaster counties to make state resources, including the National Guard, available to support county and local recovery operations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged from 1 to 3 inches. Ice accumulations from sleet and heavy freezing rain averaged between 0.25 and 0.50 inch. A six-mile section of the Pennsylvania Turnpike was closed following a crash that killed a truck driver and closed the toll highway for about 14 hours. The eastbound and westbound lanes between the Harrisburg West Shore and Gettysburg Pike interchanges reopened shortly before 1330 pm EST on 2/5. Four tractor-trailers and a passenger car were involved in the chain-reaction crash that occurred near mile marker 338 in Cumberland County at around 2330 pm EST on 2/4. The driver of one of the trucks was killed at the scene.

Dense fog persisted longer in Upper Bucks County and the Lehigh Valley, into the afternoon of the 15th  A weak cold front that passed through the area during the early evening of the 15th prevented a second night of widespread dense fog from occurring. Black ice occurred from the far northwest Philadelphia suburbs northwestward through Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. Untreated roadways were hazardous.

One of the harshest arctic outbreaks in years occurred in Eastern Pennsylvania on the 7th.  Record calendar day low temperatures occurred and combined with strong northwest winds produced wind chill factors as low as around 30 degrees below zero in the Poconos and around 20 degrees below zero elsewhere. High temperatures struggled to get above zero in the Poconos and reach double digit figures elsewhere. The excessive cold  caused many schools and universities to either cancel classes or have delayed openings.||The severe cold led to Pennsylvania Governor Tom Corbett signed a Proclamation of Propane and Heating Oil Emergency��� to waive federal and state motor carrier regulations related to hours of service for drivers of commercial motor vehicles transporting propane gas and heating oil in Pennsylvania. AAA Mid-Atlantic reported an 81 percent increase in service calls, mainly for dead batteries. Amtrak reported extensive delays in its rail service. The cold weather also affected SEPTA Regional Rail service in the Philadelphia area as there were delays on the Paoli/Thorndale line. The cold weather strained power supplies. PJM Interconnection, the agency that oversees the electric grid supplying the region, said electricity suppliers were struggling to keep up with surging demand as the cold forced some power plants to shut. An all-time winter record usage was recorded at 8 a.m. EST on the 7th, 138,600 megawatts surpassing the previous record from 2007. Utilities asked their customers where possible to switch to diesel or fuel oil.  Three thousand homeless people in Philadelphia were sheltered.| |The low temperature of 4 degrees above zero at the Philadelphia International Airport was the coldest of the winter and the coldest air temperature day since January 24, 2005 when the low reached 3 degrees above zero. The low temperature of zero at Reading (Berks County) was the coldest of the winter season and matched the low last set on January 24, 2011. A period of freezing rain affected Eastern Pennsylvania on the 5th and caused hazardous traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Ice accumulations averaged one to two tenths. Precipitation started briefly as sleet in most areas before quickly transitioning to freezing rain and then eventually to some spotty light rain. Freezing rain occurred during the morning of the 5th in Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, in Berks County and the far northwestern Philadelphia suburbs from the mid morning into the afternoon, in the Lehigh Valley from the late morning through the early evening and in the valleys of the Poconos from the afternoon through the evening of the 5th. The air mass was so cold and dense that higher terrain locations in the Poconos just experienced plain rain.  ||Representative ice accumulations included two tenths of an inch in Shillington (Berks County) and East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), one tenth of an inch in East Coventry Township (Chester County), Doylestown (Bucks County), the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Forks Township (Northampton County) and one twentieth of an inch at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The freezing rain was caused by the return flow off the Atlantic Ocean assisted by a weak stationary front over the Middle Atlantic States. While the air mass aloft warmed, the arctic air mass in place near the surface kept temperatures below freezing as precipitation moved through.

An arctic cold front crossed the Upper Ohio Valley on the 6th, bringing record low temperatures and extreme wind chills the morning of the 7th. It was the coldest January 7th on record in Pittsburgh with a low temperature of 9 below zero, and a high temperature of 4 above zero. Across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland, low temperatures ranged from 5 to 15 degrees below zero the morning of the 7th, with the lowest wind chill readings from 25 to 55 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low temperature of 9 degrees below zero with a wind chill of 28 degrees below zero was recorded near Carmichaels the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In spite of air temperatures being slightly above freezing during the early morning of the 11th, the combination of intermittent light rain, fog and the recent unseasonably cold weather produced some icy spots on roadways in Berks County and the Poconos. The icy conditions contributed to vehicular accidents, but no serious injuries were reported. In Monroe County, one traffic accident in East Stroudsburg involved a police vehicle.  In Berks County,  icy road conditions closed Pennsylvania State Routes 662 and 73 in Ruscombmanor Township. Pennsylvania State Route 662 was closed between Pennsylvania State Route 12 and the Fleetwood Borough line. Pennsylvania State Route 73 was closed between Pennsylvania State Route 12 and the Maidencreek Township line.  The roadways were reopened by 9 a.m. EST that morning. As air temperatures rose, icy raod conditions ceased.

One of the harshest arctic outbreaks in years occurred in Eastern Pennsylvania on the 7th.  Record calendar day low temperatures occurred and combined with strong northwest winds produced wind chill factors as low as around 30 degrees below zero in the Poconos and around 20 degrees below zero elsewhere. High temperatures struggled to get above zero in the Poconos and reach double digit figures elsewhere. The excessive cold  caused many schools and universities to either cancel classes or have delayed openings.||The severe cold led to Pennsylvania Governor Tom Corbett signed a Proclamation of Propane and Heating Oil Emergency��� to waive federal and state motor carrier regulations related to hours of service for drivers of commercial motor vehicles transporting propane gas and heating oil in Pennsylvania. AAA Mid-Atlantic reported an 81 percent increase in service calls, mainly for dead batteries. Amtrak reported extensive delays in its rail service. The cold weather also affected SEPTA Regional Rail service in the Philadelphia area as there were delays on the Paoli/Thorndale line. The cold weather strained power supplies. PJM Interconnection, the agency that oversees the electric grid supplying the region, said electricity suppliers were struggling to keep up with surging demand as the cold forced some power plants to shut. An all-time winter record usage was recorded at 8 a.m. EST on the 7th, 138,600 megawatts surpassing the previous record from 2007. Utilities asked their customers where possible to switch to diesel or fuel oil. A period of freezing rain affected Eastern Pennsylvania on the 5th and caused hazardous traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Ice accumulations averaged one to two tenths. Precipitation started briefly as sleet in most areas before quickly transitioning to freezing rain and then eventually to some spotty light rain. Freezing rain occurred during the morning of the 5th in Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, in Berks County and the far northwestern Philadelphia suburbs from the mid morning into the afternoon, in the Lehigh Valley from the late morning through the early evening and in the valleys of the Poconos from the afternoon through the evening of the 5th. The air mass was so cold and dense that higher terrain locations in the Poconos just experienced plain rain.  ||Representative ice accumulations included two tenths of an inch in Shillington (Berks County) and East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), one tenth of an inch in East Coventry Township (Chester County), Doylestown (Bucks County), the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Forks Township (Northampton County) and one twentieth of an inch at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The freezing rain was caused by the return flow off the Atlantic Ocean assisted by a weak stationary front over the Middle Atlantic States. While the air mass aloft warmed, the arctic air mass in place near the surface kept temperatures below freezing as precipitation moved through.

An arctic cold front crossed the Upper Ohio Valley on the 6th, bringing record low temperatures and extreme wind chills the morning of the 7th. It was the coldest January 7th on record in Pittsburgh with a low temperature of 9 below zero, and a high temperature of 4 above zero. Across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland, low temperatures ranged from 5 to 15 degrees below zero the morning of the 7th, with the lowest wind chill readings from 25 to 55 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low temperature of 13 degrees below zero with a wind chill of 39 degrees below zero was recorded near Franklin the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front coupled with an upper air disturbance triggered bands of accumulating snow showers across Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos on the morning of the 18th. Snowfall averaged one to around three inches in the Poconos and Northern Berks and Lehigh Counties and closer to one inch in the rest of the Lehigh Valley.  The snow showers started between 2 a.m. EST and 4 a.m. EST on the 18th and occurred until 8 a.m. EST in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and until 11 a.m. EST in the Poconos. The snow showers were heavy at times in the Poconos. Untreated roadways became slippery.  ||Representative snowfall included 3.0 inches in Lake Harmony and Albrightsville in Carbon County and Reeders in Monroe County, 2.5 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County) and Bushkill Township (Northampton County), 2.3 inches in Slatington (Lehigh County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 1.5 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 1.3 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 1.0 inch at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and Bath (Northampton County) and 0.8 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County).

In spite of air temperatures being slightly above freezing during the early morning of the 11th, the combination of intermittent light rain, fog and the recent unseasonably cold weather produced some icy spots on roadways in Berks County and the Poconos. The icy conditions contributed to vehicular accidents, but no serious injuries were reported. In Monroe County, one traffic accident in East Stroudsburg involved a police vehicle.  In Berks County,  icy road conditions closed Pennsylvania State Routes 662 and 73 in Ruscombmanor Township. Pennsylvania State Route 662 was closed between Pennsylvania State Route 12 and the Fleetwood Borough line. Pennsylvania State Route 73 was closed between Pennsylvania State Route 12 and the Maidencreek Township line.  The roadways were reopened by 9 a.m. EST that morning. As air temperatures rose, icy raod conditions ceased.

A moist, southwest flow of air preceding a cold front dropped two to four inches of snow across most of Eastern Pennsylvania during the daytime hours on the 25th. While less snow accumulated on roadways, untreated and less traveled ones became slippery.  Accidents in particular were reported in the Lehigh Valley and included incidents on U.S. Route 22 and Interstate 78.  In the Philadelphia suburbs, northbound travel on the Pennsylvania Turnpike Northeast Extension between Lansdale (Montgomery County) and Quakertown (Bucks County) was slowed because of a truck accident. A truck skidded off the road and was teetering over an embankment. No injuries were reported. Snow began on the 25th around sunrise in the Poconos and slowly spread to the southeast reaching the Lehigh Valley around 9 a.m. EST and the local Philadelphia area around 11 a.m. EST. The steady snow ended that afternoon between 4 p.m. EST and 5 p.m. EST in most areas. The snow fell at its heaviest during the early afternoon in the local Philadelphia area, and during the middle of the afternoon in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and Poconos. Isolated snow squalls that night dropped up to an additional inch of snow as the cold front itself moved through.||Representative snowfall included 4.0 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County), 3.0 inches in Ridley Park (Delaware County) and Ardmore (Montgomery County), 2.8 inches in Huffs Church/District Township (Berks County), 2.6 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County), 2.5 inches in Lehigton (Carbon County), 2.3 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County) and Bushkill Township (Northampton County), 2.2 inches in Furlong (Bucks County) and Mount Airy (Philadelphia), 2.1 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and Slatington (Lehigh County), 2.0 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County) and Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 1.8 inches in Quakertown (Bucks County) and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 1.5 inches in Bensalem (Bucks County) and the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 1.3 inches in Kutztown (Berks County) and New London (Chester County).

The very persistent and strong northwest flow that started during the afternoon of the 27th caused blowout tide conditions during the morning low tide cycle on the 28th along tidal sections of the Delaware River in Pennsylvania. In Philadelphia, the low tide reached 1.87 feet below mean lower low water and on Newbold Island (Bucks County) the low tide reached 1.84 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at both locations at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

A high pressure system that moved over Pennsylvania coupled with fresh snow cover from the winter storm on the 2nd and 3rd gave the area one of its coldest winter mornings in years. This was the first of three arctic blasts in the state during the month.  While this was the coldest morning of the winter for more rural areas that are normally colder on calm wind nights, it was not the harshest.  Because the high pressure system was over the region, wind chill factors and actual air temperatures were nearly the same. This was not the case a few days later and again around the 22nd of January. ||The low temperature reached 8 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport, the coldest morning in Philadelphia since January 24, 2011.  The low temperature at the Lehigh Valley International Airport was a daily record breaking 4 degrees below zero, the coldest morning of the winter and the coldest since the same minimum temperature on January 24, 2011.  The low temperature reached 1 degree above zero in Reading (Berks County), the coldest morning since it was zero degrees on January 24, 2011.  The low temperature reached 9 degrees below zero in Mount Pocono, this tied January 22nd as the coldest morning of January and the coldest day since January 17, 2009 when the low temperature reached 14 degrees below zero.||Other low temperatures on the morning of the 4th included 9 degrees below zero in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 5 degrees below zero in Martins Creek (Northampton County) and Glenmoore (Chester County), 4 degrees below zero in West Chester (Chester County), 3 degrees below zero in Lehighton (Carbon County) and Boyertown (Berks County), 2 degrees below zero in Doylestown (Bucks County) and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 1 degree in Warrington (Bucks County), 2 degrees in Horsham (Montgomery County) and 4 degrees in Havertown (Delaware County).

A period of freezing rain affected Eastern Pennsylvania on the 5th and caused hazardous traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Ice accumulations averaged one to two tenths. Precipitation started briefly as sleet in most areas before quickly transitioning to freezing rain and then eventually to some spotty light rain. Freezing rain occurred during the morning of the 5th in Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, in Berks County and the far northwestern Philadelphia suburbs from the mid morning into the afternoon, in the Lehigh Valley from the late morning through the early evening and in the valleys of the Poconos from the afternoon through the evening of the 5th. The air mass was so cold and dense that higher terrain locations in the Poconos just experienced plain rain.  ||Representative ice accumulations included two tenths of an inch in Shillington (Berks County) and East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), one tenth of an inch in East Coventry Township (Chester County), Doylestown (Bucks County), the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Forks Township (Northampton County) and one twentieth of an inch at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The freezing rain was caused by the return flow off the Atlantic Ocean assisted by a weak stationary front over the Middle Atlantic States. While the air mass aloft warmed, the arctic air mass in place near the surface kept temperatures below freezing as precipitation moved through.

An arctic cold front crossed the Upper Ohio Valley on the 6th, bringing record low temperatures and extreme wind chills the morning of the 7th. It was the coldest January 7th on record in Pittsburgh with a low temperature of 9 below zero, and a high temperature of 4 above zero. Across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland, low temperatures ranged from 5 to 15 degrees below zero the morning of the 7th, with the lowest wind chill readings from 25 to 55 degrees below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low temperature of 14 degrees below zero with a wind chill of 38 degrees below zero was recorded near Burgettstown the morning of the 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure system over Pennsylvania combined with moist conditions at the surface from the rain earlier in the day on the 14th to produce widespread dense fog and even some black ice from the middle of the evening on the 14th through the morning on the 15th. Dense fog persisted longer in Upper Bucks County and the Lehigh Valley, into the afternoon of the 15th  A weak cold front that passed through the area during the early evening of the 15th prevented a second night of widespread dense fog from occurring. Black ice occurred from the far northwest Philadelphia suburbs northwestward through Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. Untreated roadways were hazardous.

A strong area of deepening low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on January 5th. This low moved across northwestern Pennsylvania early on the 6th and then continued rapidly northeast. At the same time, arctic high pressure settled over the Great Plains resulting in a very strong pressure gradient over the Upper Ohio Valley. Strong west to northwest winds ushered in an unusually cold and dangerous airmass and resulted in the coldest temperatures in northwestern Pennsylvania since January 1994. Record low temperatures and unprecedented wind chills were reported throughout the region on both January 6th and 7th. Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph on the 6th. By daybreak on the 7th, sustained winds had dropped to 15 to 25 mph with gusts to 30 mph but this provided only slight relief from the brutal wind chills. Temperatures during the evening of the 5th were in the 40s across all of western Pennsylvania. By daybreak on the 6th, readings were in the teens or lower 20s with readings finally dropping below zero late in the afternoon.  Wind chills dropped below negative 25 by late afternoon and eventually bottomed out around -35.  Wind chills finally warmed above minus 25 during the early afternoon hours of the 7th.  New records lows of -9 and -10 were established at Erie International Airport on the 6th and 7th.  Low temperatures at the Meadville Airport on the 6th and 7th were -13 and -13.  High temperatures on the 7th were only in the lower single digits above zero.   All schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brutal cold weather settled over the area on January 6th and 7th. The coldest temperatures since January 1994 were reported with lows of minus 10 or colder on both the 6th and 7th. Low temperatures at the Meadville Airport on the 6th and 7th were -13 and -13. Wind chills dipped below minus 25 degrees by late afternoon on the 6th and then continued for almost 24 hours.  Sustained west to northwest winds were as high as 25 to 35 mph with gusts to 45 mph. All of the schools in the area were closed on both the 6th and 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland the afternoon of the 25th. A heavy band of snow with the front reduced visibilities to below one quarter mile for a time as it crossed the region. Over a 12 to 18 hour period, snow showers ahead of the front, combined with several lake enhanced and up-slope snow bands behind it, produced a general 3 to 5 inches of snow across the region, with 4 to 8 inches of snow in the higher ridges of West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pittsburgh set a record snowfall for January 25th with 5.2 inches measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved east across the Tennessee Valley on January 2nd. A trough extending north from this low caused snow to develop just after midnight on January 2nd. The trough moved into western Pennsylvania during the late afternoon hours of the 2nd causing the flow to become northerly off of Lake Erie. Snowfall intensities began to increase during the morning hours with visibilities dropping below a quarter mile for several hours during the afternoon and early evening hours. Snowfall rates during this time approached two inches per hour.  The snow finally tapered off during the early morning hours of January 3rd.  Northeast to north winds gusted to as much as 25 mph during this event causing a lot of blowing and drifting. Six to eight inches of snow fell across much of Erie and Crawford Counties.   A peak total of 10.0 inches was reported at Girard in Erie County.  Officially 5.9 inches of snow fell at Erie International Airport.  In Crawford County, a peak total of 7.7 inches was reported west of Meadville.  Travel was difficult during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of snow on the ground, a moist southerly flow and a slowly northward moving warm front all helped produce dense fog across most of Eastern Pennsylvania from the evening of the 5th into the overnight.    Some overnight improvement occurred around the local Philadelphia area as the warm front advanced farther to the north. The dense fog dispersed on the morning of the 6th as a strong cold front moved across the state.

A low pressure system tracked east from the TN/KY border through NC on 21 January 2014, and then strengthened off the Mid-Atlantic coast dumping heavy snow across the southern tier of Central Pennsylvania. The snow began during the pre-dawn and early morning hours and became heavy at times in the afternoon, before it tapered off during the late evening. Total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 8 inches. Hazardous and restricted driving conditions were commonplace across the Lower Susquehanna Valley in the afternoon and evening.  State office facilities let employees leave work early in the afternoon on Tuesday. Schools were closed across most of the southern PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were common across the southern half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest snow-producing storm of the 2013-2014 winter season affected much of central Pennsylvania on 13 February 2014. The heavy snow was produced by a strong coastal low pressure system that moved northeastward from the Virginia Capes over the Delmarva peninsula.  A heavy deformation snow band pivoted slowly to the northwest during the day, and persisted over the central zones into the evening before dissipating.  The snow ended across the region by midnight on 14 February 2014.|Total snow accumulations generally ranged from 12 to 18 inches over the south-central and lower Susquehanna Valley zones with locally higher amounts (21.6 inches in Glencoe, Somerset County; 19.5 inches in Shrewsbury, York County).  Snow amounts of 6 to 12 inches fell over the central ridge-valley region into the mid/upper Susquehanna valley, while 2 to 5 inches were common reports in the northwest mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorm clusters associated with a passing cold front and upper level disturbance produced significant urban flooding around the Scranton metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was major urban flooding taking place with ditches and storm drains spilling over and flooding streets. Water was flowing into homes in some parts of the city.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorm clusters associated with a passing cold front and upper level disturbance produced significant urban flooding around the Scranton metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major urban flooding was occurring in the Throop, Jessup, Archbald, Simpson and Jermyn areas. City streets and secondary roads were flooded with cars stuck in the water and water flowing into homes.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 15 inches.
