EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of snow wrapping around the nor'easter accumulated 1 to 3 inches across sections of Eastern Pennsylvania with some higher amounts in northwest Berks County mainly during the morning and afternoon of the 11th. Temperatures were lower than the 10th and the snow accumulated more efficiently on roadways and walkways. Untreated roadways, mainly over the higher terrain became slippery.  Berks County bore the brunt of the impact from the snow. Some school districts in the county either had delayed openings or dismissed children early. In Richmond Township, one vehicle drove into the ditch, but the driver was not injured. ||Representative snowfall included 5.0 inches in Hamburg (Berks County), 3.5 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County), 3.3 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 3.0 inches in Bechtelsville (Berks County), 2.5 inches in Alburtis (Lehigh County) and Mertztown (Berks County), 2.1 inches in Salisbury Township (Lehigh County), 1.5 inches in Hilltown Township (Bucks County), Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Malvern (Chester County) and 1.3 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County). ||On the 11th, the nor'easter moved from the southern Maine coast northwest to the Saint Lawrence River Valley by the end of the day. Precipitation in the form of snow was able to wrap from the Atlantic Ocean counterclockwise around this system and arrive in our region from western Pennsylvania. The nor'easter then started moving to the northeast again and relinquished its effect on the region.

A wintry mix of precipitation affected the Poconos, Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the far northern Philadelphia suburbs from the late morning into the evening of the 2nd. A combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred and caused slippery traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Snow and sleet accumulations were less than an inch and mainly confined to the Poconos while ice accumulations were around one tenth of an inch or less.||Precipitation started as snow in nearly all areas from south to north during the late morning and early afternoon of the 2nd. It changed over to sleet from south to north during the first half of the afternoon and then to freezing rain during the middle of the afternoon.  Precipitation changed to plain rain during the first half of the evening, except in the Poconos where the transition took the entire evening into the very early morning of the 3rd.  ||Representative snow and sleet accumulations included 0.5 inches in Tannersville (Monroe County), 0.4 inches in Bowmanstown (Carbon County) and 0.3 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County) and Palmerton (Carbon County). ||The wintry mix was caused by double barrel low pressure systems. During the afternoon of the 2nd, one low pressure system was located in the Tennessee Valley, while the second was off the Delmarva Peninsula. The Delmarva low pressure system became the stronger low overnight and managed to bring in warmer air at the surface into Eastern Pennsylvania. As the low pressure system passed south of the Twin Forks of Long Island, the rain ended before sunrise on the 3rd.

Cold west to northwest flow across Lake Erie caused lake effect snow showers to develop just before daybreak on November 13th. The snow showers picked up in intensity during the daytime hours of the 13th with periods of heavy snow throughout the day. The heaviest snow initially was close to the Lake Erie Shoreline. The snow showers became more scattered during the evening of the 13th and then reorganized by early on the 14th as the flow became northwest. This change spread the heavier snow inland across Erie County. The snow diminished in intensity and coverage during the evening of the 14th and ended early on the 15th. Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour and visibilities were a half mile or less. Snowfall accumulations in Erie County ranged from 10 to 14 inches across the northern half of the county with lesser amounts in the south.  The heaviest snow fell during the daylight hours of the 13th.   Some of the higher totals included 17.0 inches south of North East and 16.8 inches in Amity Township.  12.6 inches of snow fell at Erie International Airport on the 13th with 7.8 inches falling between 7 am and 1 pm.  Another 3.0 inches fell on the 14th and 15th combined giving a storm total of 15.6 inches.  This was the first significant snow of the season and travel was severely hampered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The coldest air mass in nearly two decades brought extreme cold temperatures and dangerous wind chills to much of central Pennsylvania from January 6-8. Temperatures bottomed on the morning of the 7th with lows ranging from 0�� to -20��F with wind chills between -25�� to -50��F. The most recent comparable, though colder arctic outbreaks occurred in January 1994 and 1996 respectively. The arctic cold outbreak closed schools, set or tied low temperature records and prompted Gov. Tom Corbett to issue a ���Proclamation of Propane and Heating Oil Emergency��� to waive federal and state motor carrier regulations related to hours of service for drivers of commercial motor vehicles transporting propane gas and heating oil in Pennsylvania. The extreme cold outbreak caused serious logistical problems associated with the distribution and delivery of propane gas and heating oil within the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures generally varied between -10�� and -20��F with wind chills around -40��F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked east from the TN/KY border through NC on 21 January 2014, and then strengthened off the Mid-Atlantic coast dumping heavy snow across the southern tier of Central Pennsylvania. The snow began during the pre-dawn and early morning hours and became heavy at times in the afternoon, before it tapered off during the late evening. Total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 8 inches. Hazardous and restricted driving conditions were commonplace across the Lower Susquehanna Valley in the afternoon and evening.  State office facilities let employees leave work early in the afternoon on Tuesday. Schools were closed across most of the southern PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 6 to 8 inches were common across the southern half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of deepening low pressure moved northeast across Ohio on December 24th. The low moved north over Lake Erie during the early evening.  A cold front from the low swept east across western Pennsylvania causing the winds to become southwest and increase.  Gusts in excess of 50 mph were reported with the strongest winds near Lake Erie.  Downed power lines and trees were reported in a few areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed a few trees and large limbs in northern Erie County.  Some power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The interaction between a strong arctic cold front and coastal low pressure system produced heavy snow across portions of Central Pennsylvania on 2 January 2014. The longer-duration snow across the Northern Mountains, influenced primarily by the arctic front, fell mostly during the morning and afternoon hours. The bulk of the heavy snow in the Lower Susquehanna Valley fell during the late evening with occasional 1 inch/hour snowfall rates, as the surface low pressure system strengthened off the Mid-Atlantic coast. Very cold air surged into the region behind the arctic front, causing blowing snow and frigid wind chills over the northern tier.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of light to moderate snow produced general accumulations between 4 and 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving low pressure system streaked across the Central Appalachians and southern Mid-Atlantic states on 2-3 February 2014, producing a stripe of moderate to heavy snow on the northwest side of the low track over southern Pennsylvania. The heavy snowfall was aided by the placement of a coupled upper-level jet and strong low-mid level warm advection/isentropic lift. A general 4 to 8 inches of snow was common along and south of the US22/I-81 corridors, with 2-4 inches to the north from US22 to I-80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

Total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 8 inches. Hazardous and restricted driving conditions were commonplace across the Lower Susquehanna Valley in the afternoon and evening.  State office facilities let employees leave work early in the afternoon on Tuesday. Schools were closed across most of the southern PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of 6 inches were common across the southern half of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of snow wrapping around the nor'easter accumulated 1 to 3 inches across sections of Eastern Pennsylvania with some higher amounts in northwest Berks County mainly during the morning and afternoon of the 11th. Temperatures were lower than the 10th and the snow accumulated more efficiently on roadways and walkways. Untreated roadways, mainly over the higher terrain became slippery.  Berks County bore the brunt of the impact from the snow. Some school districts in the county either had delayed openings or dismissed children early. In Richmond Township, one vehicle drove into the ditch, but the driver was not injured. ||Representative snowfall included 5.0 inches in Hamburg (Berks County), 3.5 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County), 3.3 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 3.0 inches in Bechtelsville (Berks County), 2.5 inches in Alburtis (Lehigh County) and Mertztown (Berks County), 2.1 inches in Salisbury Township (Lehigh County), 1.5 inches in Hilltown Township (Bucks County), Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Malvern (Chester County) and 1.3 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County). ||On the 11th, the nor'easter moved from the southern Maine coast northwest to the Saint Lawrence River Valley by the end of the day. Precipitation in the form of snow was able to wrap from the Atlantic Ocean counterclockwise around this system and arrive in our region from western Pennsylvania. The nor'easter then started moving to the northeast again and relinquished its effect on the region.

A line of strong convection developed along a cold front moving across eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania the evening of December 23rd. Scattered damage was mainly to trees, with a few wires down as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees and power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient with a powerful cold front produced wind gusts over 45 MPH in locations across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and the northern panhandle of West Virginia the evening of December 24th. Highest reported winds in Pennsylvania were 56 MPH in Du Bois, 55 MPH in Pittsburgh, 54 MPH at Latrobe, and 45 MPH near Mcmurray in Washington county.  Highest winds in Ohio were 55 MPH at Zanesville, 52 MPH in Coshocton, and 45 MPH in Hanoverton in Columbiana county. Winds gusted to 55 MPH in Wheeling, WV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Allegheny County Airport reported a wind gust to 55 MPH at 833 PM.

A glazing of freezing rain occurred at the onset of precipitation in the higher terrain of the Poconos on the evening of the 22nd. As warmer air moved in at the surface, air temperatures rose overnight and the freezing rain ended. Some slippery conditions on untreated surfaces occurred.  The freezing rain was caused by the combination of a low pressure system that formed in the Gulf of Mexico and a high pressure system that passed across eastern Canada and northern New England that left some cold air at the surface in place.  The low pressure system formed in the Gulf of Mexico on the 21st and moved northeast to the South Carolina coast on the morning of the 22nd. The low pressure system then turned more to the north and was just east of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST on the 22nd just east of the Delmarva Peninsula at 7 a.m. EST on the 23rd. By then the surface high pressure system reached the Canadian Maritimes and ended its sub freezing influence on the Poconos.

A stalled frontal boundary across central New York resulted in light to moderate snowfall across the northern tier of Pennsylvania after midnight on Thursday, January 2nd. This snow intensified and dropped south into northeast Pennsylvania during the morning hours of Thursday, January 2nd as a low pressure system tracked through the Ohio Valley and re-developed off of the eastern seaboard. The highest snowfall totals occurred across the northern tier of Pennsylvania with a couple of 12 inch reports in Bradford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 7-12 inches across the county. Windy conditions resulted in significant blowing snow and cold temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold northwest flow and additional lift from a shortwave helped to enhance snowfall along the lake effect snow belt overnight on the 27th. A strong but unorganized band of lake enhanced snow slowly sunk southward through the overnight hours. Where it was stationed the longest, along I-76 and I-376 west of Pittsburgh, snow amounts ranged from 3-5 inches. Because of it's timing, there were significant impacts for the late evening commute Thursday and Friday morning including closures of the Parkway West due to accidents and slick conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted Central Pennsylvania from late on 4 February into the afternoon on Wednesday 5 February 2014. A wintry mix of heavy snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain occurred across the region from a dynamic low pressure system and upper-level energy that shifted from the Ohio Valley to the Mid-Atlantic coast. The snow started during the late evening on 2/4 across the western Alleghenies before changing to sleet and freezing rain during the pre-dawn and early morning hours on 2/5. Sleet quickly changed to all freezing rain over the southern tier counties, where widespread heavy icing (0.25-0.50 inch) was observed.  The greatest ice accumulations were focused over the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation ended by the early afternoon on 2/5. Total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches over a relatively narrow sw-ne stripe through the zones located just north of I-80. Locally higher amounts close to a foot were observed in Tioga County and into the southern Poconos. Snow amounts of 1 to 5 inches were common in the south-central mountains, tempered by sleet and freezing rain. Over the far southeast, storm total (liquid) precipitation amounts averaged between 1 and 2 inches. ||Impacts: The heavy snow and significant icing prompted many schools to close on 2/5. Road travel varied from hazardous to extremely dangerous across the area with severe traffic impacts on the morning commute. The wintry precipitation caused numerous vehicle accidents and road closures.  A fatal vehicle accident involving four tractor trailers and a passenger car closed Interstate 76 (PA Turnpike) in Lower Allen Township, Cumberland County, around 1130 PM EST on 2/4 until about 1 PM EST on 2/5. The storm also downed several trees and utility lines creating widespread power outages. At the height of the storm nearly 850,000 customers statewide were without power (primarily in the southeastern PA). The State EOC maintained activation throughout the storm. Shelters and warming centers were opened throughout the southeast region. Governor Tom Corbett declared a disaster emergency for York and Lancaster counties to make state resources, including the National Guard, available to support county and local recovery operations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice accumulations from sleet and heavy freezing rain averaged between 0.25 and 0.50 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bands of snow wrapping around the nor'easter accumulated 1 to 3 inches across sections of Eastern Pennsylvania with some higher amounts in northwest Berks County mainly during the morning and afternoon of the 11th. Temperatures were lower than the 10th and the snow accumulated more efficiently on roadways and walkways. Untreated roadways, mainly over the higher terrain became slippery.  Berks County bore the brunt of the impact from the snow. Some school districts in the county either had delayed openings or dismissed children early. In Richmond Township, one vehicle drove into the ditch, but the driver was not injured. ||Representative snowfall included 5.0 inches in Hamburg (Berks County), 3.5 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County), 3.3 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 3.0 inches in Bechtelsville (Berks County), 2.5 inches in Alburtis (Lehigh County) and Mertztown (Berks County), 2.1 inches in Salisbury Township (Lehigh County), 1.5 inches in Hilltown Township (Bucks County), Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Malvern (Chester County) and 1.3 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County). ||On the 11th, the nor'easter moved from the southern Maine coast northwest to the Saint Lawrence River Valley by the end of the day. Precipitation in the form of snow was able to wrap from the Atlantic Ocean counterclockwise around this system and arrive in our region from western Pennsylvania. The nor'easter then started moving to the northeast again and relinquished its effect on the region.

A tight pressure gradient with a powerful cold front produced wind gusts over 45 MPH in locations across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and the northern panhandle of West Virginia the evening of December 24th. Highest reported winds in Pennsylvania were 56 MPH in Du Bois, 55 MPH in Pittsburgh, 54 MPH at Latrobe, and 45 MPH near Mcmurray in Washington county.  Highest winds in Ohio were 55 MPH at Zanesville, 52 MPH in Coshocton, and 45 MPH in Hanoverton in Columbiana county. Winds gusted to 55 MPH in Wheeling, WV.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 54 MPH wind gust was recorded at the Latrobe airport at 919 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The largest snow-producing storm of the 2013-2014 winter season affected much of central Pennsylvania on 13 February 2014. The heavy snow was produced by a strong coastal low pressure system that moved northeastward from the Virginia Capes over the Delmarva peninsula.  A heavy deformation snow band pivoted slowly to the northwest during the day, and persisted over the central zones into the evening before dissipating.  The snow ended across the region by midnight on 14 February 2014.|Total snow accumulations generally ranged from 12 to 18 inches over the south-central and lower Susquehanna Valley zones with locally higher amounts (21.6 inches in Glencoe, Somerset County; 19.5 inches in Shrewsbury, York County).  Snow amounts of 6 to 12 inches fell over the central ridge-valley region into the mid/upper Susquehanna valley, while 2 to 5 inches were common reports in the northwest mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of deepening low pressure moved northeast across Ohio on December 24th. The low moved north over Lake Erie during the early evening.  A cold front from the low swept east across western Pennsylvania causing the winds to become southwest and increase.  Gusts in excess of 50 mph were reported with the strongest winds near Lake Erie.  Downed power lines and trees were reported in a few areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts as high as 50 mph downed a few trees and large limbs across inland Erie County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving low pressure system streaked across the Central Appalachians and southern Mid-Atlantic states on 2-3 February 2014, producing a stripe of moderate to heavy snow on the northwest side of the low track over southern Pennsylvania. The heavy snowfall was aided by the placement of a coupled upper-level jet and strong low-mid level warm advection/isentropic lift. A general 4 to 8 inches of snow was common along and south of the US22/I-81 corridors, with 2-4 inches to the north from US22 to I-80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent warm air advection atop a shallow wedge of cold air near the surface with temperatures in the mid to upper 20s resulted in a prolonged period of light freezing rain from mid-day through the evening hours across southeastern PA. Minor to moderate ice accumulations were observed with up to three-tenths of an inch observed in Adams and Franklin counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Steady, light freezing rain produced up to 0.30 of ice accretion across Adams County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across central New York resulted in light to moderate snowfall across the northern tier of Pennsylvania after midnight on Thursday, January 2nd. This snow intensified and dropped south into northeast Pennsylvania during the morning hours of Thursday, January 2nd as a low pressure system tracked through the Ohio Valley and re-developed off of the eastern seaboard. The highest snowfall totals occurred across the northern tier of Pennsylvania with a couple of 12 inch reports in Bradford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 7-9 inches across the county. Windy conditions resulted in significant blowing snow and cold temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over New England spread cold air and precipitation west into northwestern Pennsylvania.  The relatively warm waters of Lake Erie helped enhance the snowfall.  The snow began a couple of hours before daybreak and increased in intensity during the morning hours.   Periods of heavy snow then continued through the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east during the evening hours.  Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour.  North to northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph during this event causing near whiteout conditions at times and considerable drifting.  Snowfall totals for this event were the greatest on the higher terrain inland from Lake Erie.  Snowfall totals south of Interstate 90 ranged from 6 to 8 inches in Erie County with a peak of 8.7 inches at Colt Station at the eastern end of the county.  Around 6 inches of snow was reported in northern and eastern Crawford County.  Travel was severely hampered during this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over New England spread cold air and precipitation west into northwestern Pennsylvania. The relatively warm waters of Lake Erie helped enhance the snowfall. The snow began a couple of hours before daybreak and increased in intensity during the morning hours. Periods of heavy snow then continued through the afternoon hours before tapering off from west to east during the evening hours. Snowfall rates at times exceeded an inch per hour. North to northwest winds gusted to as much as 30 mph during this event causing near whiteout conditions at times and considerable drifting.  Around 6 inches of snow was reported in northern and eastern Crawford County. Travel was severely hampered during this event.

Snow amounts of 1 to 5 inches were common in the south-central mountains, tempered by sleet and freezing rain. Over the far southeast, storm total (liquid) precipitation amounts averaged between 1 and 2 inches. ||Impacts: The heavy snow and significant icing prompted many schools to close on 2/5. Road travel varied from hazardous to extremely dangerous across the area with severe traffic impacts on the morning commute. The wintry precipitation caused numerous vehicle accidents and road closures.  A fatal vehicle accident involving four tractor trailers and a passenger car closed Interstate 76 (PA Turnpike) in Lower Allen Township, Cumberland County, around 1130 PM EST on 2/4 until about 1 PM EST on 2/5. The storm also downed several trees and utility lines creating widespread power outages. At the height of the storm nearly 850,000 customers statewide were without power (primarily in the southeastern PA). The State EOC maintained activation throughout the storm. Shelters and warming centers were opened throughout the southeast region. Governor Tom Corbett declared a disaster emergency for York and Lancaster counties to make state resources, including the National Guard, available to support county and local recovery operations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 4 inches. Ice accumulations from sleet and heavy freezing rain were between 0.25-0.30 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across central New York resulted in light to moderate snowfall across the northern tier of Pennsylvania after midnight on Thursday, January 2nd. This snow intensified and dropped south into northeast Pennsylvania during the morning hours of Thursday, January 2nd as a low pressure system tracked through the Ohio Valley and re-developed off of the eastern seaboard. The highest snowfall totals occurred across the northern tier of Pennsylvania with a couple of 12 inch reports in Bradford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5-7 inches across the county. Windy conditions resulted in significant blowing snow and cold temperatures.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave crossing the upper Ohio Valley brought light freezing rain through the morning hours of the 22nd. While strong warm advection was ongoing ahead of the system aloft...a strong inversion at the surface did not allow for temperatures to warm above freezing at the onset precipitation. This allowed for a light glazing of ice on untreated surfaces over portions of eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia panhandle, and counties north of I-70 in Pennsylvania. Several accidents were reported across the advisory area, including a head-on collision between a car and a school bus in Columbiana county, Ohio.

The largest snow-producing storm of the 2013-2014 winter season affected much of central Pennsylvania on 13 February 2014. The heavy snow was produced by a strong coastal low pressure system that moved northeastward from the Virginia Capes over the Delmarva peninsula.  A heavy deformation snow band pivoted slowly to the northwest during the day, and persisted over the central zones into the evening before dissipating.  The snow ended across the region by midnight on 14 February 2014.|Total snow accumulations generally ranged from 12 to 18 inches over the south-central and lower Susquehanna Valley zones with locally higher amounts (21.6 inches in Glencoe, Somerset County; 19.5 inches in Shrewsbury, York County).  Snow amounts of 6 to 12 inches fell over the central ridge-valley region into the mid/upper Susquehanna valley, while 2 to 5 inches were common reports in the northwest mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 10 inches.

A fatal vehicle accident involving four tractor trailers and a passenger car closed Interstate 76 (PA Turnpike) in Lower Allen Township, Cumberland County, around 1130 PM EST on 2/4 until about 1 PM EST on 2/5. The storm also downed several trees and utility lines creating widespread power outages. At the height of the storm nearly 850,000 customers statewide were without power (primarily in the southeastern PA). The State EOC maintained activation throughout the storm. Shelters and warming centers were opened throughout the southeast region. Governor Tom Corbett declared a disaster emergency for York and Lancaster counties to make state resources, including the National Guard, available to support county and local recovery operations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged from 2 to 4 inches. Ice accumulations from sleet and heavy freezing rain were between 0.25-0.30 inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of precipitation affected the Poconos, Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the far northern Philadelphia suburbs from the late morning into the evening of the 2nd. A combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred and caused slippery traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Snow and sleet accumulations were less than an inch and mainly confined to the Poconos while ice accumulations were around one tenth of an inch or less.||Precipitation started as snow in nearly all areas from south to north during the late morning and early afternoon of the 2nd. It changed over to sleet from south to north during the first half of the afternoon and then to freezing rain during the middle of the afternoon.  Precipitation changed to plain rain during the first half of the evening, except in the Poconos where the transition took the entire evening into the very early morning of the 3rd.  ||Representative snow and sleet accumulations included 0.5 inches in Tannersville (Monroe County), 0.4 inches in Bowmanstown (Carbon County) and 0.3 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County) and Palmerton (Carbon County). ||The wintry mix was caused by double barrel low pressure systems. During the afternoon of the 2nd, one low pressure system was located in the Tennessee Valley, while the second was off the Delmarva Peninsula. The Delmarva low pressure system became the stronger low overnight and managed to bring in warmer air at the surface into Eastern Pennsylvania. As the low pressure system passed south of the Twin Forks of Long Island, the rain ended before sunrise on the 3rd.

A moist onshore and upslope flow along a developing coastal front produced an icy mixture of sleet and freezing rain over the higher terrain of the Poconos during the first half of the day on the 17th. Precipitation started as sleet shortly after Midnight EST on the 17th. The precipitation changed to plain rain in the valleys, but freezing rain over the higher terrain around 3 a.m. EST on the 17th. The precipitation slowly changed to rain as warmer air moved into the Poconos and the freezing rain was over by 10 a.m. EST.  Ice accumulations averaged less than one-tenth of an inch and mostly were reported on exposed surfaces: vehicles, decks and railings.

A winter storm on the 26th, the day before Thanksgiving Day, dropped heavy snow over parts of Eastern Pennsylvania, caused power outages as well as additional traveling difficulties. Snowfall averaged 5 to 11 inches in the Poconos, 3 to 9 inches in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and 1 to 4 inches in the Philadelphia suburbs. Generally less than an inch of snow and sleet fell in Philadelphia. The snowfall was also elevation dependent, with the highest accumulations occurring over the higher terrain in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and Poconos. ||In the Poconos, precipitation started as snow around 7 a.m. EST on the 26th, fell heavy at times from the mid morning into the mid afternoon and ended close to Midnight EST on the 27th.  In Berks County and the Lehigh Valley, precipitation started as rain around 6 a.m. EST on the 26th and changed to snow between 8 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST. The snow fell at its heaviest during the late morning and early afternoon and ended around 9 p.m. EST on the 26th.  In the Philadelphia northwest suburbs, precipitation began as rain between 4 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST on the 26th. As precipitation intensity increased, the rain changed to snow between 9 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST that morning. The snow ended around 9 p.m. EST that evening. In the local Philadelphia area, rain began around 4 a.m. EST.  As precipitation intensity increased, the rain mixed with or even changed to all sleet and snow during the midday hours before changing back to rain during the late afternoon. The rain ended between 9 p.m. and 10 p.m. EST on the 26th. ||The wintry precipitation caused many schools and community colleges to either cancel classes or dismiss students early. Some state offices closed early also. Several municipalities, especially in the Lehigh Valley declared snow emergencies. As snow started to fall, a surge of accidents occurred, especially in the Philadelphia suburbs. In Bucks County, in Newtown, an accident with a snow plow pinned a driver.  About 170 flights were cancelled at the Philadelphia International Airport, hundreds more were delayed.   Thunder occurred during heavier precipitation bursts during the late morning on the 26th in the local Philadelphia area. ||Representative snowfall included 11.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 9.0 inches in Hereford Township (Berks County) and Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 8.5 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County) and Williams Township (Northampton County), 7.8 inches in Salisbury Township (Lehigh County), 7.0 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 6.8 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County), 6.1 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County), 6.0 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 5.5 inches in Nazareth (Northampton County), 5.4 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 5.2 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 5.0 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County), 4.9 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 4.3 inches in Quakertown (Bucks County), 4.0 inches in Exton (Chester County), 3.8 inches in Wayne (Delaware County), 3.5 inches in Boyertown (Berks County) and Perkasie (Bucks County), 3.4 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 2.9 inches in Reading (Berks County), 2.8 inches in Graterford (Montgomery County), 2.5 inches in Chadds Ford (Delaware County), 2.1 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 2.0 inches in Spring City (Chester County), 1.6 inches in Royersford (Montgomery County) and 0.7 inches in the Somerton section of Philadelphia.||The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that formed in the Gulf of Mexico on a cold front on the 25th. The low pressure system moved north-northeast, crossed northern Florida during the late evening on the 25th, was located near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 26th, off the Virginia coastal waters at 1 p.m. EST on the 26th, about 150 miles east of the central New Jersey coast at 7 p.m. EST on the 26th and passed near Nantucket Island, Massachusetts during the late evening on the 26th.

A significant winter storm impacted Central Pennsylvania from late on 4 February into the afternoon on Wednesday 5 February 2014. A wintry mix of heavy snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain occurred across the region from a dynamic low pressure system and upper-level energy that shifted from the Ohio Valley to the Mid-Atlantic coast. The snow started during the late evening on 2/4 across the western Alleghenies before changing to sleet and freezing rain during the pre-dawn and early morning hours on 2/5. Sleet quickly changed to all freezing rain over the southern tier counties, where widespread heavy icing (0.25-0.50 inch) was observed.  The greatest ice accumulations were focused over the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation ended by the early afternoon on 2/5. Total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches over a relatively narrow sw-ne stripe through the zones located just north of I-80. Locally higher amounts close to a foot were observed in Tioga County and into the southern Poconos. Snow amounts of 1 to 5 inches were common in the south-central mountains, tempered by sleet and freezing rain. Over the far southeast, storm total (liquid) precipitation amounts averaged between 1 and 2 inches. ||Impacts: The heavy snow and significant icing prompted many schools to close on 2/5. Road travel varied from hazardous to extremely dangerous across the area with severe traffic impacts on the morning commute. The wintry precipitation caused numerous vehicle accidents and road closures.  A fatal vehicle accident involving four tractor trailers and a passenger car closed Interstate 76 (PA Turnpike) in Lower Allen Township, Cumberland County, around 1130 PM EST on 2/4 until about 1 PM EST on 2/5. The storm also downed several trees and utility lines creating widespread power outages. At the height of the storm nearly 850,000 customers statewide were without power (primarily in the southeastern PA). The State EOC maintained activation throughout the storm. Shelters and warming centers were opened throughout the southeast region. Governor Tom Corbett declared a disaster emergency for York and Lancaster counties to make state resources, including the National Guard, available to support county and local recovery operations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet accumulations ranged between 5 and 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of precipitation affected the Poconos, Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the far northern Philadelphia suburbs from the late morning into the evening of the 2nd. A combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred and caused slippery traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Snow and sleet accumulations were less than an inch and mainly confined to the Poconos while ice accumulations were around one tenth of an inch or less.||Precipitation started as snow in nearly all areas from south to north during the late morning and early afternoon of the 2nd. It changed over to sleet from south to north during the first half of the afternoon and then to freezing rain during the middle of the afternoon.  Precipitation changed to plain rain during the first half of the evening, except in the Poconos where the transition took the entire evening into the very early morning of the 3rd.  ||Representative snow and sleet accumulations included 0.5 inches in Tannersville (Monroe County), 0.4 inches in Bowmanstown (Carbon County) and 0.3 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County) and Palmerton (Carbon County). ||The wintry mix was caused by double barrel low pressure systems. During the afternoon of the 2nd, one low pressure system was located in the Tennessee Valley, while the second was off the Delmarva Peninsula. The Delmarva low pressure system became the stronger low overnight and managed to bring in warmer air at the surface into Eastern Pennsylvania. As the low pressure system passed south of the Twin Forks of Long Island, the rain ended before sunrise on the 3rd.

The largest snow-producing storm of the 2013-2014 winter season affected much of central Pennsylvania on 13 February 2014. The heavy snow was produced by a strong coastal low pressure system that moved northeastward from the Virginia Capes over the Delmarva peninsula.  A heavy deformation snow band pivoted slowly to the northwest during the day, and persisted over the central zones into the evening before dissipating.  The snow ended across the region by midnight on 14 February 2014.|Total snow accumulations generally ranged from 12 to 18 inches over the south-central and lower Susquehanna Valley zones with locally higher amounts (21.6 inches in Glencoe, Somerset County; 19.5 inches in Shrewsbury, York County).  Snow amounts of 6 to 12 inches fell over the central ridge-valley region into the mid/upper Susquehanna valley, while 2 to 5 inches were common reports in the northwest mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant winter storm impacted Central Pennsylvania from late on 4 February into the afternoon on Wednesday 5 February 2014. A wintry mix of heavy snow, sleet, freezing rain and rain occurred across the region from a dynamic low pressure system and upper-level energy that shifted from the Ohio Valley to the Mid-Atlantic coast. The snow started during the late evening on 2/4 across the western Alleghenies before changing to sleet and freezing rain during the pre-dawn and early morning hours on 2/5. Sleet quickly changed to all freezing rain over the southern tier counties, where widespread heavy icing (0.25-0.50 inch) was observed.  The greatest ice accumulations were focused over the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The wintry precipitation ended by the early afternoon on 2/5. Total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches over a relatively narrow sw-ne stripe through the zones located just north of I-80. Locally higher amounts close to a foot were observed in Tioga County and into the southern Poconos. Snow amounts of 1 to 5 inches were common in the south-central mountains, tempered by sleet and freezing rain. Over the far southeast, storm total (liquid) precipitation amounts averaged between 1 and 2 inches. ||Impacts: The heavy snow and significant icing prompted many schools to close on 2/5. Road travel varied from hazardous to extremely dangerous across the area with severe traffic impacts on the morning commute. The wintry precipitation caused numerous vehicle accidents and road closures.  A fatal vehicle accident involving four tractor trailers and a passenger car closed Interstate 76 (PA Turnpike) in Lower Allen Township, Cumberland County, around 1130 PM EST on 2/4 until about 1 PM EST on 2/5. The storm also downed several trees and utility lines creating widespread power outages. At the height of the storm nearly 850,000 customers statewide were without power (primarily in the southeastern PA). The State EOC maintained activation throughout the storm. Shelters and warming centers were opened throughout the southeast region. Governor Tom Corbett declared a disaster emergency for York and Lancaster counties to make state resources, including the National Guard, available to support county and local recovery operations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations ranged between 5 to 10 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of precipitation affected the Poconos, Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the far northern Philadelphia suburbs from the late morning into the evening of the 2nd. A combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred and caused slippery traveling conditions on untreated roadways. Snow and sleet accumulations were less than an inch and mainly confined to the Poconos while ice accumulations were around one tenth of an inch or less.||Precipitation started as snow in nearly all areas from south to north during the late morning and early afternoon of the 2nd. It changed over to sleet from south to north during the first half of the afternoon and then to freezing rain during the middle of the afternoon.  Precipitation changed to plain rain during the first half of the evening, except in the Poconos where the transition took the entire evening into the very early morning of the 3rd.  ||Representative snow and sleet accumulations included 0.5 inches in Tannersville (Monroe County), 0.4 inches in Bowmanstown (Carbon County) and 0.3 inches in Stroudsburg (Monroe County) and Palmerton (Carbon County). ||The wintry mix was caused by double barrel low pressure systems. During the afternoon of the 2nd, one low pressure system was located in the Tennessee Valley, while the second was off the Delmarva Peninsula. The Delmarva low pressure system became the stronger low overnight and managed to bring in warmer air at the surface into Eastern Pennsylvania. As the low pressure system passed south of the Twin Forks of Long Island, the rain ended before sunrise on the 3rd.

A winter storm on the 26th, the day before Thanksgiving Day, dropped heavy snow over parts of Eastern Pennsylvania, caused power outages as well as additional traveling difficulties. Snowfall averaged 5 to 11 inches in the Poconos, 3 to 9 inches in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and 1 to 4 inches in the Philadelphia suburbs. Generally less than an inch of snow and sleet fell in Philadelphia. The snowfall was also elevation dependent, with the highest accumulations occurring over the higher terrain in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and Poconos. ||In the Poconos, precipitation started as snow around 7 a.m. EST on the 26th, fell heavy at times from the mid morning into the mid afternoon and ended close to Midnight EST on the 27th.  In Berks County and the Lehigh Valley, precipitation started as rain around 6 a.m. EST on the 26th and changed to snow between 8 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST. The snow fell at its heaviest during the late morning and early afternoon and ended around 9 p.m. EST on the 26th.  In the Philadelphia northwest suburbs, precipitation began as rain between 4 a.m. EST and 6 a.m. EST on the 26th. As precipitation intensity increased, the rain changed to snow between 9 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST that morning. The snow ended around 9 p.m. EST that evening. In the local Philadelphia area, rain began around 4 a.m. EST.  As precipitation intensity increased, the rain mixed with or even changed to all sleet and snow during the midday hours before changing back to rain during the late afternoon. The rain ended between 9 p.m. and 10 p.m. EST on the 26th. ||The wintry precipitation caused many schools and community colleges to either cancel classes or dismiss students early. Some state offices closed early also. Several municipalities, especially in the Lehigh Valley declared snow emergencies. As snow started to fall, a surge of accidents occurred, especially in the Philadelphia suburbs. In Bucks County, in Newtown, an accident with a snow plow pinned a driver.  About 170 flights were cancelled at the Philadelphia International Airport, hundreds more were delayed.   Thunder occurred during heavier precipitation bursts during the late morning on the 26th in the local Philadelphia area. ||Representative snowfall included 11.0 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 9.0 inches in Hereford Township (Berks County) and Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 8.5 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County) and Williams Township (Northampton County), 7.8 inches in Salisbury Township (Lehigh County), 7.0 inches in Fleetwood (Berks County), 6.8 inches in Forks Township (Northampton County), 6.1 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County), 6.0 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County), 5.5 inches in Nazareth (Northampton County), 5.4 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 5.2 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 5.0 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County), 4.9 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 4.3 inches in Quakertown (Bucks County), 4.0 inches in Exton (Chester County), 3.8 inches in Wayne (Delaware County), 3.5 inches in Boyertown (Berks County) and Perkasie (Bucks County), 3.4 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 2.9 inches in Reading (Berks County), 2.8 inches in Graterford (Montgomery County), 2.5 inches in Chadds Ford (Delaware County), 2.1 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 2.0 inches in Spring City (Chester County), 1.6 inches in Royersford (Montgomery County) and 0.7 inches in the Somerton section of Philadelphia.||The wintry mix was caused by a low pressure system that formed in the Gulf of Mexico on a cold front on the 25th. The low pressure system moved north-northeast, crossed northern Florida during the late evening on the 25th, was located near Wilmington, North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 26th, off the Virginia coastal waters at 1 p.m. EST on the 26th, about 150 miles east of the central New Jersey coast at 7 p.m. EST on the 26th and passed near Nantucket Island, Massachusetts during the late evening on the 26th.

An area of light to moderate freezing rain moved quickly across Central Pennsylvania between 700 and 1000 AM EST on 19 February 2014. The quick glazing of ice on road surfaces lead to hazardous travel which severely impacted the the early morning commute. The icy roads caused a major traffic accident on I-80W involving 40-50 vehicles, several of which were tractor-trailers. A 10-mile section of I-80W was closed in Clearfield County between the Woodland and Kylertown Exits. There were a total of 17 injuries but no fatalities.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A quick glazing of ice from freezing rain caused a major traffic accident on I-80W and closed a 10-mile section section between Kylertown and Woodland (Exits 123-133).

An upper level shortwave crossing the upper Ohio Valley brought light freezing rain through the morning hours of the 22nd. While strong warm advection was ongoing ahead of the system aloft...a strong inversion at the surface did not allow for temperatures to warm above freezing at the onset precipitation. This allowed for a light glazing of ice on untreated surfaces over portions of eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia panhandle, and counties north of I-70 in Pennsylvania. Several accidents were reported across the advisory area, including a head-on collision between a car and a school bus in Columbiana county, Ohio.

A line of strong convection developed along a cold front moving across eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania the evening of December 23rd. Scattered damage was mainly to trees, with a few wires down as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported multiple trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of very strong low pressure moved northeast across the Central Great Lakes on November 24th. A cold front trailing this low swept east across western Pennsylvania during the early afternoon hours.   Southwest winds gusted to as much as 60 mph behind the cold front causing damage throughout Erie and Crawford Counties. Many trees, large limbs and power lines came down.  Power outages were reported in some areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds gusted to as much as 60 mph downing a few trees, large limbs and power lines. Power outages were reported in some areas.

Coastal low pressure system brought snow to areas south and east of Pittsburgh during the day on the 26th. 6-8 inches of snow was reported across Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia and Garrett county in Maryland.

Cold northwest flow and additional lift from a shortwave helped to enhance snowfall along the lake effect snow belt overnight on the 27th. A strong but unorganized band of lake enhanced snow slowly sunk southward through the overnight hours. Where it was stationed the longest, along I-76 and I-376 west of Pittsburgh, snow amounts ranged from 3-5 inches. Because of it's timing, there were significant impacts for the late evening commute Thursday and Friday morning including closures of the Parkway West due to accidents and slick conditions.

The precipitation was still a result of the moist circulation around the trundling low pressure system moving through the Canadian Maritimes.

A light coating of ice was reported with some accidents on untreated roads due to slick conditions across Garrett county Maryland, eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, and northern West Virginia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of deepening low pressure moved up the Ohio Valley on March 12th. The low shifted to the east coast during the evening hours. Precipitation associated with the low spread across the area by daybreak on the 12th and then transitioned to snow from west to east beginning around daybreak. Much of the area also saw a period of freezing rain.  The snow increased in intensity during the afternoon hours with visibilities less than one half mile into the evening hours. North to northeast winds increased as the low passed to the south of the area. Winds gusted to more than 45 mph during the early afternoon hours. Winds continued to gust to more than 30 mph through the evening hours. A peak gust of 55 mph was measured at Erie International Airport around 1 pm.  The strong winds combined with the snow to create significant blowing and drifting and very treacherous driving conditions. Snowfall totals of around 8 inches were reported across most of Erie County with slightly less snow in Crawford County.  There was also a slight glaze of ice in many areas.  A peak snowfall total of 10.0 inches was reported in Mill Creek Township near Erie.  Many schools across most of northwestern Pennsylvania were closed on the 12th and the evening commute was very difficult. Dozens of accidents and a some power outages were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mixed precipitation spread across the area early on the 12th and then transitioned to snow from west to east. Much of the area also saw a period of freezing rain. The snow increased in intensity during the late morning hours with visibilities less than one half mile. North to northeast winds increased as the low passed to the south of the area. Winds gusted to more than 50 mph during the early afternoon hours. Winds continued to gust to more than 30 mph through the evening hours. The strong winds combined with the snow to create significant blowing and drifting and very treacherous driving conditions. 6 to 10 inches of snow was reported in Erie County along with up to a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation. A peak snow total of 10.0 inches was reported in Mill Creek Township.  Many schools in northwestern Pennsylvania  were closed on the 12th and the evening commute was very difficult. Dozens of accidents and a some power outages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along an arctic boundary moving into northern West Virginia and strengthened as it moved to the Mid Atlantic coast. This produced heavy snow across the ridges of Fayette and Westmoreland counties in Pennsylvania, Marion, Monongalia, Tucker and Preston counties in West Virginia, and Garret county Maryland. Snowfall totals were from 8 to 12 inches, with the highest amounts near a foot across the higher elevations of Preston, Garret and Tucker counties. Amounts near 8 inches were reported in Morgantown northeast into the Chestnut and Laurel Ridges of Pennsylvania. Lesser snow amounts of 2 to 5 inches fell from Waynesburg to Greensburg in western Pennsylvania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large area of low pressure west of the Great Lakes combined with high pressure over New England to produce strong southeast winds on the 29th. This allowed sustained winds of over 25 MPH with down-slope from the ridges of western Pennsylvania allowing for winds to gust over 50 MPH at times. The highest wind speed recorded was 63 MPH at the Arnold Palmer Regional Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported trees down at multiple locations in the county along Route 36.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills near 20 degrees below zero were recorded in the North Hills area of Allegheny county the morning of the 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland the morning of the 27th. Visibilities were reduced to near zero at times in snow showers along the front. As high pressure moved over the region the 28th and 29th, morning low temperatures ranged from 5 below zero to 25 below zero across the region. Light winds did not allow for extreme wind chills, with the exception being wind chills near 30 below zero across parts of Garrett and Tucker counties just after midnight on the 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures at Pittsburgh International Airport were 8 below zero on the 28th, and 4 below zero on the 29th. Other low temperature readings across Allegheny county county ranged from 6 below to 16 below on the 28th, and 3 below to 15 below zero on the 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex and fast moving storm system swept across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland from late evening of the 4th into late morning of the 5th. Snowfall from 4 to 10 inches fell along and north of a line from Zanesville in Ohio, to Wheeling in West Virginia, and from Pittsburgh to Latrobe in Pennsylvania. The snow then changed to sleet and freezing rain in the early morning hours of the 5th, with sleet accumulations of an inch or more, and freezing rain accretion from one quarter to one half inch. Outside of this region freezing rain was reported up to one half inch in parts of Greene county in Pennsylvania, and Monroe county in Ohio. Sleet accumulated more than 2 inches across the Fayette county ridges and in Garrett county Maryland, topped by about one quarter inch of freezing rain. Across northern West Virginia south of the Pennsylvania border, warmer air at the surface limited freezing rain amounts to around one tenth of an inch with a mix of sleet as well.

A low-amplitude mid/upper level trough coupled with moderate instability allowed for thunderstorm development over the higher terrain of central PA during the late morning. Storms encountered a more favorable convective environment as they moved east during the afternoon, becoming more organized near edge of stronger northwesterly flow aloft and deep-layer shear. The storms that intensified to severe levels produced sporadic wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires along SR72 near Manheim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated damaging wind gusts and small hail occurred with thunderstorms along a southeastward progressing cold front across the Lower Susquehanna Valley during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires in Lititz.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Milder temperatures ahead of a cold front combined with moderate rainfall produced ice jam flooding on numerous streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported ice jam flooding on Buffalo Creek near Boggsville and Yellow Dog. The water levels were fluctuating wildly  affected a few roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Milder temperatures ahead of a cold front combined with moderate rainfall produced ice jam flooding on numerous streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported several roads flooded due to numerous small stream and creeks out of their banks. This was most numerous from New Sewickley Township to Independence Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Milder temperatures ahead of a cold front combined with moderate rainfall produced ice jam flooding on numerous streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported ice jam flooding on Oil Creek extending to the confluence with the Allegheny River. Numerous homes were impacted along Seneca Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure advancing across the region brought a wintry mix of precipitation across eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania late on the 17th through the 18th. Light freezing rain and sleet impacted much of the region south of Pittsburgh before changing to snow, while areas further north experienced mainly snow. Freezing rain and sleet amounts were generally a trace across the region. Snowfall amounts north of Pittsburgh ranged from 3-5 inches with isolated higher amounts of up to 6 inches across Lawrence and Butler counties in PA. An enhanced area of snow produced 6-8.5 inches across Jefferson county, PA.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow squalls with visibilities under a quarter of a mile developed with an advancing upper level trough during the morning hours on the 27th. Several accidents were reported along interstate 79 during this time, including a multiple car accident in Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 12-vehicle crash occurred just before noon on February 27th on Interstate 79 near the Houston exit in Washington county. A utility truck was unable to stop for slowed traffic, crashing into a tractor trailer and another vehicle. Five people were transported to the hospital, and three had moderate injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure quickly developed over the lower Ohio Valley the night of the 2nd and moved northeast along a stalled Arctic front across West Virginia the morning of the 3rd. This brought a band of heavy snow across northern West Virginia, extreme southwest Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland. Amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches, with highest totals of one foot of snow in Wetzel and Greene counties in Pennsylvania, and 10 inches in Garrett county Maryland, and Monongalia county in West Virginia. Just north of the heavy band of snow a general 2 to 5 inches of snow fell along a line east of Zanesville to downtown Pittsburgh, and south of the town of Indiana. There was a sharp edge to the snow with little accumulation north of this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure quickly developed over the lower Ohio Valley the night of the 2nd and moved northeast along a stalled Arctic front across West Virginia the morning of the 3rd. This brought a band of heavy snow across northern West Virginia, extreme southwest Pennsylvania and Garrett county Maryland. Amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches, with highest totals of one foot of snow in Wetzel and Greene counties in Pennsylvania, and 10 inches in Garrett county Maryland, and Monongalia county in West Virginia. Just north of the heavy band of snow a general 2 to 5 inches of snow fell along a line east of Zanesville to downtown Pittsburgh, and south of the town of Indiana. There was a sharp edge to the snow with little accumulation north of this area.

A complex and fast moving storm system swept across eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland from late evening of the 4th into late morning of the 5th. Snowfall from 4 to 10 inches fell along and north of a line from Zanesville in Ohio, to Wheeling in West Virginia, and from Pittsburgh to Latrobe in Pennsylvania. The snow then changed to sleet and freezing rain in the early morning hours of the 5th, with sleet accumulations of an inch or more, and freezing rain accretion from one quarter to one half inch. Outside of this region freezing rain was reported up to one half inch in parts of Greene county in Pennsylvania, and Monroe county in Ohio. Sleet accumulated more than 2 inches across the Fayette county ridges and in Garrett county Maryland, topped by about one quarter inch of freezing rain. Across northern West Virginia south of the Pennsylvania border, warmer air at the surface limited freezing rain amounts to around one tenth of an inch with a mix of sleet as well.

A low-amplitude mid/upper level trough coupled with moderate instability allowed for thunderstorm development over the higher terrain of central PA during the late morning. Storms encountered a more favorable convective environment as they moved east during the afternoon, becoming more organized near edge of stronger northwesterly flow aloft and deep-layer shear. The storms that intensified to severe levels produced sporadic wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Philipsburg.

Low pressure moved along an arctic boundary slowly pushing south across the upper Ohio Valley producing light snowfall. Areas in western Pennsylvania from I-70 to just north of Pittsburgh received 2 to 4 inches.

An area of low pressure moved east across the Tennessee Valley on January 2nd. A trough extending north from this low caused snow to develop just after midnight on January 2nd. The trough moved into western Pennsylvania during the late afternoon hours of the 2nd causing the flow to become northerly off of Lake Erie. Snowfall intensities began to increase during the morning hours with visibilities dropping below a quarter mile for several hours during the afternoon and early evening hours. Snowfall rates during this time approached two inches per hour.  The snow finally tapered off during the early morning hours of January 3rd.  Northeast to north winds gusted to as much as 25 mph during this event causing a lot of blowing and drifting. Six to eight inches of snow fell across much of Erie and Crawford Counties.   A peak total of 10.0 inches was reported at Girard in Erie County.  Officially 5.9 inches of snow fell at Erie International Airport.  In Crawford County, a peak total of 7.7 inches was reported west of Meadville.  Travel was difficult during this event and many accidents were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved toward the upper Ohio Valley on the 29th before transitioning to the east coast on the 30th. The system produced light snow across much eastern Ohio, western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and Garrett county Maryland. Colder northwest flow behind the system brought additional snowfall to the Laurel and Chestnut Ridges of Fayette county in Pennsylvania, the higher elevations of Preston and Tucker counties in West Virginia, and Garrett county in Maryland. 24 hour snow amounts were generally 2 to 4 inches in these areas, with isolated 7 to 8 inch reports above 2000 feet.

Low pressure tracked across the northern Great Lakes on January 26 and 27th, pulling an arctic cold front east across the area during the early morning hours of January 28th. Temperatures ahead of the front were in the upper 30s and lower 40s, falling through the day on the 27th with dangerously cold wind chill values developing during the early morning hours of the 28th. West to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with temperatures as low as -11 degrees to produce the wind chill conditions. Meadville recorded wind chill values as low as -26 degrees around 7 am on the 28th. Temperatures only recovered into the single digits during the day on the 28th and then fell to sub zero values again overnight. Nearly all schools were closed on the 28th with most school also closed on the 29th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A dangerously cold air mass settled over northwest Pennsylvania from January 28th with wind chill values of -20 to -30 degrees during the early morning hours of January 28th. The wind chill dropped to -26 at Meadville around 7 am with west to southwest winds of 5 to 15 mph. Conditions started to improve by late morning as temperatures gradually recovered into the single digits but dropped below zero again overnight. All area school were closed on January 28th and most were closed on January 29th.

With strengthening shear, several storms maintained strong updrafts, producing quarter to baseball sized hail over Ohio and the northern West Virginia panhandle.

A few strong thunderstorms developed late in the evening on the 13th. With limited shear...most storms did not maintain themselves to severe levels but one inch hail was reported in Westmoreland county, PA.

Shortwave moving ahead of a cold front supported the development of scattered showers and thunderstorms in the evening on May 21st. Due to the late timing of convection, the best instability was over northern Ohio and Pennsylvania, where the strongest storms occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains the evening of June 24th caused high water to remain across portions of Venango county. Minor road flooding also occurred along roadways near the Connoquenssing and Slippery Rock Creeks as the creeks came out of there banks in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported water on roadways near Slippery Rock Creek.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a person trapped in vehicle form rising water near Carnegie. Numerous roads quickly flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorm clusters developed over northwest/north-central PA well in advance of a cold front crossing the lower great lakes region. Low-level lapse rates steepened in the wake of earlier clouds/precipitation, fostering the development of modest instability bolstered by middle-60s dewpoints. As such, strong to severe storms produced isolated damaging wind gusts during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm wind gusts estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Wilcox.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) coupled with strong heating and convergence along a stalled frontal boundary fostered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. The environment near the stalled boundary was favorable for rotating storms with pronounced veering of winds in the lowest 1-2km AGL. While most storm reports consisted of large hail and damaging winds, an EF1 tornado was confirmed in Bedford County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed well ahead of a slow moving cold front. Some of the thunderstorms were severe, and also produced flash flooding across much of northern Venango county in Pennsylvania, with some flooding in Tuscarawas county Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey found straight line wind damage brought down around 50 trees with 4 falling onto homes in the Glenwillard area. Maximum estimated winds were 85 MPH with the most pronounced damage near Saint Catherine of Siena Church near Route 151.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over the Great Lakes/Ohio Valley progressed slowly east across the Mid-Atlantic and New England states during the afternoon and evening hours. Meanwhile at lower levels, a secondary cold frontal surge crossed the central Appalachians triggering another round of diurnal storms. Strong heating of a very moist, high PW airmass along with moderate deep shear and sufficient large scale forcing promoted organized thunderstorm clusters/bands which produced numerous reports of damaging winds and isolated, marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Mill Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with shearing winds above the surface triggered supercell type thunderstorms that occurred across Eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the afternoon of the 22nd. Hail as large as tennis balls caused crop, siding and automobile damage and even damaged a Berks County mall. A U.S. Airways jet landed with a cracked windshield caused by large hail at Philadelphia International Airport.  The adverse late afternoon weather prevented takeoffs from Philadelphia International Airport until 630 p.m. EDT that evening. There was also some damaging winds, but was not as widespread as the large hail. Nearly 1,000 homes and businesses lost power in Berks County. No serious injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Elsewhere in West Chester, a severe thunderstorm dropped ping pong ball size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong progressive shortwave trough over the Upper MS valley tracked southeastward into the OH valley and Central Appalachians. Rather strong large scale forcing for ascent and destabilization occurred ahead of the trough, resulting a large area of severe weather of central PA. Moderate instability coupled with cool temperatures aloft (steep lapse rates) and favorable effective shear profiles sponsored organized storm/supercell structures which produced several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail (0.88 inch diameter) was reported in Shiremanstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A belt of relatively strong mid-level flow associated within a background cyclonic flow contributed to organized strong to severe storms across central PA. Moderate instability combined with the stronger bulk shear supported isolated damaging wind gusts and hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down utility wires near Blosserville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An 8-year-old boy was struck and killed by lightning while running across an open field after getting out of the Conowingo Creek in Fulton Township Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two boys were struck by lightning while running across a field after getting out of the Little Conowingo Creek in Fulton Township near Peach Bottom in Lancaster County. Authorities said the boys were swimming with friends near Nottingham and Soapstone Roads when a thunderstorm moved through the area. One boy was killed while the other sustained minor injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent upper level troughing from the upper Great Lakes to the Appalachians maintained a broad belt of cyclonic flow from the Northern Plains to the Tennessee Valley and New England. Thunderstorm coverage, intensity and organization escalated gradually through the afternoon as a cold front impinged on a moist and unstable prefrontal airmass over PA and adjacent states. This environment was supportive of a few severe storms that produced isolated damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees on Kilmer Road near Subula.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over the Great Lakes/Ohio Valley progressed slowly east across the Mid-Atlantic and New England states during the afternoon and evening hours. Meanwhile at lower levels, a secondary cold frontal surge crossed the central Appalachians triggering another round of diurnal storms. Strong heating of a very moist, high PW airmass along with moderate deep shear and sufficient large scale forcing promoted organized thunderstorm clusters/bands which produced numerous reports of damaging winds and isolated, marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in several locations across the southern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system located over southern Ontario along with surface boundaries in the vicinity of northeast Pennsylvania resulted in severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening hours. These storms developed in an unstable airmass with severe thunderstorms producing torrential rainfall and flooding. A solitary, long lasting thunderstorm produced in excess of 6 of rain across northwest Susquehanna county. There was significant devastation to the road network, and a trailer park was seriously flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe flash flooding damaged several roads in the area, with significant flooding on County Route 267. Three to four other roads were destroyed and impassable.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads in the northwest and central part of the county were inundated by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A remnant mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) coupled with strong heating and convergence along a stalled frontal boundary fostered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. The environment near the stalled boundary was favorable for rotating storms with pronounced veering of winds in the lowest 1-2km AGL. While most storm reports consisted of large hail and damaging winds, an EF1 tornado was confirmed in Bedford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey confirmed an EF1 tornado in Bedford County, with maximum winds around 95 mph. The tornado touched down around 1910 EDT near Everett and crossed U.S. Route 30, starting an approximate 11 mile damage path to the northeast toward Hopewell. The damage path was not continuous, as the tornado intermittently lifted up and touched back down several times during its lifespan. Damage included sporadic uprooted and snapped trees, along with structural damage to two homes and two barns - one of which was destroyed. The tornado lifted for the final time around 1920 EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with shearing winds above the surface triggered supercell type thunderstorms that occurred across Eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the afternoon of the 22nd. Hail as large as tennis balls caused crop, siding and automobile damage and even damaged a Berks County mall. A U.S. Airways jet landed with a cracked windshield caused by large hail at Philadelphia International Airport.  The adverse late afternoon weather prevented takeoffs from Philadelphia International Airport until 630 p.m. EDT that evening. There was also some damaging winds, but was not as widespread as the large hail. Nearly 1,000 homes and businesses lost power in Berks County. No serious injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped ping pong ball size hail in Glen Mills along the Concord and Middletown Township line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low over Ontario moved south with its primary cold front sagging across the Great Lakes region into the Ohio Valley. Moist and unstable conditions ahead of the cold front where dewpoints climbed into the upper 60s to low 70s and temperatures with the 80s and 90s. Thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon and become organized in conjunction with increasing mid-level height falls and winds aloft. The severe storms produced multiple reports of damaging winds and a few reports of hail from the Allegheny Mountains into the Middle Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Petersburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cyclonic flow aloft prevailed over the Northeast states on southern fringe of nearly stationary upper low centered over eastern Hudson Bay. Shortwave impulses rotating around the southeast flank of the low maintained a seasonably strong 700-500mb west-southwest flow. The combination of strong heating, high moisture content (PW) and moderately steep lapse rates contributed to the development of severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires around the Williamsport area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers and thunderstorms impacted the area during the late evening as a strong cold front moved across the region. In an environment characterized by 1.75 to 2 inch PWS, training/slow-moving thunderstorm cells with very heavy rain rates produced isolated flash flooding in Lancaster County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding in Earl Township. Locations that experienced flooding included Bareville, Hinkletown and New Holland. Flood waters closed several secondary roads in the affected areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms produced isolated flash flooding during the evening across the lower Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding and closed several roads across the county. High water forced several road closures from the west shore area to the Market Street bridge and Lemoyne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent upper level troughing from the upper Great Lakes to the Appalachians maintained a broad belt of cyclonic flow from the Northern Plains to the Tennessee Valley and New England. Thunderstorm coverage, intensity and organization escalated gradually through the afternoon as a cold front impinged on a moist and unstable prefrontal airmass over PA and adjacent states. This environment was supportive of a few severe storms that produced isolated damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees on Knobs Road east of Parker Dam State Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong progressive shortwave trough over the Upper MS valley tracked southeastward into the OH valley and Central Appalachians. Rather strong large scale forcing for ascent and destabilization occurred ahead of the trough, resulting a large area of severe weather of central PA. Moderate instability coupled with cool temperatures aloft (steep lapse rates) and favorable effective shear profiles sponsored organized storm/supercell structures which produced several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in and around Hollidaysburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A survey team confirmed a microburst in South Annville Township around 1925 EDT. The straight line wind damage was most concentrated just north of U.S. 322 from Mt. Pleasant Road to SR 934 (S. White Oak Street). The damage consisted of a portion of a metal roof off a farm house, minor structural damage to several farm/outbuildings, one utility pole down, crop damage to corn fields and numerous trees snapped off or knocked down. Based on the damage surveyed, the maximum thunderstorm winds were estimated at 80 mph. The maximum path width was approximately 50 yards with damage spread out (sporadically) along a 2 mile path.
