EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread shower and thunderstorm activity developed across central PA in vicinity of a weak surface boundary as an upper level trough slowly tracked east across the region. Strong southerly flow aided deep moisture transport, sponsoring thunderstorms locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced significant flash flooding, which eventually transitioned into a long-duration flooding event as standing water and road closures persisted into 13 June 2014. The Franklin County EOC was partially activated due to the heavy rainfall. There were two water rescues for stranded vehicles in St. Thomas and Antrim Townships. All occupants in both vehicles were rescued safely. Lincoln Highway WB in St. Thomas Township was closed. The Peckman Bridge in St. Thomas Township is closed due to bridge damage. There were multiple homes flooded in the Red Run Park Subdivision in Washington Township. Flooding impacts in Montgomery Township included Welch Run Road, Ft Loudon Rd, Montgomery Church Road, Mercersburg Road, Hunter Rd, Two Top Rd Blairs Valley Rd, Shimbstown Rd and Punchbowl Intersection of Welch Run Rd. Multiple homes (single family dwellings) experienced flooding. Flooding impacts in Peters Township included the closure of Geibs and McDowell roads with up to 12 inches of standing water on most township roads. Flooding impacts in Antrim Township included closure of the following roads: Gitner Rd; 6700 block and 2800 block of Cold Smith Rd; Rabbit Road South off of Buchannan Trail West. Gilford Township Angle Road was completed submerged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms reached marginally severe limits from late morning into the afternoon. A mid-level impulse moved through south-central Ontario and into Quebec with a trailing cold front extending southward from near Toronto to western Lake Erie and the Upper Ohio Valley. Initial storms over northwest PA formed along a weak pre-frontal convergence zone with enhancement from lake breeze, while later storms were triggered via the more defined wind shift/cold front which crossed the Alleghenies during the mid-afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail (1 inch diameter) was reported in Tidioute.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain preceding a cold frontal passage caused flash flooding in southeastern Pennsylvania in and around Philadelphia.  Event precipitation totals reached as high as around 3 inches in Chester County, most of which fell within two hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavy rain caused flash flooding along the Frankford Creek. The Frankford Creek at Castor Avenue was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1225 a.m. EDT through 1239 a.m. EDT on the 28th. It crested at 8.27 feet at 1230 a.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 1.43 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 1.37 inches in Wyndmoor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain preceding a cold frontal passage caused flash flooding in southeastern Pennsylvania in and around Philadelphia.  Event precipitation totals reached as high as around 3 inches in Chester County, most of which fell within two hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused flash flooding in and around Elverson. A tributary to the North Branch of the French Creek flooded South Brick Lane. Flooding likely also occurred near the headwaters of the East Branch of the Conestoga River. A Skywarn spotter measured 2.6 inches in one hour in Elverson. Other event totals included 3.11 inches in Honey Brook, 2.56 inches in Warwick and 2.39 inches in Glenmoore.

Hail as large as tennis balls caused crop, siding and automobile damage and even damaged a Berks County mall. A U.S. Airways jet landed with a cracked windshield caused by large hail at Philadelphia International Airport.  The adverse late afternoon weather prevented takeoffs from Philadelphia International Airport until 630 p.m. EDT that evening. There was also some damaging winds, but was not as widespread as the large hail. Nearly 1,000 homes and businesses lost power in Berks County. No serious injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down several large trees along South Concord Road from Amelia Drive to Westtown Road in West Chester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong progressive shortwave trough over the Upper MS valley tracked southeastward into the OH valley and Central Appalachians. Rather strong large scale forcing for ascent and destabilization occurred ahead of the trough, resulting a large area of severe weather of central PA. Moderate instability coupled with cool temperatures aloft (steep lapse rates) and favorable effective shear profiles sponsored organized storm/supercell structures which produced several reports of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Bellefonte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain preceding a cold frontal passage caused flash flooding in southeastern Pennsylvania in and around Philadelphia.  Event precipitation totals reached as high as around 3 inches in Chester County, most of which fell within two hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused minor flooding along the West Branch of the Brandywine Creek during the early morning of the 28th.  The West Branch of the Brandywine Creek near Honey Brook was above its 7 foot flood stage from 211 a.m. EDT through 625 a.m. EDT on the 28th. It crested at 7.71 feet at 415 a.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 3.11 inches in Honey Brook, 2.56 inches in Warwick and 2.39 inches in Glenmoore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave moving along a stalled front initiated thunderstorms that produced flash flooding and 2 weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief EF-0 tornado was found from near Fuller and Center Roads to near the State Game Lands. Maximum estimated winds were 80 MPH with a path width of 20 yards. Damage was limited to hardwood trees with no structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread shower and thunderstorm activity developed across central PA in vicinity of a weak surface boundary as an upper level trough slowly tracked east across the region. Strong southerly flow aided deep moisture transport, sponsoring thunderstorms locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall that initially produced flash flooding on 12 June 2014 transitioned into a longer duration flood event, with standing water and road closures persisting into the morning of 13 June 2014. EMA offials reported roads in Lurgan Township (Upper Horse Valley) had notable flooding and PennDOT deployed detour signs. Pumps were assembled for transport from three local fire departments to the St. Thomas Fire Department. Franklin County Department of Emergency Services deployed 500 sandbags to St. Thomas. The St. Thomas EMA reported Back Creek came out of its banks and flooded the Route 30 Bridge. St. Thomas Fire Department ordered the evacuation of residents along Back Creek with additional resources requested from Huntington County Fire Company 10.  The very upper end of Back Creek crested late on the 12th and begun to drop. A total of 26 agencies from Franklin County and municipalities, in addition to resources from Adams County (Fairfield) and Huntington County (Smithfield) were deployed for this flood incident. ||Shortly after midnight on the 13th, streams stopped rising and flood waters started to recede. The Mercersburg area was still dealing with several flooded basements. By 2 pm on the 13th, most roads were open and repair to bridges that took damage commenced. A Community assessment team reported that corn crops are under water in the Back Creek area of St Thomas and Antrim Township. No municipal disaster declarations were made, but a county-wide disaster declaration was approved and took effect on June 17 2014.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cyclonic flow aloft prevailed over the Northeast states on southern fringe of nearly stationary upper low centered over eastern Hudson Bay. Shortwave impulses rotating around the southeast flank of the low maintained a seasonably strong 700-500mb west-southwest flow. The combination of strong heating, high moisture content (PW) and moderately steep lapse rates contributed to the development of severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph uprooted several large trees along Route 419 just east of Cornwall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy downpours produced flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and far southeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. In this last band, Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 4 inches, while in the other two bands, Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 1.5 and 3.0 inches.

Most of this rain fell within an hour.

Strong heating of a very moist, high PW airmass along with moderate deep shear and sufficient large scale forcing promoted organized thunderstorm clusters/bands which produced numerous reports of damaging winds and isolated, marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Mahaffey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over the Great Lakes/Ohio Valley progressed slowly east across the Mid-Atlantic and New England states during the afternoon and evening hours. Meanwhile at lower levels, a secondary cold frontal surge crossed the central Appalachians triggering another round of diurnal storms. Strong heating of a very moist, high PW airmass along with moderate deep shear and sufficient large scale forcing promoted organized thunderstorm clusters/bands which produced numerous reports of damaging winds and isolated, marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Millerstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front with shearing winds above the surface triggered supercell type thunderstorms that occurred across Eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the afternoon of the 22nd. Hail as large as tennis balls caused crop, siding and automobile damage and even damaged a Berks County mall. A U.S. Airways jet landed with a cracked windshield caused by large hail at Philadelphia International Airport.  The adverse late afternoon weather prevented takeoffs from Philadelphia International Airport until 630 p.m. EDT that evening. There was also some damaging winds, but was not as widespread as the large hail. Nearly 1,000 homes and businesses lost power in Berks County. No serious injuries were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped half dollar size hail in Exton (West Whiteland Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of rich deep-layer moisture extended from the Southeast states through the Mid-Atlantic region on the equatorward side of sustained cyclonic flow over the Northeast U.S. Sufficient bulk shear in the 30-35KT range proved favorable for a few isolated reports of severe weather during the afternoon and evening. Marginal instability generally limited the overall coverage and extent of severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail (1 inch diameter) was reported in Pine Grove Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southerly flow in advance of frontal zone lured a warm and very moist airmass into central PA, with PW values above the 90th percentile for late June. Very slow storm motions coupled with hourly rainfall rates in excess of 2 inches contributed to localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water from Two Mile Run caused approximately thirty residents of Sheffield Township to be evacuated to the Sheffield Fire Department. The American Red Cross established a shelter at the Sheffield United Methodist Church. The shelter has been closed. Several roadways were reported closed including SR 27 and SR 666. Small landslides were also reported. Rock and debris blocked the northbound lanes of SR 62 approximately 2 miles north of the Tidioute Bridge. SR 27 had approximately 2 feet of water on it. Neiltown Road was impassable. SR 666 was closed from Queen Road to the Forest County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southerly flow in advance of frontal zone lured a warm and very moist airmass into central PA, with PW values above the 90th percentile for late June. Very slow storm motions coupled with hourly rainfall rates in excess of 2 inches contributed to localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters caused some residents of Mansfield Borough and Richmond Township to be evacuated. A shelter was established at the Mansfield Fire Station.  In Mansfield |Borough, the following areas were evacuated: Fourth Street, Second Street; and East Main Street. In Richmond Township, New Town Hill was evacuated. A shelter was been opened at the Mansfield Fire Station. Corey Creek came out of its banks and flooded 2-3 homes. Route 6 was flooded at the intersection of SR 549 in Richmond Township. A landslide also occurred on Route 6 covering the roadway with water, gravel and mud. When the water receded, fire department personnel were engaged in basement pumping.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist boundary layer coupled with strong heating/instability resulted in heavy showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon. PW values near 2 inches were pooled ahead of a cold front that approached the area from the lower Great Lakes region. The high moisture content of the airmass in place along with some training and interaction with topography promoted intense rainfall rates and excessive rainfall amounts - which lead to localized flash flooding over portions of southeast PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flash flooding near Red Lion and Craley. Flood waters damaged and closed West Broadway and South Main Street in Red Lion. Bull Run came out of its banks and flooded Bull Run Road near Craley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper low over Ontario moved south with its primary cold front sagging across the Great Lakes region into the Ohio Valley. Moist and unstable conditions ahead of the cold front where dewpoints climbed into the upper 60s to low 70s and temperatures with the 80s and 90s. Thunderstorms developed during the early afternoon and become organized in conjunction with increasing mid-level height falls and winds aloft. The severe storms produced multiple reports of damaging winds and a few reports of hail from the Allegheny Mountains into the Middle Susquehanna Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires in Johnstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot start to July in Eastern Pennsylvania peaked on the 2nd with (except for the Poconos) high temperatures in the lower to mid 90s and afternoon heat index values reaching 100 to 105 degrees. An approaching cold front which started to trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms made the 3rd slightly cooler. The passage of Hurricane Arthur to the east on Independence Day ended the run of 90 degree weather. Highest temperatures included 96 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and Yardley (Bucks County), 95 degrees in Blue Bell (Montgomery County), 94 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Line Lexington (Bucks County), Modena and Kennett Square (Chester County), Swarthmore (Delaware County), Martins Creek (Northampton County) and Reading (Berks County), 93 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County) and Forks Township (Northampton County) and 92 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County).

An approaching lee side trof coupled with a hot and humid air mass triggered a line of strong to locally severe thunderstorms that affected Eastern Pennsylvania during the late evening of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in Stroudsburg, especially on the south side of the borough.  Some roadways were closed. One vehicle was damaged by a downed tree on Bryant Street. Even a Red Cross Disaster Relief Trailer was damaged by a downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass and a lee side trough helped trigger a powerful squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through all of Eastern Pennsylvania during the evening of the 8th. About 400,000 homes and businesses lost power in central and eastern Pennsylvania.  PECO Energy reported 260,000 of its customers lost power in southeastern Pennsylvania as this line moved through. About 100,000 customers were without power on the morning of the 9th and 40,000 on the afternoon of the 9th. Chester County had about half of the remaining outages. It took until the afternoon of the 11th to have power fully restored. Restoration crews from a dozen states assisted. Metropolitan Edison reported about 61,000 of its Pennsylvania customers lost power and Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 75,000 of its Pennsylvania Customers (28,000 on the morning of the 9th) also lost power. Power was restored to all of their customers by the night of the 10th. In addition, about 7,000 homes and businesses lost power in Berks County. Preceding this line, an isolated severe thunderstorm affected Bucks County during the afternoon of the 8th.||The downed trees and power lines also caused commuting problems. Numerous roads were blocked by fallen trees. Amtrak suspended its Keystone Line between Philadelphia and Harrisburg because of downed trees. In southeast Pennsylvania, SEPTA Regional Commuter Rail Service had to suspend service along Paoli-Thorndale, Fox Chase, Airport and Chestnut Hill Lines because of tree and line problems. Some SEPTA Trolly service was also suspended in Delaware County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line severe thunderstorm knocked down a large tree and several large tree limbs in Wernersville Borough.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported Oak Grove Street had been flooded and closed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of severe thunderstorms knocked down numerous trees and wires mainly across the northern half of Lehigh County. In North Whitehall Township, a back-up generator started a house fire. Wind gusts of 56 mph and 47 mph were measured within Allentown and at the Lehigh Valley International Airport respectively. The combination of damaging winds and lightning caused about 6,500 homes and businesses to lose power in the Lehigh Valley. It took until the night of the 10th to have power fully restored.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level shortwave trough in the vicinity of Lake Michigan moved eastward to the Upper OH valley by the evening and across the Appalachians overnight. An associated surface cyclone moved from Lake Huron into southwest Quebec, while a weak secondary low developed over the Mid-Atlantic Piedmont and moved north from NC to NJ. In the wake of the surface lows, a cold front progressed eastward from the OH valley through the TN valley and Appalachians. Modest buoyancy supported by a corridor of 65-70F dewpoints preceding the cold front coincided with increasing mid-level flow/shear to produce a broken band of strong to severe storms with local damaging winds from eastern OH into western PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees down in Mead, Glade, Elk, Pine Grove, Deerfield and Freehold Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms that formed near a stalled frontal boundary along the Pennsylvania border caused flooding in Chester County overnight on the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused minor flooding along the East Branch of the Brandywine Creek during the early morning of the 11th.  The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek below Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1250 a.m. EDT through 249 a.m. EDT on the 11th. It crested at 7.24 feet at 200 a.m. EDT. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around 3.5 inches in central Chester County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot start to July in Eastern Pennsylvania peaked on the 2nd with (except for the Poconos) high temperatures in the lower to mid 90s and afternoon heat index values reaching 100 to 105 degrees. An approaching cold front which started to trigger a series of showers and thunderstorms made the 3rd slightly cooler. The passage of Hurricane Arthur to the east on Independence Day ended the run of 90 degree weather. Highest temperatures included 96 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and Yardley (Bucks County), 95 degrees in Blue Bell (Montgomery County), 94 degrees at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Line Lexington (Bucks County), Modena and Kennett Square (Chester County), Swarthmore (Delaware County), Martins Creek (Northampton County) and Reading (Berks County), 93 degrees in Doylestown (Bucks County) and Forks Township (Northampton County) and 92 degrees in Pottstown (Montgomery County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy downpours produced flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and far southeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. There were a couple of corridors of very heavy rain: one across the lower Lehigh Valley, a second across central Montgomery and Bucks Counties and the third and heaviest along the Delaware River in Philadelphia and Bucks Counties. In this last band, Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 4 inches, while in the other two bands, Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 1.5 and 3.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused poor drainage flash flooding in Center City Philadelphia.  Storm drains could not keep up with the run-off near Thomas Jefferson University Hospital at the intersection of 12th and Chestnut Streets. Event precipitation totals included 2.16 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 1.86 inches in Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of weak low pressure around the Northern Great Lakes and an upper level disturbance passing over portions of eastern Pennsylvania produced scattered strong thunderstorms with heavy rainfall that resulted in areas of flash flooding and poor drainage flooding from the late evening of the 21st into the early morning of the 22nd. Event precipitation totals reached as high as 2 to around 4 inches across portions of eastern Pennsylvania, especially in Northampton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage and small stream flash flooding in Northampton County. Road closures were reported in Forks Township and Belfast. A sinkhole developed at the intersection of Freemansburg Avenue and Emrick Boulevard in Bethlehem Township. Entire event precipitation totals included 4.12 inches in Nazareth, 3.56 inches in Palmer Township, 3.22 inches in Plainfield Township, 2.47 inches in Martins Creek, and 2.05 inches in Bethlehem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid air mass and a lee side trough helped trigger a powerful squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through all of Eastern Pennsylvania during the evening of the 8th. About 400,000 homes and businesses lost power in central and eastern Pennsylvania.  PECO Energy reported 260,000 of its customers lost power in southeastern Pennsylvania as this line moved through. About 100,000 customers were without power on the morning of the 9th and 40,000 on the afternoon of the 9th. Chester County had about half of the remaining outages. It took until the afternoon of the 11th to have power fully restored. Restoration crews from a dozen states assisted. Metropolitan Edison reported about 61,000 of its Pennsylvania customers lost power and Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 75,000 of its Pennsylvania Customers (28,000 on the morning of the 9th) also lost power. Power was restored to all of their customers by the night of the 10th. In addition, about 7,000 homes and businesses lost power in Berks County. Preceding this line, an isolated severe thunderstorm affected Bucks County during the afternoon of the 8th.||The downed trees and power lines also caused commuting problems. Numerous roads were blocked by fallen trees. Amtrak suspended its Keystone Line between Philadelphia and Harrisburg because of downed trees. In southeast Pennsylvania, SEPTA Regional Commuter Rail Service had to suspend service along Paoli-Thorndale, Fox Chase, Airport and Chestnut Hill Lines because of tree and line problems. Some SEPTA Trolly service was also suspended in Delaware County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike started a house fire in William Township on Morgan Hill Road. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a cold frontal passage and an unseasonably hot and humid air mass generated a series of strong to locally severe thunderstorms that moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and subsequent fire damaged a home on Harding Avenue in Abington Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front coupled with a hot and humid air mass triggered strong to locally severe thunderstorms that affected Philadelphia and its nearby suburbs during the evening and overnight on the 27th.  Lightning strikes also occurred damage in Chester County. The thunderstorms also caused some flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees in Lower Salford Township. Trees were knocked down along Cressman Road, Shadynook Road, Old Skippack Road, Main Street, Maple Avenue, Clemens Road and Astor Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of lines of strong to severe thunderstorms triggered by another lee side trof moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. This line not only caused wind, hail and lightning damage around the local Philadelphia area, but also caused flash flooding. PECO Energy reported 17,600 of its customers lost power in southeastern Pennsylvania. About 800 customers were still without power the next day.  Philadelphia International Airport experienced one hour flight delays during the height of the thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and injured a 14-year-old boy on East Ashland Avenue in Glenolden Borough. The boy suffered a cut to his hand and reported tingling in that arm. Lightning caused about 5,000 PECO Energy customers to lose power in Delaware County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm also knocked down a couple of trees in central Philadelphia. One downed tree damaged a vehicle on Palmetto Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weakening approaching cold front triggered a series of strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and in particular during the evening in Eastern Pennsylvania. Lightning strikes also caused two fires in Bucks County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and ensuing fire heavily damaged four units of the Orangewood Apartments off of Mill Creek Road in the Violetwood section of Levittown in Bristol Township.  The fire spread along the roof and attic portions of the two story apartment building. Four families were displaced. Approximately 500 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes in Bucks County between 8 p.m. and 9 p.m. EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and a hot and humid air mass combined to trigger some strong to locally severe thunderstorms over southeast Pennsylvania during the evening of the 2nd.  The thunderstorms caused a ground stop at Philadelphia International Airport that evening and departure delays of over two hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a retirement home in Pocopson Township.  No serious damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal wave of low pressure that originated in the Gulf of Mexico developed into a strong coastal storm as it moved northeast just off the East Coast. This high-impact system brought a wet, moderate-to-heavy snowfall to much of Central Pennsylvania which wreaked havoc on Thanksgiving holiday travelers. The snow expanded from south to north during the early to mid morning hours and continued, heavy at times, through the afternoon before tapering off in the evening. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches with locally higher amounts around a foot on the ridge tops near South Mountain. The precipitation began as rain for areas to the southeast of I-81 before changing to snow, which cut down on storm total amounts. Numerous state offices and schools were closed and multiple flights were delayed or cancelled. The storm could not have come at a worse time on what is typically one of the busiest travel days of the year (day before Thanksgiving). Williamsport set a daily snowfall record of 4.8 inches, which broke the previous record of 1.8 inches set in 2013. The storm made the NESIS scale as a category 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high impact snowfall of 3 to 6 inches affected the area basically from dawn to dusk.

The snow expanded from south to north during the early to mid morning hours and continued, heavy at times, through the afternoon before tapering off in the evening. Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches with locally higher amounts around a foot on the ridge tops near South Mountain. The precipitation began as rain for areas to the southeast of I-81 before changing to snow, which cut down on storm total amounts. Numerous state offices and schools were closed and multiple flights were delayed or cancelled. The storm could not have come at a worse time on what is typically one of the busiest travel days of the year (day before Thanksgiving). Williamsport set a daily snowfall record of 4.8 inches, which broke the previous record of 1.8 inches set in 2013. The storm made the NESIS scale as a category 1.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high-impact snowfall of 3 to 6 inches affected the area basically from dawn to dusk. Lesser amounts were observed in and around the Harrisburg metro area/southeast of I-81.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front with waves of low pressure produced bands of heavy rain with embedded thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania on the 15th. The most widespread heavy rain fell in Berks and western Carbon County. Localized heavy rain produced some poor drainage flooding elsewhere including Philadelphia. The steady rain started during the early morning on the 15th, fell at its heaviest from late morning into the early evening on the 15th and ended from west to east early on the 16th as the cold front cleared Eastern Pennsylvania. Event totals averaged 1.5 to 4.0 inches.  Dry antecedent conditions prevented much stream and creek flooding from occurring.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain forced the closure of Ford Road in West Fairmount Park within Philadelphia.  Heavy rain caused additional poor drainage flooding within the city.  Event precipitation totals included 1.48 inches at Northeast Philadelphia Airport and 1.27 inches in Center City Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The pressure gradient (difference) between a strong high pressure system over the lower Mississippi Valley and a strong low pressure system east of James Bay, Canada produced gusty northwest winds over the higher terrain of the Poconos during the morning of the 18th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and knocked down weak tree limbs and power lines. Pennsylvania Power and Light reported about 500 of its customers lost power. Peak wind gusts included 46 mph at Mount Pocono (Monroe County). As the high pressure system continued to move closer to the area and the low pressure system farther away in Canada, winds slowly diminished during the afternoon and more rapidly at night.

Strong gusty northwest winds around 45 mph knocked over some weak tree limbs and wires over the higher terrain of the Poconos during the second half of the morning and early afternoon on the 26th. Some isolated power outages occurred. The peak wind gust in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) was 46 mph.

The first significant lake effect snow of the 2014-15 winter season brought heavy accumulations up to a foot across the perennial snowbelt of northwest Warren County. Lower amounts of 6 inches or less were common over the southeastern part of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow spotters in northwest Warren County reported lake effect snow amounts in excess of 6 inches with local amounts up to 1 foot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant lake effect snow impacted the favored snowbelt region of northwest Warren County. Local amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total lake-effect snowfall amounts ranged from 6 to 12 inches over the northwest part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain and road icing resulted in very hazardous travel across the Central Mountains during the afternoon and evening. The quick glaze of ice on roads caused several vehicle accidents and closed multiple roads. Interstate 80 in Centre and Clinton Counties was closed in both directions at multiple locations. One of the incidents included a double fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain caused traffic accidents and road closures throughout the county, including portions of Interstates 80 and 99. Two people were killed after a crash on Interstate 80 in Marion Township. Police said 59 year-old woman and 64 year-old man died after the Volvo they were driving in was crushed between two tractor trailers. The fatality accident occurred around 1630PM EST on I-80 westbound at mile marker 170.||State Police at Rockview said the tractor trailer and Volvo were slowing down for traffic, when another tractor trailer behind them failed to slow down and slammed into the back of the Volvo, pushing it into the back of the other tractor trailer. At least 20 cars were reportedly involved in fender benders and wrecks along I-99 in the Port Matilda area also because of icy conditions.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain caused traffic accidents and road closures throughout the county, including portions of Interstates 80 and 99. There were no serious injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds during the early morning of the 24th tore down weak tree limbs in southeastern Pennsylvania and caused about 1,000 PECO Energy customers to lose power, mainly in Delaware and Philadelphia Counties. Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in Newtown Square (Delaware County), 49 mph at Chestnut Hill (Philadelphia) and 46 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport.  The strong winds occurred as a warm front was lifting north of the Philadelphia region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated damaging wind gusts and small hail occurred with thunderstorms along a southeastward progressing cold front across the Lower Susquehanna Valley during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lancaster ASOS measured a peak thunderstorm wind gust of 50 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed near the gulf coast on February 4th and tracked northeast through the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys to near Pittsburgh by the morning of February 5th. As this system tracked northeast it brought heavy snow to northwest Pennsylvania. The heaviest snow occurred closer to Lake Erie with reports of 7-12 inches.  Snowfall totals decreased towards Crawford County where snowfall averaged closer to 6 inches with up to a tenth of an inch of icing as precipitation mixed with freezing rain and sleet on the morning of the 5th. Temperatures were in the mid 20s when the mixed precipitation occurred on the morning of the 5th allowing slick conditions to develop quickly. Northeast winds shifted to the northwest by the afternoon of the 5th with speeds increasing to 15 to 20 mph with gusts to 25 mph inland and as high as 37 mph near Lake Erie.  The combination of falling snow and strong winds caused periods of near white out conditions near Erie on the morning of the 5th.  The snow tapered off from west to east during the afternoon of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed near the gulf coast on February 4th and tracked northeast through the Tennessee and Ohio Valleys to near Pittsburgh by the morning of February 5th. As this system tracked northeast it brought heavy snow to northwest Pennsylvania. The heaviest snow occurred closer to Lake Erie with reports of 7-12 inches. Northeast winds shifted to the northwest by the afternoon of the 5th with speeds increasing to 15 to 20 mph with gusts as high as 37 mph near Lake Erie.  The combination of falling snow and strong winds caused periods of white out conditions on the morning of the 5th. Temperatures were in the mid 20s during the snowstorm.  The snow tapered off during the afternoon of the 5th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail (1 inch diameter) was reported in the Lakemont section of Altoona.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated pulse-severe thunderstorms produced large hail and localized damaging winds near Altoona and Johnstown during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a few trees along Courtland Avenue on the southwest side of Altoona.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail (1 inch diameter) was reported near South Fork. A few larger stones up to golf-ball size were also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A developing cold front triggered snow showers in Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 26th. The snow showers and snowfall were greatest in Berks County, Philadelphia and its suburbs averaging 1 to 2 inches. Less than one inch of snow on average fell elsewhere in Eastern Pennsylvania. The snow showers started between 5 a.m. and 6 a.m. EST on the 26th, fell at their heaviest intensity during the middle of the morning and finished moving through between 10 a.m. EST and 1130 a.m. EST that day. During the heavier snow bursts, untreated and less traveled roadways became snow covered and slippery.  ||Representative snowfall included 1.9 inches in West Grove (Chester County), 1.8 inches in Kutztown (Berks County),1.3 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County) and Norwood (Delaware County), 1.2 inches in Warwick (Chester County) and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 1.1 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.0 inch in Nottingham (Chester County) and Fleetwood (Berks County). A cold front developed over Western Pennsylvania early on the 26th and progressed southeast. It moved through Eastern Pennsylvania  between 9 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST on the 26th and coincided with the end of the heavier snow showers.

A major winter storm brought heavy snow, sleet and freezing rain to the Poconos, a heavy wintry mixture to the Lehigh Valley and Berks County and a crippling ice storm to Philadelphia and in particular its western suburbs. Snowfall reached as high as ten inches in the Poconos and ice accumulations were as high as half an inch. The combination of new ice and snow still on the trees from the just concluded winter storm caused a record breaking 715,000 customer power outages in the PECO Energy service area in and around Philadelphia (849.000 throughout Pennsylvania). This was the worst power outage event ever for the winter season, represented about 45 percent of all its customers and the second worst behind Sandy for the utility. It took about one week to have power fully restored. The weight of the snow, then sleet and freezing rain on limbs all collaborated to cause more tree damage then would have occurred if trees were bare at the start of this event.  Governor Tom Corbett declared a State of Emergency. President Barack Obama also declared a state of emergency in Pennsylvania. Many counties and municipalities also declared snow emergencies or states of emergency. Government offices, schools and businesses were closed. Some schools as well as universities, such as Villanova and West Chester, remained closed for the rest of the week. More than a dozen hospitals in the Philadelphia area ran on generators. Red Cross shelters and warming centers were opened. Some boil water advisories were also in effect because water treatment plants lost power.||In Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, precipitation started as sleet (and a bit of snow) during the late evening on the 4th. Precipitation changed over to freezing rain within a couple of hours of the start. Precipitation then changed to plain rain from south to north between 6 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 5th. The rain ended that afternoon. In Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, precipitation started as snow right around Midnight EST on the 5th. The snow fell heavy at times before sunrise in the Poconos. In the Lehigh Valley and Berks County, precipitation changed to sleet between 1 a.m. EST and 2 a.m. EST on the 5th and to freezing rain between 2 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the 5th. Precipitation changed to plain rain between 8 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST that morning and ended during that afternoon. In the Poconos, precipitation changed to sleet around 7 a.m. EST on the 5th, then freezing rain around 9 a.m. EST that morning. Some valley locations changed to plain rain, but over the higher terrain freezing rain persisted until precipitation ended during the middle of the afternoon.  A very light wintry mix then occurred in the Poconos during the early evening of the 5th. |  |The greatest impact from the winter and ice storm occurred in the Philadelphia suburbs as power was lost for up to one week. Repair crews from throughout North America assisted. About 5,100 workers were involved in the restoration process. Sections of the Schuylkill Expressway and U.S. Routes 202 and 322 were closed because of downed trees. Downed trees also littered the Pennsylvania Turnpike, the Pennsylvania Turnpike Northeast Extension and U.S. Routes 30 and 1. There were posted speed reductions on the Pennsylvania Turnpike and an empty tractor-trailer ban. Commuter rail and bus service was also affected. Amtrak rail service between Philadelphia and Harrisburg was suspended until the 8th. SEPTA suspended four regional rail lines until the 6th. Others ran with delays of at least 30 minutes. ||In Chester County, whole municipalities lost power as did 87 percent of the county overall. There were about 400 tree blocked roadways in the county.  Hardest hit municipalities included Highland, Charlestown, East Nantmeal, Parkesburg and West Vincent. West Chester University was used as a shelter. About 265 Verizon telephone customers lost service in West Fallowfield Township. In Montgomery County, there were 340 calls for electrical fires, 1,001 road obstructions, 153 crashes, and more than 4,000 calls for police assistance. Fifty major roadways were blocked by fallen trees. In Philadelphia, there were 139 cancelled flights at the Philadelphia International Airport on the 5th. A fallen tree limb injured one man. In Bucks County, over 90 percent of Doylestown, New Hope and Yardley lost power. Eighty state maintained highways were blocked by fallen trees. Power outages in Delaware County were the worst along its border with Chester County. Throughout the Philadelphia area, dozens of people suffered carbon monoxide poisoning because of improper ventilation of generators. ||In the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, power outages were less as more precipitation fell as snow and sleet. The total number of customers without power were around 1,000. In Lynn Township (Lehigh County), a collision between a pick-up truck and van resulted in the death of a 52-year-old man and a 58-year-old woman and one additional injury. In Northampton County,  Pennsylvania State Route 33 Northbound was closed near Freemansburg Avenue in Bethlehem Township due to a crash that involved a tractor trailer and a bus.||The number of PECO Energy customer outages peaked at 715,000 on the 5th. It dropped to 499,000 on the morning of the 6th, 440,000 on the evening of the 6th, 275,000 on the afternoon of the 7th, 182,000 on the evening of the 7th, 160,000 on the morning of the 8th, 82,000 on the evening of the 8th, 23,000 on the afternoon of the 9th and 5,600 on the 10th. This ice storm also made it the worst winter for outages in the utility's history: 1,097,900 outages. This low pressure system moved northeast and reached Mississippi during the early evening of the 4th and the Tennessee River Valley around Midnight EST on the 5th. About the same time, a secondary low pressure system was forming on the primary low's warm front off the North Carolina coast. Both low pressure systems in tandem proceeded to move northeast and at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th, the primary low pressure system was over southeast Ohio with the secondary low pressure system off the Delaware coast. As is typically the case, the primary low pressure system weakened and reached western Pennsylvania during the early afternoon on the 5th while the stronger secondary low pressure system was just off of Long Beach Island on the New Jersey coast. This secondary low pressure system intensified as it rapidly moved northeast and was southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts at 4 p.m. EST on the 5th. What was left of the primary low pressure system trundled across Eastern Pennsylvania and Northern New Jersey during the late afternoon and early evening of the 5th.

A developing cold front triggered snow showers in Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 26th. The snow showers and snowfall were greatest in Berks County, Philadelphia and its suburbs averaging 1 to 2 inches. Less than one inch of snow on average fell elsewhere in Eastern Pennsylvania. The snow showers started between 5 a.m. and 6 a.m. EST on the 26th, fell at their heaviest intensity during the middle of the morning and finished moving through between 10 a.m. EST and 1130 a.m. EST that day. During the heavier snow bursts, untreated and less traveled roadways became snow covered and slippery.  ||Representative snowfall included 1.9 inches in West Grove (Chester County), 1.8 inches in Kutztown (Berks County),1.3 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County) and Norwood (Delaware County), 1.2 inches in Warwick (Chester County) and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 1.1 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.0 inch in Nottingham (Chester County) and Fleetwood (Berks County). A cold front developed over Western Pennsylvania early on the 26th and progressed southeast. It moved through Eastern Pennsylvania  between 9 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST on the 26th and coincided with the end of the heavier snow showers.

Snow fell across Eastern Pennsylvania mainly from the early morning through the late afternoon on the 3rd. Snowfall varied greatly across the area but in general a widespread 6.0 inches of snow was recorded, with the greatest amounts falling in the Lehigh and Delaware Valleys. The heavy, wet snow caused power outages and laid the foundation for the ice storm which followed in two days in the Philadelphia area. ||The snow caused slippery traveling conditions on untreated and less traveled roadways.  In Berks County and the Philadelphia Metropolitan Area, precipitation started as rain around Midnight EST on the 3rd and changed over to snow between 3 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST that morning. Precipitation fell as snow throughout the event in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley and reached the area between 3 a.m. and 5 a.m. EST on the 3rd.  The heaviest snow fell during the daytime hours of the morning and ended in all areas between 4 p.m. and 6 p.m. EST that day.||Many municipalities declared snow emergencies. Approximately 60,000 PECO Energy customers lost power in the Philadelphia Metropolitan Area. Power was restored to all by the 4th. Many schools were closed and other events were postponed or cancelled. Speed reductions were in place on all of the major interstates. The snow caused 400 flight cancellations at the Philadelphia International Airport and on average four hour flight delays.  In the Lehigh Valley, LANTA suspended all commuter bus service. Tractor-trailers became stuck on hills. Airport Road was closed due a truck accident. The heavy snow caused awning collapses in Wilson Borough. The heavy snow caused suspension of trash and recycling collections and caused salt supplies to run low. ||Representative snowfall amounts included 9.3 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport in Allentown (Lehigh County), 9.2 inches in Trappe (Montgomery County), 9.0 inches in Lower Heidelberg Township (Berks County) and Hilltown Township (Bucks County), 8.7 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 8.5 inches in East Coventry Township (Chester County), Jenkintown (Montgomery County) and New Tripoli (Lehigh County), 8.2 inches in Wernersville (Berks County) and Spring City (Chester County), 7.8 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe  County), 7.3 inches in Bowmanstown (Carbon County), 7.0 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 6.4 inches in Chapman (Carbon County), 6.2 inches in Franklinville (Philadelphia County), 5.2 inches in Somerton (Philadelphia County), 4.5 inches in Drexel Hill and Norwood (both Delaware County),  3.8 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County) and 3.5 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.||The heavy snow was caused by a low pressure system that formed on a cold front in eastern Texas on the morning of the 2nd. The low moved northeast and reach Mississippi on the evening of the 2nd, western North Carolina on the morning of the 3rd, the Delmarva coastal waters during the afternoon on the 3rd and then moved quickly offshore. As the precipitation intensity increased and colder air moved into the state, the rain changed to snow.

A major winter storm brought heavy snow, sleet and freezing rain to the Poconos, a heavy wintry mixture to the Lehigh Valley and Berks County and a crippling ice storm to Philadelphia and in particular its western suburbs. Snowfall reached as high as ten inches in the Poconos and ice accumulations were as high as half an inch. The combination of new ice and snow still on the trees from the just concluded winter storm caused a record breaking 715,000 customer power outages in the PECO Energy service area in and around Philadelphia (849.000 throughout Pennsylvania). This was the worst power outage event ever for the winter season, represented about 45 percent of all its customers and the second worst behind Sandy for the utility. It took about one week to have power fully restored. The weight of the snow, then sleet and freezing rain on limbs all collaborated to cause more tree damage then would have occurred if trees were bare at the start of this event.  Governor Tom Corbett declared a State of Emergency. President Barack Obama also declared a state of emergency in Pennsylvania. Many counties and municipalities also declared snow emergencies or states of emergency. Government offices, schools and businesses were closed. Some schools as well as universities, such as Villanova and West Chester, remained closed for the rest of the week. More than a dozen hospitals in the Philadelphia area ran on generators. Red Cross shelters and warming centers were opened. Some boil water advisories were also in effect because water treatment plants lost power.||In Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, precipitation started as sleet (and a bit of snow) during the late evening on the 4th. Precipitation changed over to freezing rain within a couple of hours of the start. Precipitation then changed to plain rain from south to north between 6 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 5th. The rain ended that afternoon. In Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, precipitation started as snow right around Midnight EST on the 5th. The snow fell heavy at times before sunrise in the Poconos. In the Lehigh Valley and Berks County, precipitation changed to sleet between 1 a.m. EST and 2 a.m. EST on the 5th and to freezing rain between 2 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the 5th. Precipitation changed to plain rain between 8 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST that morning and ended during that afternoon. In the Poconos, precipitation changed to sleet around 7 a.m. EST on the 5th, then freezing rain around 9 a.m. EST that morning. Some valley locations changed to plain rain, but over the higher terrain freezing rain persisted until precipitation ended during the middle of the afternoon.  A very light wintry mix then occurred in the Poconos during the early evening of the 5th. |  |The greatest impact from the winter and ice storm occurred in the Philadelphia suburbs as power was lost for up to one week. Repair crews from throughout North America assisted. About 5,100 workers were involved in the restoration process. Sections of the Schuylkill Expressway and U.S. Routes 202 and 322 were closed because of downed trees. Downed trees also littered the Pennsylvania Turnpike, the Pennsylvania Turnpike Northeast Extension and U.S. Routes 30 and 1. There were posted speed reductions on the Pennsylvania Turnpike and an empty tractor-trailer ban. Commuter rail and bus service was also affected. Amtrak rail service between Philadelphia and Harrisburg was suspended until the 8th. SEPTA suspended four regional rail lines until the 6th. Others ran with delays of at least 30 minutes. ||In Chester County, whole municipalities lost power as did 87 percent of the county overall. There were about 400 tree blocked roadways in the county.  Hardest hit municipalities included Highland, Charlestown, East Nantmeal, Parkesburg and West Vincent. West Chester University was used as a shelter. About 265 Verizon telephone customers lost service in West Fallowfield Township. In Montgomery County, there were 340 calls for electrical fires, 1,001 road obstructions, 153 crashes, and more than 4,000 calls for police assistance. Fifty major roadways were blocked by fallen trees. In Philadelphia, there were 139 cancelled flights at the Philadelphia International Airport on the 5th. A fallen tree limb injured one man. In Bucks County, over 90 percent of Doylestown, New Hope and Yardley lost power. Eighty state maintained highways were blocked by fallen trees. Power outages in Delaware County were the worst along its border with Chester County. Throughout the Philadelphia area, dozens of people suffered carbon monoxide poisoning because of improper ventilation of generators. ||In the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, power outages were less as more precipitation fell as snow and sleet. The total number of customers without power were around 1,000. In Lynn Township (Lehigh County), a collision between a pick-up truck and van resulted in the death of a 52-year-old man and a 58-year-old woman and one additional injury. In Northampton County,  Pennsylvania State Route 33 Northbound was closed near Freemansburg Avenue in Bethlehem Township due to a crash that involved a tractor trailer and a bus.||The number of PECO Energy customer outages peaked at 715,000 on the 5th. It dropped to 499,000 on the morning of the 6th, 440,000 on the evening of the 6th, 275,000 on the afternoon of the 7th, 182,000 on the evening of the 7th, 160,000 on the morning of the 8th, 82,000 on the evening of the 8th, 23,000 on the afternoon of the 9th and 5,600 on the 10th. This ice storm also made it the worst winter for outages in the utility's history: 1,097,900 outages. A major winter storm brought heavy snow, sleet and freezing rain to the Poconos, a heavy wintry mixture to the Lehigh Valley and Berks County and a crippling ice storm to Philadelphia and in particular its western suburbs. Snowfall reached as high as ten inches in the Poconos and ice accumulations were as high as half an inch. The combination of new ice and snow still on the trees from the just concluded winter storm caused a record breaking 715,000 customer power outages in the PECO Energy service area in and around Philadelphia (849.000 throughout Pennsylvania). This was the worst power outage event ever for the winter season, represented about 45 percent of all its customers and the second worst behind Sandy for the utility. It took about one week to have power fully restored. The weight of the snow, then sleet and freezing rain on limbs all collaborated to cause more tree damage then would have occurred if trees were bare at the start of this event.  Governor Tom Corbett declared a State of Emergency. President Barack Obama also declared a state of emergency in Pennsylvania. Many counties and municipalities also declared snow emergencies or states of emergency. Government offices, schools and businesses were closed. Some schools as well as universities, such as Villanova and West Chester, remained closed for the rest of the week. More than a dozen hospitals in the Philadelphia area ran on generators. Red Cross shelters and warming centers were opened. Some boil water advisories were also in effect because water treatment plants lost power.||In Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, precipitation started as sleet (and a bit of snow) during the late evening on the 4th. Precipitation changed over to freezing rain within a couple of hours of the start. Precipitation then changed to plain rain from south to north between 6 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 5th. The rain ended that afternoon. In Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, precipitation started as snow right around Midnight EST on the 5th. The snow fell heavy at times before sunrise in the Poconos. In the Lehigh Valley and Berks County, precipitation changed to sleet between 1 a.m. EST and 2 a.m. EST on the 5th and to freezing rain between 2 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the 5th. Precipitation changed to plain rain between 8 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST that morning and ended during that afternoon. In the Poconos, precipitation changed to sleet around 7 a.m. EST on the 5th, then freezing rain around 9 a.m. EST that morning. Some valley locations changed to plain rain, but over the higher terrain freezing rain persisted until precipitation ended during the middle of the afternoon.  A very light wintry mix then occurred in the Poconos during the early evening of the 5th. |  |The greatest impact from the winter and ice storm occurred in the Philadelphia suburbs as power was lost for up to one week. Repair crews from throughout North America assisted. About 5,100 workers were involved in the restoration process. Downed trees also littered the Pennsylvania Turnpike, the Pennsylvania Turnpike Northeast Extension and U.S. Routes 30 and 1. There were posted speed reductions on the Pennsylvania Turnpike and an empty tractor-trailer ban. Commuter rail and bus service was also affected. Snowfall reached as high as ten inches in the Poconos and ice accumulations were as high as half an inch. The combination of new ice and snow still on the trees from the just concluded winter storm caused a record breaking 715,000 customer power outages in the PECO Energy service area in and around Philadelphia (849.000 throughout Pennsylvania). This was the worst power outage event ever for the winter season, represented about 45 percent of all its customers and the second worst behind Sandy for the utility. It took about one week to have power fully restored. The weight of the snow, then sleet and freezing rain on limbs all collaborated to cause more tree damage then would have occurred if trees were bare at the start of this event.  Governor Tom Corbett declared a State of Emergency. President Barack Obama also declared a state of emergency in Pennsylvania. Many counties and municipalities also declared snow emergencies or states of emergency. Government offices, schools and businesses were closed. Some schools as well as universities, such as Villanova and West Chester, remained closed for the rest of the week. More than a dozen hospitals in the Philadelphia area ran on generators. Red Cross shelters and warming centers were opened. Some boil water advisories were also in effect because water treatment plants lost power.||In Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, precipitation started as sleet (and a bit of snow) during the late evening on the 4th. Precipitation changed over to freezing rain within a couple of hours of the start. Precipitation then changed to plain rain from south to north between 6 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 5th. The rain ended that afternoon. In Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, precipitation started as snow right around Midnight EST on the 5th. The snow fell heavy at times before sunrise in the Poconos. In the Lehigh Valley and Berks County, precipitation changed to sleet between 1 a.m. EST and 2 a.m. EST on the 5th and to freezing rain between 2 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the 5th. Precipitation changed to plain rain between 8 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST that morning and ended during that afternoon. In the Poconos, precipitation changed to sleet around 7 a.m. EST on the 5th, then freezing rain around 9 a.m. EST that morning. Some valley locations changed to plain rain, but over the higher terrain freezing rain persisted until precipitation ended during the middle of the afternoon.  A very light wintry mix then occurred in the Poconos during the early evening of the 5th. |  |The greatest impact from the winter and ice storm occurred in the Philadelphia suburbs as power was lost for up to one week. Repair crews from throughout North America assisted. About 5,100 workers were involved in the restoration process. Sections of the Schuylkill Expressway and U.S. Routes 202 and 322 were closed because of downed trees. Downed trees also littered the Pennsylvania Turnpike, the Pennsylvania Turnpike Northeast Extension and U.S. Routes 30 and 1. There were posted speed reductions on the Pennsylvania Turnpike and an empty tractor-trailer ban. Commuter rail and bus service was also affected. Amtrak rail service between Philadelphia and Harrisburg was suspended until the 8th. SEPTA suspended four regional rail lines until the 6th. Others ran with delays of at least 30 minutes. ||In Chester County, whole municipalities lost power as did 87 percent of the county overall. There were about 400 tree blocked roadways in the county.  Hardest hit municipalities included Highland, Charlestown, East Nantmeal, Parkesburg and West Vincent. West Chester University was used as a shelter. About 265 Verizon telephone customers lost service in West Fallowfield Township. In Montgomery County, there were 340 calls for electrical fires, 1,001 road obstructions, 153 crashes, and more than 4,000 calls for police assistance. Fifty major roadways were blocked by fallen trees. In Philadelphia, there were 139 cancelled flights at the Philadelphia International Airport on the 5th. A fallen tree limb injured one man. In Bucks County, over 90 percent of Doylestown, New Hope and Yardley lost power. Eighty state maintained highways were blocked by fallen trees. Power outages in Delaware County were the worst along its border with Chester County. Throughout the Philadelphia area, dozens of people suffered carbon monoxide poisoning because of improper ventilation of generators. ||In the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, power outages were less as more precipitation fell as snow and sleet. The total number of customers without power were around 1,000. In Lynn Township (Lehigh County), a collision between a pick-up truck and van resulted in the death of a 52-year-old man and a 58-year-old woman and one additional injury. In Northampton County,  Pennsylvania State Route 33 Northbound was closed near Freemansburg Avenue in Bethlehem Township due to a crash that involved a tractor trailer and a bus.||The number of PECO Energy customer outages peaked at 715,000 on the 5th. It dropped to 499,000 on the morning of the 6th, 440,000 on the evening of the 6th, 275,000 on the afternoon of the 7th, 182,000 on the evening of the 7th, 160,000 on the morning of the 8th, 82,000 on the evening of the 8th, 23,000 on the afternoon of the 9th and 5,600 on the 10th. This ice storm also made it the worst winter for outages in the utility's history: 1,097,900 outages. The previous winter season record occurred in 1993-1994 with 1,011,377 outages. ||Representative ice accumulations included 0.50 inches in West Chester (Chester County) and Wayne (Delaware County), 0.40 inches in Northeast Philadelphia, Allentown (Lehigh County), Blandon (Berks County) and Royersford (Montgomery County), 0.30 inches in Upper Darby (Delaware County), Bushkill Township (Northampton County) and Eaglesville (Montgomery County), 0.25 inches in Furlong (Bucks County) and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County) and 0.20 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and Forks Township (Northampton County). ||Representative snowfall and sleet accumulations included 10.0 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County), 9.5 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 9.0 inches in Tobyhanna and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 7.0 inches in Kidder Township (Carbon County) and Bushkill Township (Northampton County), 6.5 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 6.2 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 6.0 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 4.0 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County) and Forks Township (Northampton County), 3.7 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 3.1 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County), 3.0 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 2.4 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 2.0 inches in Kutztown (Berks County), 1.2 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County) and 0.8 inches in Chester Springs (Chester County).  ||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that formed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 4th and a surface high pressure system that passed over the region and with the assistance of the snow cover locked in cold air near the surface. This low pressure system moved northeast and reached Mississippi during the early evening of the 4th and the Tennessee River Valley around Midnight EST on the 5th. About the same time, a secondary low pressure system was forming on the primary low's warm front off the North Carolina coast. Both low pressure systems in tandem proceeded to move northeast and at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th, the primary low pressure system was over southeast Ohio with the secondary low pressure system off the Delaware coast. As is typically the case, the primary low pressure system weakened and reached western Pennsylvania during the early afternoon on the 5th while the stronger secondary low pressure system was just off of Long Beach Island on the New Jersey coast. This secondary low pressure system intensified as it rapidly moved northeast and was southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts at 4 p.m. EST on the 5th. What was left of the primary low pressure system trundled across Eastern Pennsylvania and Northern New Jersey during the late afternoon and early evening of the 5th.

Snow in Eastern Pennsylvania occurred during the first half of the day on the 18th caused slippery and hazardous traveling conditions on untreated roadways.  Snowfall averaged 1.5 to 3.5 inches. Snow overspread eastern Pennsylvania from 130 a.m. EST through 330 a.m. EST from the southwest to the Poconos. In Berks County and Philadelphia and its suburbs, the snow ended between 530 a.m. EST and 730 a.m. EST. In the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, a second shot of snow occurred during the mid and late morning on the 18th and prolonged the event. In fact, some widely scattered snow showers even occurred in the Poconos during the afternoon of the 18th.||The snow caused school delayed openings. The snow also caused the rerouting of about 25 SEPTA regional bus routes in the Philadelphia area. In Delaware County, a truck accident caused the closure of U.S. Route 322 in Concord Township.  The continued loading of snow on structures caused additional roof collapses. In Montgomery County in Hatboro a man was injured when the home's carport collapsed on him. In Lehigh County, in Fountain Hill, the porch roofs of three attached homes collapsed. The residents were uninjured, but trapped inside. Eight homes around the collapse were evacuated. In Chester County, in Unionville, the roof of the unoccupied banquet hall at the Stone Barn collapsed.  Even if roofs did not collapse, the freeze/thaw cycle was causing ice dams and roof leaks. In addition, municipal budgets continued to get strained because of the wintry weather and salt shortages persisted.| |Representative snowfall totals included 3.5 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County), 3.2 inches in Conshohocken (Montgomery County), 3.0 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 2.7 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County) and Fox Chase (Philadelphia County), 2.5 inches in Perkasie (Bucks County), Royersford (Montgomery County) and Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 2.3 inches in East Coventry Township (Chester County) and Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 2.2 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County) and Forks Township (Northampton County), 2.1 inches in Spring City (Chester County), 2.0 inches in Norwood (Delaware County) and Glendon (Northampton County), 1.8 inches in Morgantown (Berks County) and Downingtown (Chester County), 1.6 inches in Bushkill Township (Northampton County), 1.5 inches in New Tripoli (Lehigh County) and 1.3 inches at Pocono Summit (Monroe County), Lehighton (Carbon County) and the Lehigh Valley International Airport.||The snow was caused by an occluded front that moved into Western Pennsylvania during the early morning of the 18th and cleared the eastern part of the state late in the morning on the 18th.

For the second morning in a row, wintry weather affected most of Eastern Pennsylvania (except for Philadelphia and Delaware Counties) on the 19th. The air mass aloft was warmer and the wintry precipitation was more of a mixture of sleet and freezing rain with some snow occurring over the higher terrain of the Poconos. In addition, thunderstorms with freezing rain occurred in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs. Snowfall averaged around two inches in the higher terrain of the Poconos. Ice accumulations averaged one to two tenths of an inch. Untreated roadways and sidewalks became slippery and hazardous, especially in Chester County.||Precipitation started briefly as sleet between 8 a.m. EST and 930 a.m. EST on the morning of the 19th. The precipitation then changed to freezing rain. In the Poconos. as the precipitation rate intensity increased, the freezing rain changed to snow over the higher terrain. The snow changed back to sleet just before ending around Noon EST.  Elsewhere, as warmer air reached the surface, the freezing rain changed over to plain rain around 11 a.m. EST. The rain also ended around Noon EST. ||In Chester County, the icing conditions contributed to a fourteen vehicle pile-up, including a state police cruiser, in West Bradford Township on Shadyside Road. Several injuries occurred. An additional multi-vehicular accident in Coatesville on Pennsylvania State Route 82 closed the roadway for several hours.  In Lehigh County, the icing conditions forced the closure of Pennsylvania State Route 873 in both directions in Slatington. Several dozen flights in and out of Philadelphia International Airport were cancelled.||Representative snowfall included 2.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County) and Lake Harmony (Carbon County), 1.3 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County) and 0.6 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County). Ice accumulations included about two tenths of an inch in Allentown (Lehigh County) and Reading (Berks County), one tenth of an inch in Forks Township (Northampton County) and Pottstown (Montgomery County) and one-sixteenth of an inch in Bushkill Township (Northampton County).  ||The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by a southwest flow of milder and moister air accompanying a warm front that was located in the Ohio Valley on the morning of the 19th. The warm front reached Eastern Pennsylvania during the early afternoon of the 19th. By doing so, it brought above freezing temperatures into the area and also its proximity ended the necessary lift needed to generate precipitation.

A major winter storm affected all of Eastern Pennsylvania with heavy snow and sleet and even some freezing rain. The heaviest snow of the season occurred during this storm as 12 to 20 inches of snow fell across most of Eastern Pennsylvania. Ice accumulations were generally less than one tenth of an inch. The heavy snow also caused structural failures even after it ended and was a contributing factor to a major multi-vehicule accident in the Philadelphia suburbs on the morning of the 14th.  During this event, 14 counties and 154 municipalities have declared Disaster Declaration Emergencies. A state of emergency remained in effect for Pennsylvania. ||Snow began in Eastern Pennsylvania and spread from the south to the north between 9 p.m. EST and Midnight EST on the 12th.  Two bands of heavy snow, in which snowfall rates averaged two to four inches per hour, moved through the state on the 13th. The first one mainly affected the local Philadelphia area between 5 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST.  The second one moved north through the entire eastern half of Pennsylvania between 730 a.m. EST and 2 p.m. EST on the 13th. After the snow bands moved through, precipitation changed to freezing rain and then rain in the immediate Philadelphia area during the late morning of the 13th, the Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the northwest Philadelphia suburbs  around midday on the 13th and during the afternoon of the 13th in the Poconos. Some pockets of freezing rain and sleet persisted in the higher terrain of the Poconos. Scattered thunderstorms moved through during the late afternoon and early evening on the 13th, some with small hail. Then as the low pressure system pulled offshore, precipitation changed back to snow during the evening on the 13th. Some heavy snow occurred again in the Philadelphia western suburbs, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. The last of the snow exited eastern Pennsylvania from west to east between Midnight EST and 4 a.m. EST on the 14th. The snow exited lower Bucks County last.||Schools, malls, businesses, government, state and local offices as well as municipal courts never opened on the 13th. Many schools were also closed on the 14th. There was a trailer ban on the Pennsylvania Turnpike and reduced speeds on all major roadways including the Delaware River crossings. Commuter bus service from the Lehigh Valley to New York City was cancelled. SEPTA Regional Commuter Services detoured bus and trolley routes in and around Philadelphia and suspended bus service for a while during the day on the 13th. Regional Rail lines ran with 60 minute delays. SEPTA then halted all rail and bus service overnight. Over 400 flights were cancelled at the Philadelphia International Airport and all flights were cancelled at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. Because the heavy snow started early in the morning, the number of accidents and calls for assistance in the Philadelphia area was less than usual. In the Lehigh Valley, there were more disabled vehicles on side roads because of the later arrival of the heavy snow bands.  There were also slip and fall accidents and at least one person who lost fingers clearing a snow thrower.  The snow did not cling to tree limbs as well and there were only about 6,000 homes and businesses that lost power. The greatest numbers were in Bucks and Montgomery Counties. One of the hardest hit businesses were florists as the heavy snow wreaked havoc (tens of thousands delayed) on the flower deliveries for Valentine's Day.  After the snow ended, on the morning of the 14th, 30 people were injured and about 100 vehicles were damaged in a chain reaction multi-vehicle (including several tractor-trailers) accident on the Eastbound Pennsylvania Turnpike in Bucks County. A major winter storm affected all of Eastern Pennsylvania with heavy snow and sleet and even some freezing rain. The heaviest snow of the season occurred during this storm as 12 to 20 inches of snow fell across most of Eastern Pennsylvania. Ice accumulations were generally less than one tenth of an inch. The heavy snow also caused structural failures even after it ended and was a contributing factor to a major multi-vehicule accident in the Philadelphia suburbs on the morning of the 14th.  During this event, 14 counties and 154 municipalities have declared Disaster Declaration Emergencies. A state of emergency remained in effect for Pennsylvania. ||Snow began in Eastern Pennsylvania and spread from the south to the north between 9 p.m. EST and Midnight EST on the 12th.  Two bands of heavy snow, in which snowfall rates averaged two to four inches per hour, moved through the state on the 13th. The first one mainly affected the local Philadelphia area between 5 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST.  The second one moved north through the entire eastern half of Pennsylvania between 730 a.m. EST and 2 p.m. EST on the 13th. After the snow bands moved through, precipitation changed to freezing rain and then rain in the immediate Philadelphia area during the late morning of the 13th, the Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the northwest Philadelphia suburbs  around midday on the 13th and during the afternoon of the 13th in the Poconos. Some pockets of freezing rain and sleet persisted in the higher terrain of the Poconos. Scattered thunderstorms moved through during the late afternoon and early evening on the 13th, some with small hail. Then as the low pressure system pulled offshore, precipitation changed back to snow during the evening on the 13th. Some heavy snow occurred again in the Philadelphia western suburbs, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos. The last of the snow exited eastern Pennsylvania from west to east between Midnight EST and 4 a.m. EST on the 14th. The snow exited lower Bucks County last.||Schools, malls, businesses, government, state and local offices as well as municipal courts never opened on the 13th. Many schools were also closed on the 14th. There was a trailer ban on the Pennsylvania Turnpike and reduced speeds on all major roadways including the Delaware River crossings. Commuter bus service from the Lehigh Valley to New York City was cancelled. SEPTA Regional Commuter Services detoured bus and trolley routes in and around Philadelphia and suspended bus service for a while during the day on the 13th. Regional Rail lines ran with 60 minute delays. SEPTA then halted all rail and bus service overnight. Over 400 flights were cancelled at the Philadelphia International Airport and all flights were cancelled at the Lehigh Valley International Airport. Because the heavy snow started early in the morning, the number of accidents and calls for assistance in the Philadelphia area was less than usual. In the Lehigh Valley, there were more disabled vehicles on side roads because of the later arrival of the heavy snow bands.  There were also slip and fall accidents and at least one person who lost fingers clearing a snow thrower. One of the hardest hit businesses were florists as the heavy snow wreaked havoc (tens of thousands delayed) on the flower deliveries for Valentine's Day.  After the snow ended, on the morning of the 14th, 30 people were injured and about 100 vehicles were damaged in a chain reaction multi-vehicle (including several tractor-trailers) accident on the Eastbound Pennsylvania Turnpike in Bucks County.  The combination of an icy roadway, sun glare and excessive speed helped cause the accident. The Turnpike was closed for about 8 hours.
