Precipitation then persisted following a cold frontal passage brought a wintry mix of precipitation to the higher terrain of the Poconos from the evening of the 12th into the morning of the 13th. While overall ice (less than one tenth of an inch) and snow (less than one inch) amounts were light, plunging temperatures from the 40s on the 12th into the single numbers on the 13th caused treacherous driving conditions untreated roadways.  Precipitation started as rain during the afternoon of the 12th. After the cold front passed through the Poconos late that afternoon, temperatures started plunging and precipitation persisted. The rain changed to freezing rain that evening and then to snow before midnight. The steady snow ended early on the 13th, but scattered snow showers and even some snow squalls persisted through the morning. Combined with strong winds, poor visibilities and blowing and drifting of snow also occurred that morning.

A low pressure system exiting the South Carolina coast brought a light accumulating snow to Philadelphia and southeastern Pennsylvania on the 3rd with little if any snow in places farther to the north. While snowfall was relatively light (averaging 1 to 3 inches), temperatures started above freezing as precipitation began as rain and fell steadily below freezing during the day. This made for slippery traveling conditions on untreated roadways.||Precipitation started as sleet in the Philadelphia far west suburbs and then changed quickly to snow during the late evening. In the northern Philadelphia suburbs, precipitation started as snow early on the 3rd. In and around Philadelphia itself, precipitation started as rain during the afternoon of the 2nd, mixed with sleet during the late evening and changed over to snow early on the 3rd. The snow fell moderately at times during the morning commute and ended from north to south between 700 a.m. EST and 900 a.m. EST that morning.   | |Representative snowfall included 3.4 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 3.1 inches in Nottingham (Chester County) and Brookhaven (Delaware County), 2.3 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 2.1 inches in Fox Chase (Philadelphia County), 2.0 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 1.4 inches in Bensalem (Bucks County), 1.3 inches in Parkesburg (Chester County) and 0.8 inches in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County). ||The snow was caused by a series of low pressure systems that formed on the cold front that passed through Delaware on the afternoon of the 2nd. The low pressure systems formed in the Tennessee River Valley. An arctic high pressure system located over the Northern Plains on the evening of the 2nd slowly moved to the southeast and prevented the surface low pressure systems from having much northern movement. The fractionalized low pressure systems took until the morning of the 3rd to merge into one in western South Carolina. From there, the low pressure system moved east and exited the South Carolina coast during the early evening of the 3rd. The track of the low was too far south to bring heavy snow into southeastern Pennsylvania.

From there the low pressure system moved northeast and intensified even more rapidly as it passed just east of Long Island, New York and passed near Boston, Massachusetts as a 976 millibar low pressure system at 4 a.m. EST on the 14th. This low pressure system deepened to 968 millibars at 10 a.m. EST on the 14th while over Maine before finally leveling off. The close pass to the New Jersey coast permitted this low pressure system to bring in above freezing (milder) air into Eastern Pennsylvania, if not at the surface, at least aloft and caused a period of non-snow to occur before ending as snow again as the low pressure system departed.

The arctic air mass following the snow storm that affected the Middle Atlantic States dropped wind chill factors in the Poconos to around 15 degrees below zero during the early morning of the 4th. The lowest hourly wind chill factor at Mount Pocono was 16 degrees below zero while the actual low temperature was one degree above zero.

The lowest hourly wind chill factor at Mount Pocono was 16 degrees below zero while the actual low temperature was one degree above zero.

A low pressure system exiting the South Carolina coast brought a light accumulating snow to Philadelphia and southeastern Pennsylvania on the 3rd with little if any snow in places farther to the north. While snowfall was relatively light (averaging 1 to 3 inches), temperatures started above freezing as precipitation began as rain and fell steadily below freezing during the day. This made for slippery traveling conditions on untreated roadways.||Precipitation started as sleet in the Philadelphia far west suburbs and then changed quickly to snow during the late evening. In the northern Philadelphia suburbs, precipitation started as snow early on the 3rd. In and around Philadelphia itself, precipitation started as rain during the afternoon of the 2nd, mixed with sleet during the late evening and changed over to snow early on the 3rd. The snow fell moderately at times during the morning commute and ended from north to south between 700 a.m. EST and 900 a.m. EST that morning.   | |Representative snowfall included 3.4 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 3.1 inches in Nottingham (Chester County) and Brookhaven (Delaware County), 2.3 inches in Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 2.1 inches in Fox Chase (Philadelphia County), 2.0 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 1.4 inches in Bensalem (Bucks County), 1.3 inches in Parkesburg (Chester County) and 0.8 inches in East Nantmeal Township (Chester County). ||The snow was caused by a series of low pressure systems that formed on the cold front that passed through Delaware on the afternoon of the 2nd. The low pressure systems formed in the Tennessee River Valley. An arctic high pressure system located over the Northern Plains on the evening of the 2nd slowly moved to the southeast and prevented the surface low pressure systems from having much northern movement. The fractionalized low pressure systems took until the morning of the 3rd to merge into one in western South Carolina. From there, the low pressure system moved east and exited the South Carolina coast during the early evening of the 3rd. The track of the low was too far south to bring heavy snow into southeastern Pennsylvania.

As a low pressure system slowly moved off the New Jersey coast, a wintry mix of precipitation occurred over the Poconos from the evening of the 30th into the early morning of the 31st. The steady rain changed to freezing rain and then sleet during the evening of the 29th. Precipitation changed over to all snow before it ended on the 31st. The snow and sleet accumulations were less than an inch. But, coupled with a coating of ice that preceded it and temperatures that stayed below freezing until later in the morning on the 31st untreated roadways and walkways were hazardous. A low pressure system just off the New Jersey coast on the 30th slowly eased its way east. This brought in colder air that changed the rain to a wintry mix. The low pressure system went far enough to the east that the precipitation shield moved east of the Poconos.

The strong pressure gradient (difference) between an intensifying strong low pressure system and a high pressure system in the Ohio Valley caused strong northwest winds to occur across Pennsylvania from the evening of the 12th following a cold frontal passage through the daytime hours on the 13th. Peak wind gusts averaged around  50 mph and the highest winds occurred overnight on the 12th. The prolonged period of strong winds caused weak tree limbs, trees and power lines to be knocked down and caused widely scattered power outages. Downed trees and/or power lines blocked roadways in Delaware (Concordville and Lansdowne), Chester (Willistown), Montgomery (Ambler), Lehigh (Allentown) and Philadelphia Counties. ||Peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Newtown (Delaware County), 54 mph in Port Richmond (Philadelphia), 53 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), Coatesville (Chester County) and the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 49 mph in Reading and Lenhartsville (Berks County) and Strickersville and Kelton (Chester County), 48 mph at the Lehigh Valley and Philadelphia International Airports, 45 mph in Perkasie and Doylestown (Bucks County) and 44 mph in Pottstown (Montgomery County). ||The low pressure system responsible for the strong winds emerged from the Rockies on the evening of the 10th. It reached central Missouri on the evening of the 11th and western Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 12th. It already was an intense 984 millibar low pressure system. The northeast moving 982 millibar low pressure system reached central New Jersey at 8 p.m. EDT on the 12th as its cold front just about cleared the Eastern Shore. The 981 millibar low pressure system was near Boston, Massachusetts at 11 p.m. EDT on the 12th and reached Nova Scotia as a 976 millibar low pressure system at 5 a.m. EDT on the 13th. It continued to move northeast through the Canadian Maritimes during the day on the 13th and very slowly weakened. By evening on the 13th, the high pressure system was close enough to the Eastern Shore that the pressure gradient (difference) weakened and the strong winds stopped.

Eastern Pennsylvania was at the far northwestern shield of an intense oceanic low pressure system as only light snow fell from around midday on the 25th until around Midnight EDT on the 26th. Snowfall was less than an inch. The greatest impact by the oceanic low pressure system were the strong, gusty north to northwest winds that occurred during the daytime hours on the 26th. Peak wind gusts averaged 40 to 45 mph and downed weak tree limbs.  Peak wind gusts included 47 mph at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 45 mph in Perkasie (Bucks County) and 44 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Mount Pocono (Monroe County). ||The strong northwest winds were caused by the rapid intensification as well as the overall intensity of the offshore low pressure system.  It made its closest pass to Pennsylvania early on the 26th.  At 8 a.m. EDT on the 26th, the oceanic storm's surface pressure dropped to 964 millibars (it would bottom at 955 millibars later in the day), a drop of 43 millibars in a 24 hour period.

A large area of low pressure west of the Great Lakes combined with high pressure over New England to produce strong southeast winds on the 29th. This allowed sustained winds of over 25 MPH with down-slope from the ridges of western Pennsylvania allowing for winds to gust over 50 MPH at times. The highest wind speed recorded was 63 MPH at the Arnold Palmer Regional Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported vinyl siding and some shingles off a home near Hopwood.

Downed trees and/or power lines blocked roadways in Delaware (Concordville and Lansdowne), Chester (Willistown), Montgomery (Ambler), Lehigh (Allentown) and Philadelphia Counties. ||Peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Newtown (Delaware County), 54 mph in Port Richmond (Philadelphia), 53 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), Coatesville (Chester County) and the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 49 mph in Reading and Lenhartsville (Berks County) and Strickersville and Kelton (Chester County), 48 mph at the Lehigh Valley and Philadelphia International Airports, 45 mph in Perkasie and Doylestown (Bucks County) and 44 mph in Pottstown (Montgomery County). ||The low pressure system responsible for the strong winds emerged from the Rockies on the evening of the 10th. It reached central Missouri on the evening of the 11th and western Pennsylvania on the afternoon of the 12th. It already was an intense 984 millibar low pressure system. The northeast moving 982 millibar low pressure system reached central New Jersey at 8 p.m. EDT on the 12th as its cold front just about cleared the Eastern Shore. The 981 millibar low pressure system was near Boston, Massachusetts at 11 p.m. EDT on the 12th and reached Nova Scotia as a 976 millibar low pressure system at 5 a.m. EDT on the 13th. It continued to move northeast through the Canadian Maritimes during the day on the 13th and very slowly weakened. By evening on the 13th, the high pressure system was close enough to the Eastern Shore that the pressure gradient (difference) weakened and the strong winds stopped.

A low pressure system that traversed across the southern United States brought snow into southeastern Pennsylvania on the 17th. Snowfall in Philadelphia and its nearby suburbs averaged 1 to 4 inches. Little if any snow fell farther to the north in Pennsylvania. The snow fell during the morning commute and coupled with below freezing temperatures made for hazardous travel on untreated and less traveled roadways.  ||The snow spread into Southeastern Pennsylvania between Midnight EDT and 4 a.m. EDT on the 17th. Nearly as soon as it started in Philadelphia's far northwestern suburbs, it ended. However, in and around the local Philadelphia area the snow continued to fall into the first half of the afternoon on the 17th. It fell at its heaviest just before sunrise.  ||Representative snowfall included 4.7 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport, 4.3 inches in Brookhaven (Delaware), 3.3 inches in Nottingham (Chester County) and Lansdowne (Delaware County), 3.0 inches in Wynnewood (Montgomery County), 2.8 inches in Kennett Square (Chester County), 2.6 inches in Wayne (Delaware County), 2.4 inches in West Chester (Chester County), 2.3 inches in Exton (Chester County), 2.1 inches in Mount Airy (Philadelphia County), 2.0 inches in Marshallton (Chester County) and Fort Washington (Montgomery County), 1.8 inches in Ambler (Montgomery County), 1.2 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County) and Somerton (Philadelphia County) and 1.0 inch in Harleysville (Montgomery County).||The snow was caused by  a pair of low pressure systems that moved across the southern part of the United States.  On the morning the 16th the primary low pressure system was located over Arkansas. It moved east and at 8 p.m. EDT was located over northern Alabama. Very early on the 17th, a secondary low pressure system started forming near Wilmington, North Carolina as the primary low pressure system reached Atlanta, Georgia. While the secondary low pressure system moved offshore during the day on the 17th, the original (primary) low pressure system was slowly suppressed south as a high pressure system built into New York State. It was the combination of the southern storm track and dry air from this high pressure system that prevented the snow from spreading farther to the north in Pennsylvania.||March 17th marked the fourteenth and last time that an inch or more of snow fell in Philadelphia for the snowfall season on one calendar day. This tied for second place behind only the 1917-18 season which had seventeen calendar days of an inch or more of snow. The 2013-2014 seasonal total of 68.0 inches in Philadelphia was the second snowiest season on record, only surpassed by the 78.7 inches of snow that fell during the 2009-10 season. Eastern Pennsylvania was at the far northwestern shield of an intense oceanic low pressure system as only light snow fell from around midday on the 25th until around Midnight EDT on the 26th. Snowfall was less than an inch. The greatest impact by the oceanic low pressure system were the strong, gusty north to northwest winds that occurred during the daytime hours on the 26th. Peak wind gusts averaged 40 to 45 mph and downed weak tree limbs.  Peak wind gusts included 47 mph at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 45 mph in Perkasie (Bucks County) and 44 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Mount Pocono (Monroe County). ||The strong northwest winds were caused by the rapid intensification as well as the overall intensity of the offshore low pressure system.  It made its closest pass to Pennsylvania early on the 26th.  At 8 a.m. EDT on the 26th, the oceanic storm's surface pressure dropped to 964 millibars (it would bottom at 955 millibars later in the day), a drop of 43 millibars in a 24 hour period.

A very moist southerly flow originating from the Gulf of Mexico combined with a slow-moving low pressure system over the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes produced heavy rainfall (3-5+ inches) across southeastern Pennsylvania over a two-day period. The heavy rain lead to considerable flooding of roadways and small streams across portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley. Minor flood stages were exceeded on the Conestoga River at Lancaster and Swatara Creek at Hershey early on May 1, 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains across southeastern Pennsylvania flooded more than 20 roads in Adams County. Several creeks, including Alloway and Conewago, overflowed their banks and inundated surrounding areas. Most low-lying land and roads throughout the county experienced minor flooding. According to Adams County Emergency Management, almost all of the road closures were in the eastern half of the county. Most of the closed roads were roads that usually have issues with flooding during storms, Eline said, adding that no water rescues were reported. The following townships experienced road closures: Oxford, Hamilton, Straban, Mount Pleasant, Reading, Cumberland, Mount Joy, Conewago, and Hamiltonban.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trof combined with an unseasonably warm air mass to develop a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon of the 27th. While the thunderstorms started to weaken as they approached Philadelphia and the Delaware River, their gust fronts still produced damaging winds. About 20,000 homes and businesses lost power, with the greatest impact in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and wires across a roadway in Fort Washington (Upper Dublin Township). A wind gust of 50 mph were measured in the Maple Glen section of the township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains across southeastern Pennsylvania flooded several roads in York County. Several small creeks and streams, including Codorus and Little Conewago, overflowed their banks and inundated surrounding areas. Most low-lying land and roads throughout the county experienced minor flooding. Southern York County School District dismissed students early prior to the onset of the worst flooding. The following townships experienced road closures: York, Spring Garden, West Manchester, North Codorus, Hallam, Hellam, Dover, Springfield, Paradise, Manchester, Seven Valleys, and Newberry.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains across southeastern Pennsylvania flooded and closed roads in Dauphin County. A few small creeks and streams overflowed their banks and inundated surrounding areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A semi-organized band of convection extending southward from the Buffalo, NY vicinity to near Pittsburgh, PA and Charleston, WV moved eastward during the late afternoon and evening hours. The band evolved into a well-defined linear feature with enhanced bowing segments, increasing damaging wind potential as it moved into a moderately unstable air mass located across central and eastern PA. Widespread damaging straight-line winds with embedded microburst damage accompanied the line as it crossed the Mid-State, knocking down numerous trees and utility wires. The damaging winds also caused localized structural damage and thousands of power outages. A few discrete cells ahead of and embedded in the main convective line tapped into strong low-level shear/helicity, which proved to be a favorable environment for tornadogenesis. Following official NWS storm surveys, an EF2 tornado was confirmed near Dushore in Sullivan County along with a 90-100mph microburst in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down numerous trees across the western part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped develop a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through the southern halves of Berks County and the Philadelphia metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm dropped penny size hail in West Chester. The same line of thunderstorms elsewhere in Chester County generated a wind gust of 54 mph in Elverson as well as dropped smaller hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that moved up the eastern seaboard developed an intense narrow band of snow which was centered over the northern tier of Pennsylvania in Susquehanna County. This snow band produced tremendous snowfall rates of up to 3 inches per hour. Storm total snowfalls in a narrow 15 mile band ranged from 6-11 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts in a narrow strip across the central sections of Susquehanna county ranged from 6-11 inches. The highest snowfall total of 11 inches fell five miles west of Montrose.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped develop a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through the southern halves of Berks County and the Philadelphia metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees along westbound U.S. Route 422 in Exeter Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in West Philadelphia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm also knocked down a couple of trees in the Roxborough section of Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and isolated thunderstorms accompanying a cold front moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 15th. Isolated wind damage occurred in Philadelphia and Montgomery Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees near the Abington Memorial Hospital in Abington.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm also knocked down a couple of trees in Northeast Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southerly flow originating from the Gulf of Mexico combined with a slow-moving low pressure system over the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes produced heavy rainfall (3-5+ inches) across southeastern Pennsylvania over a two-day period. The heavy rain lead to considerable flooding of roadways and small streams across portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley. Minor flood stages were exceeded on the Conestoga River at Lancaster and Swatara Creek at Hershey early on May 1, 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains across southeastern Pennsylvania resulted in flooding and road closures across Lancaster County. Several small creeks and streams overflowed their banks and inundated surrounding areas. The Conestoga River crested just above flood stage (11.63 feet on May 1) and caused minor flooding in low-lying areas. In Sadsbury Township, fire fighters rescued a man from his car on Noble Road near Creek Road around 6 p.m. Water was about 18 inches deep. Noble Road was closed from Creek Road to Lower Valley Road because of high water. Several cars were abandoned on nearby Brick Mill Road. The portion of Sadsbury Township that borders Chester County was hit really hard according to local emergency management officials.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southerly flow originating from the Gulf of Mexico combined with a slow-moving low pressure system over the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes produced heavy rainfall (3-5+ inches) across southeastern Pennsylvania over a two-day period. The heavy rain lead to considerable flooding of roadways and small streams across portions of the Lower Susquehanna Valley. Minor flood stages were exceeded on the Conestoga River at Lancaster and Swatara Creek at Hershey early on May 1, 2014.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains across southeastern Pennsylvania flooded and closed roads in Lebanon County. A few small creeks and streams overflowed their banks and inundated surrounding areas. The Swatara Creek at Hershey crested just over flood stage at 7.08 feet early on May 1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front with waves of low pressure along it brought heavy rain to Southeast Pennsylvania on the 16th. This caused poor drainage flooding as well as some minor flooding of creeks in and around Philadelphia. Rain began during the early morning of the 16th and fell heavy at times throughout the event. It ended during the late afternoon with event totals of two to four inches, highest in Montgomery County. The combination of the heavy rain runoff, higher astronomical tides associated with the recent full moon and an up the Delaware Bay flow on the 16th caused minor tidal flooding along the Delaware River and some of its tidal tributaries with the overnight high tide cycle on the 16th. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 8.2 feet above mean lower low water, the threshold for the start of minor tidal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth was above its 5 foot flood stage from 321 p.m. EDT through 1246 a.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 5.82 feet at 845 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 2.11 inches in Wyndmoor and 1.57 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front with waves of low pressure along it brought heavy rain to Southeast Pennsylvania on the 16th. This caused poor drainage flooding as well as some minor flooding of creeks in and around Philadelphia. Rain began during the early morning of the 16th and fell heavy at times throughout the event. It ended during the late afternoon with event totals of two to four inches, highest in Montgomery County. The combination of the heavy rain runoff, higher astronomical tides associated with the recent full moon and an up the Delaware Bay flow on the 16th caused minor tidal flooding along the Delaware River and some of its tidal tributaries with the overnight high tide cycle on the 16th. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 8.2 feet above mean lower low water, the threshold for the start of minor tidal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek at Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 425 p.m. EDT through 540 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 7.11 feet at 5 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 2.21 inches in Valley Forge, 2.13 inches in West Chester, 1.99 inches in Northbrook, 1.94 inches in Nottimngham, 1.88 inches in Kennett Square, 1.70 inches in Spring City, 1.47 inches in Glenmoore and 1.32 inches in Downingtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of unseasonably cold air behind a departing cold front changed the rain to snow before it ended across most of Eastern Pennsylvania during the evening of the 15th. The duration of the snow was around three hours in the Poconos, around an hour and a half in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and less than one hour in Philadelphia and its suburbs. While it was snowing, temperatures dropped below freezing over the higher terrain of the Poconos, Lehigh Valley and Berks County, but remained above freezing elsewhere. In the former places, untreated roadways did become very slippery as temperatures fell below freezing. Snowfall in the higher terrain averaged 1 to 2 inches and less than an inch elsewhere. Representative snowfall included 2.0 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County) and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 1.5 inches in Mohnton (Berks County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 1.2 inches in Slatington (Lehigh County), 1.0 inch in Danielsville (Northampton County) and  0.3 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

A low pressure system tracked through the Ohio Valley and re-developed off of the eastern seaboard during the morning hours of Wednesday, February 5th. An intense snow band that developed produced as much as one to three inches of snow per hour during the early morning hours. Widespread snow amounts ranged from 7 to 16 inches, with the highest totals occurring across the northern tier of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts averaged around 9 inches across the county. The highest amount of 8.8 inches fell two miles west of Clarks Summit.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 10-16 inches across the county. The highest amount of 16 inches fell in Warren Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over the northern Virginia and Maryland Piedmont expanded northward into the ridge-valley region in south-central Pennsylvania during the evening. These storms were located within a conveyor of strong/moist southerly deep-layer flow. A convective environment characterized by weak instability, high surface dewpoints/low LCLs and strong low-level shear fostered a brief/weak EF0 tornado near Lock Haven in Clinton County. Isolated/localized wind damage also occurred within a few low-topped bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in State College confirmed an EF0 Tornado near Lock Haven in Clinton County Pennsylvania on May 15, 2014. ||The EF0 tornado touched down near the southern-most point of Race Street just southwest of the William T. Piper Memorial Airport. The weak tornado pealed back a portion of a roof of a warehouse and partially lifted the roof, allowing portions of an exterior wall to collapse outward. The tornado traveled northward along Racy Street, toppling a chimney and lifting a carport and throwing it 25 yards into another house. The tornado then turned to the northeast and crossed Route 15, knocking over several large trees and snapping a number of large limbs. The tornado dissipated near East Water Street between Race street and Clark Barn Road. The tornado was rated an EF0 tornado with maximum winds around 75 mph. There were no injuries or deaths.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southerly flow originating from the Gulf of Mexico combined with a slow-moving low pressure system over the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes produced heavy rainfall (3-5+ inches) across southeastern Pennsylvania over a two-day period. Minor flood stages were exceeded on the Conestoga River at Lancaster and Swatara Creek at Hershey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Conestoga River at Lancaster reached minor flood stage during the predawn hours and crested at 11.63 ft. at 1100 AM EDT. The river fell below flood stage by the early afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist southerly flow originating from the Gulf of Mexico combined with a slow-moving low pressure system over the Upper Midwest and Great Lakes produced heavy rainfall (3-5+ inches) across southeastern Pennsylvania over a two-day period. Minor flood stages were exceeded on the Conestoga River at Lancaster and Swatara Creek at Hershey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Swatara Creek at Hershey reached minor flood stage shortly after daybreak and crested at 7.08 ft. in the early afternoon. The creek fell below flood stage during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped develop a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through the southern halves of Berks County and the Philadelphia metropolitan area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped quarter size hail in Birdsboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A semi-organized band of convection extending southward from the Buffalo, NY vicinity to near Pittsburgh, PA and Charleston, WV moved eastward during the late afternoon and evening hours. The band evolved into a well-defined linear feature with enhanced bowing segments, increasing damaging wind potential as it moved into a moderately unstable air mass located across central and eastern PA. Widespread damaging straight-line winds with embedded microburst damage accompanied the line as it crossed the Mid-State, knocking down numerous trees and utility wires. The damaging winds also caused localized structural damage and thousands of power outages. A few discrete cells ahead of and embedded in the main convective line tapped into strong low-level shear/helicity, which proved to be a favorable environment for tornadogenesis. Following official NWS storm surveys, an EF2 tornado was confirmed near Dushore in Sullivan County along with a 90-100mph microburst in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires at the intersection Jackson Run/Creamary Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surge of unseasonably cold air behind a departing cold front changed the rain to snow before it ended across most of Eastern Pennsylvania during the evening of the 15th. The duration of the snow was around three hours in the Poconos, around an hour and a half in Berks County and the Lehigh Valley and less than one hour in Philadelphia and its suburbs. While it was snowing, temperatures dropped below freezing over the higher terrain of the Poconos, Lehigh Valley and Berks County, but remained above freezing elsewhere. In the former places, untreated roadways did become very slippery as temperatures fell below freezing. Snowfall in the higher terrain averaged 1 to 2 inches and less than an inch elsewhere. Representative snowfall included 2.0 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County) and Tobyhanna (Monroe County), 1.5 inches in Mohnton (Berks County) and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 1.2 inches in Slatington (Lehigh County), 1.0 inch in Danielsville (Northampton County) and  0.3 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over southeastern Canada during the late afternoon on the 17th in association with a cold front and progressive mid/upper trough moving through the Great Lakes region. Storms evolved into a mixed-mode downstream into Pennsylvania and adjacent Northeast states, where the atmosphere was moderately unstable and topped by a belt of strong winds aloft/0-6km shear. Isolated wind damage was reported across north-central PA between 2300 and 0100 EDT on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires near Rockton.

A low pressure system tracked through the Ohio Valley and re-developed off of the eastern seaboard during the morning hours of Wednesday, February 5th. An intense snow band that developed produced as much as one to three inches of snow per hour during the early morning hours. Widespread snow amounts ranged from 7 to 16 inches, with the highest totals occurring across the northern tier of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 11-14 inches across the county. The highest amount of 14 inches fell in Panther.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over the northern Virginia and Maryland Piedmont expanded northward into the ridge-valley region in south-central Pennsylvania during the evening. These storms were located within a conveyor of strong/moist southerly deep-layer flow. A convective environment characterized by weak instability, high surface dewpoints/low LCLs and strong low-level shear fostered a brief/weak EF0 tornado near Lock Haven in Clinton County. Isolated/localized wind damage also occurred within a few low-topped bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Duncannon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front with waves of low pressure along it brought heavy rain to Southeast Pennsylvania on the 16th. This caused poor drainage flooding as well as some minor flooding of creeks in and around Philadelphia. Rain began during the early morning of the 16th and fell heavy at times throughout the event. It ended during the late afternoon with event totals of two to four inches, highest in Montgomery County. The combination of the heavy rain runoff, higher astronomical tides associated with the recent full moon and an up the Delaware Bay flow on the 16th caused minor tidal flooding along the Delaware River and some of its tidal tributaries with the overnight high tide cycle on the 16th. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 8.2 feet above mean lower low water, the threshold for the start of minor tidal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crum Creek near Newtown Square was above its 6 foot flood stage from 206 p.m. EDT through 615 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 6.76 feet at 445 p.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 2.80 inches in Wayne, 2.78 inches in Norwood, 2.65 inches in Media and 2.30 inches in Drexel Hill.

A remnant mesoscale convective vortex (MCV) coupled with strong heating and convergence along a stalled frontal boundary fostered severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening. The environment near the stalled boundary was favorable for rotating storms with pronounced veering of winds in the lowest 1-2km AGL. While most storm reports consisted of large hail and damaging winds, an EF1 tornado was confirmed in Bedford County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 70 mph produced significant tree damage along Luce Road near Grampian. An NWS Survey team determined that the damage was consistent with straight-line winds, resulting in a localized microburst.

The very large hail produced significant property damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A semi-organized band of convection extending southward from the Buffalo, NY vicinity to near Pittsburgh, PA and Charleston, WV moved eastward during the late afternoon and evening hours. The band evolved into a well-defined linear feature with enhanced bowing segments, increasing damaging wind potential as it moved into a moderately unstable air mass located across central and eastern PA. Widespread damaging straight-line winds with embedded microburst damage accompanied the line as it crossed the Mid-State, knocking down numerous trees and utility wires. The damaging winds also caused localized structural damage and thousands of power outages. A few discrete cells ahead of and embedded in the main convective line tapped into strong low-level shear/helicity, which proved to be a favorable environment for tornadogenesis. Following official NWS storm surveys, an EF2 tornado was confirmed near Dushore in Sullivan County along with a 90-100mph microburst in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF2 tornado with maximum winds of 120 mph was confirmed near Dushore. The tornado produced considerable damage along a path that measured just over 2 miles long, exiting Sullivan County and crossing into the Bradford County (NWS Binghamton, NY CWA). The tornado was on the ground for about 5 minutes with a maximum path width of 400 yards. The tornado touched down in a field and crossed Elys Road, where a roof was blown off of a house and a barn. Some of this debris was thrown over 100 yards. A trailer was thrown 30 yards and demolished. Dozens of trees were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system and a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico and then the Atlantic Ocean dropped heavy rain across Eastern Pennsylvania centered on April 30th. Event precipitation totals averaged from 3 to 6 inches, with the highest amounts in the Philadelphia suburbs. This caused poor drainage and creek and river flooding that reached major levels around the Philadelphia Metropolitan Area. Over 1,000 people had to be evacuated from flooded homes and apartments. The American Red Cross opened several shelters and comfort stations in Delaware and Chester Counties. Numerous water rescues and road closures occurred. the evening commute on April 30th and the morning commute on May 1st were greatly impacted. Regional commuter rail lines in and around Philadelphia had major delays. The Schuylkill River had its highest crest since Tropical Storm Floyd in September of 1999. Other creeks and rivers had their highest crest since Tropical Storm Irene in August of 2011. The steady rain began around noon EDT on April 29th. The rain fell at a fairly steady light to moderate pace until the 30th. Then, the rain fell heavy at times from the morning into the evening of April 30th. Rainfall amounts of around 2 inches fell in the six hour period from 2 p.m. EDT through 8 p.m. EDT on April 30th in the local Philadelphia area. The rain ended from south to north between Midnight EDT and 6 a.m. EDT on May 1st.||The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of April 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of April 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on April 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of April 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on April 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on April 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused widespread poor drainage and moderate to major creek flooding in Bucks County. Flooding along the Neshaminy Creek was the worst since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  Even moderate tidal flooding occurred on the night of April 30th along the Delaware River. An apartment building along the East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek in Perkasie was evacuated because of flooding. Vehicles were stuck in flood waters in Bensalem Township. Townships reporting road closures due to flooding included Milford and New Britain.| |The Neshaminy Creek at Langhorne had major flooding and was above its 9 foot flood stage from 518 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 1232 p.m. EDT on May 1st.  It crested at 14.64 feet at 215 a.m. EDT on May 1st and was the highest crest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011. The Little Neshaminy Creek at Neshaminy had minor flooding and was above its 8 foot flood stage from 507 p.m. EDT through 1147 p.m. EDT on April 30th. It crested at 9.07 feet at 900 p.m. EDT. The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Dublin had moderate flooding and was above its 5 foot flood stage from 339 p.m. EDT through 740 p.m. EDT on April 30th. It crested at 6.82 feet at 545 p.m. EDT. ||Event precipitation totals included 4.90 inches in Quakertown and Richlandtown, 4.82 inches in Warrington, 4.79 inches in Furlong, 4.57 inches in Warwick Township and 4.42 inches in Doylestown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused widespread poor drainage, creek and river flooding throughout the county. More than forty major roadways were closed. There were twenty water rescues and over one hundred stranded vehicles. Numerous roads were closed. Creek and river flooding reached major category. Flooding along the Schuylkill River was the worst since Tropical Storm Floyd on September 16,1999 and flooding along the Perkiomen Creek was the worst since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011. Hardest hit municipalities included Bridgeport, Conshohocken, Springfield and Whitemarsh. ||In West Norriton Township, flooding along the Schuylkill River surrounded several apartment buildings at Rivercrest and The Lofts. All the residents were sheltered in place. In Lower Providence Township (Eagleville), along the Schuylkill River, the garages of the Riverview Landing Apartments were flooded. In Conshohocken Borough, flooding from the Schuylkill River flooded apartments and a hotel on Washington Street. Residents were asked to voluntarily evacuate. In Skippack Township, vehicles were stuck on Pennsylvania State Route 73. The Skippack Creek also flooded. For about an hour during the evening rush on April 30th in Lower Merion Township, Interstate 76 (Schuylkill Expressway) eastbound was closed due to flooding between State Route 3050 (exit 337) and U.S. Route 1 southbound (exit 339).  Sections of U.S. Route 422 were also closed. SEPTA Regional rail service was suspended through May 1st on the Manayunk-Norristown line. Roadways were flooded and closed in many other townships. Townships reporting multiple roadway closures included Upper Moreland, Perkiomen, Whitemarsh, Horsham, Limerick, Norristown (U.S. Route 202) and Bridgeport.||The West Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Hillegass rose above its 5 foot flood stage from 330 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 115 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 5.23 feet at 5 p.m. EDT on April 30th. It crested at 11.76 feet at 1230 a.m. EDT on May 1st.  ||The main stem of the Perkiomen Creek at East Greenville had minor flooding and was above its 4 foot flood stage from 202 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 924 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 6.02 feet at 730 p.m. EDT on April 30th.  Farther downstream, the Perkiomen Creek at Graterford had major flooding and was above its 11 foot flood stage from 319 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 906 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 16.02 feet at 1245 a.m. EDT on May 1st. This was historically the 8th highest crest on record for the creek and the highest crest since August 28, 2011, associated with Tropical Storm Irene. ||The Schuylkill River at Pottstown had minor flooding and was above its 12.5 foot flood stage from Midnight EDT through 131 p.m. EDT on May 1st. Damaging southeast winds developed in Erie County Pennsylvania on April 29th as the winds strengthened between low pressure approaching Chicago and strong high pressure over northern Quebec.  The southeasterly direction is favorable for downsloping winds off the higher terrain and a peak wind of 58 mph was recorded at the Erie Internaional airport at 1047 am EDT.  Winds remained strong into the evening with frequent gusts above 45 mph.  The strong winds brought down several trees and utility poles and one person was injured when a tree fell on them.  Phone line problems were reported at the Erie Water Plant due to the high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Erie International Airport observed a wind gust to 50 knots at 1047 am EDT.  Numerous trees and utility poles were brought down by the strong winds.  One person sustained injuries when a tree fell on him in the city of Erie.  The Erie Water Plant also reported telephone outages due to the strong winds.

States of Emergency were declared in Chester City, Darby Borough and Marple Township. Flooding along the main stem of the Brandywine Creek was the worst since Tropical Storm Floyd on September 16, 1999.  Even moderate tidal flooding occurred along the Delaware River on the night of April 30th.||Flooding along Chester Creek forced the evacuation of around 60 homes and 250 residents in the Parker Manor Section of Chester City. The American Red Cross assisted with opening shelters. Farther upstream, Brookhaven Borough recommended evacuations along Chester Creek Road. At the Kerlin Street Bridge, the creek was almost completely blocked by debris. The borough opened a welcome, warming center at the Borough Hall. Darby Borough declared an emergency as the Darby Creek flooded. Road closures included McDade, Springfield, New Walnut, Pine, 9th, Chestnut, 12th, 13th and 14th streets. A shelter was opened at the Darby Borough Recreation Center. U.S. Route 1 was closed in both directions between U.S. Route 202 (Wilmington Pike) in Chadds Ford Township to Pennsylvania State Route 52 (Lenape Rd.) in East Marlborough Township, in Chester County.| |The main stem of the Brandywine Creek at Chadds Ford had major flooding and was above its 9 foot flood stage from 505 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 1123 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 16.05 feet at 245 a.m. EDT on May 1st. This was historically the 3rd highest crest on record for the creek and the highest crest since September 16, 1999, associated with Tropical Storm Floyd.   ||The Chester Creek at Chester had moderate flooding and was above its 8 foot flood stage from 211 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 442 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 13.70 feet at 900 p.m. EDT on April 30th. The Crum Creek at Newtown Square had moderate flooding and was above its 6 foot flood stage from 152 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 200 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 9.55 feet at 815 p.m. EDT on April 30th.||Event precipitation totals included 5.56 inches in Wayne, 5.55 inches in Lansdowne, 5.52 inches in Drexel Hill and Broomall and 4.38 inches in Media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of showers and thunderstorms moved east across northwest Pennsylvania during the early evening of April 4th ahead of a cold front sweeping east across Pennsylvania.  Numerous reports of trees and power lines were reported down across Erie and Crawford Counties.  Thunderstorm winds caused trees and power lines to come down in downtown Erie, along with Cochranton, Vernon Township, and Townville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported a tree brought down by thunderstorm winds on Eagley Rd in Springfield Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over southeastern Canada during the late afternoon on the 17th in association with a cold front and progressive mid/upper trough moving through the Great Lakes region. Storms evolved into a mixed-mode downstream into Pennsylvania and adjacent Northeast states, where the atmosphere was moderately unstable and topped by a belt of strong winds aloft/0-6km shear. Isolated wind damage was reported across north-central PA between 2300 and 0100 EDT on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Monroe, Jackson, Penn and Washington Townships near Selinsgrove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger some strong to locally severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 13th. Hardest hit was Berks and the Lehigh Valley where the combination of damaging winds, lightning and flooding caused more than 6,800 homes and businesses to lose power. In addition, a couple of homes were struck and damaged by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southerly flow transported a plume of seasonably high moisture into the region along a slow-moving, north-south oriented cold front. This favorable heavy rain pattern produced a stripe of 2.5-5.0 inches of rainfall from the northern Blue Ridge into the north-central mountains of Pennsylvania. Widespread small stream flooding and multiple road closures were reported with five river forecast points in the Juniata and Lower Main Stem Susquehanna (Tributaries) basins exceeding minor to moderate flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall between 2 and 4 inches caused widespread minor flooding with several secondary road closures or one-lane restrictions. The Aughwick Creek at Shirleysburg exceeded moderate flood stage, impacting portions of Gilbo Road, Aughwick Mills Road and Keystone Road in Shirley Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered thunderstorms with heavy downpours across Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 13th. This was in addition to the heavy rain that fell across the Philadelphia northwest suburbs from the afternoon of the 12th into the early morning of the 13th preceding the same system's warm front. Total event precipitation amounts averaged between 2.0 and 3.5 inches. The initial shot of heavy rain left the ground nearly saturated and vulnerable to flash flooding which occurred in parts of Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and Montgomery County that afternoon and early evening. In addition, the combination of the preceding onshore flow, fresh water runoff and spring tides associated with the full moon caused minor tidal flooding during the overnight high tide cycle on the 13th along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 8.46 feet above mean lower low water early on the 14th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy downpours caused flash flooding of roadways and small creeks in Reading.  Several motorists were stranded within the flood waters. Spring Street was flooded and closed. The subway was submerged. Other roadways that were flooded included the Warren Street Bypass (Pennsylvania State Route 12). Event precipitation totals included 2.07 inches within Reading and 2.05 inches at the Reading Regional Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that developed along a frontal boundary helped produce clusters of showers and some strong to locally severe thunderstorms that moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning of the 19th. Several homes were also struck by lightning.  Approximately 4,500 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes occurred in the Mount Holly warning and forecast area as the thunderstorms moved through. Bucks County suffered the most outages in the area from the combined lightning and wind from the thunderstorms. About 30,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county. This ended the brief hot spell across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees along South 59th Street in South Philadelphia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A semi-organized band of convection extending southward from the Buffalo, NY vicinity to near Pittsburgh, PA and Charleston, WV moved eastward during the late afternoon and evening hours. The band evolved into a well-defined linear feature with enhanced bowing segments, increasing damaging wind potential as it moved into a moderately unstable air mass located across central and eastern PA. Widespread damaging straight-line winds with embedded microburst damage accompanied the line as it crossed the Mid-State, knocking down numerous trees and utility wires. The damaging winds also caused localized structural damage and thousands of power outages. A few discrete cells ahead of and embedded in the main convective line tapped into strong low-level shear/helicity, which proved to be a favorable environment for tornadogenesis. Following official NWS storm surveys, an EF2 tornado was confirmed near Dushore in Sullivan County along with a 90-100mph microburst in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires near New Freedom. One tree fell onto a house, causing minor structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The onshore flow that started on April 29th caused minor tidal flooding along tidal sections of the Delaware River with both high tide cycles on April 30th. But, the combination of the onshore flow and the freshwater runoff caused moderate tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River with the high tide cycle early on May 1st. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 9.94 feet above mean lower low water at 306 a.m. EDT on May 1st. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water. This was the 7th highest tide on record in Philadelphia. | |The tidal flooding was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of April 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of April 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on April 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of April 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on April 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on April 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning.  The formation of the second low pressure system and the passage of the warm front helped veer the winds from the east to the southeast and enhanced the surface flow that produced the moderate tidal flooding.

A shortwave moving across the region in the afternoon helped to produce showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe. In addition weak upper level flow allowed for several storms to produce heavy rainfall. The heavy rain caused flooding to several portions of Westmoreland county, PA including Latrobe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency manager reported flooded basements on Snyder Avenue in Unity Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger some strong to locally severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 13th. Hardest hit was Berks and the Lehigh Valley where the combination of damaging winds, lightning and flooding caused more than 6,800 homes and businesses to lose power. In addition, a couple of homes were struck and damaged by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a townhouse fire at the Stonegate Village townhouse development in Richland Township. It took firefighters about half an hour to extinguish the blaze. No serious injuries were reported.

In addition, a couple of homes were struck and damaged by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires within Allentown. A trained Skywarn spotter measured a wind gust of 54 mph in the south side of the city. The greatest number of power outages within Lehigh County was in Salisbury Township, just south of Allentown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms tracked into northwest Pennsylvania as a mid-level impulse interacted with a moist and unstable airmass over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained weather spotter reported thunderstorm winds brought down a large limb with a diameter of 16-18 inches and other large limbs down on Route 18 south of Albion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that developed along a frontal boundary helped produce clusters of showers and some strong to locally severe thunderstorms that moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the early morning of the 19th. Several homes were also struck by lightning.  Approximately 4,500 cloud-to-ground lightning strikes occurred in the Mount Holly warning and forecast area as the thunderstorms moved through. Bucks County suffered the most outages in the area from the combined lightning and wind from the thunderstorms. About 30,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county. This ended the brief hot spell across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning bolt damaged the chimney of a home at Wisteria Lane and Fox Glove Court in Doylestown. The lightning strike caused the brick chimney to explode. Bricks and mortar were left strewn across the home's roof, front yard and were even thrown into the street. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A semi-organized band of convection extending southward from the Buffalo, NY vicinity to near Pittsburgh, PA and Charleston, WV moved eastward during the late afternoon and evening hours. The band evolved into a well-defined linear feature with enhanced bowing segments, increasing damaging wind potential as it moved into a moderately unstable air mass located across central and eastern PA. Widespread damaging straight-line winds with embedded microburst damage accompanied the line as it crossed the Mid-State, knocking down numerous trees and utility wires. The damaging winds also caused localized structural damage and thousands of power outages. A few discrete cells ahead of and embedded in the main convective line tapped into strong low-level shear/helicity, which proved to be a favorable environment for tornadogenesis. Following official NWS storm surveys, an EF2 tornado was confirmed near Dushore in Sullivan County along with a 90-100mph microburst in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near New Franklin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A semi-organized convective line and accompanying cool pool progressed eastward from the Upper Ohio Valley across central and southern PA during the late evening hours between 2000 and 2300 EDT. Thunderstorms along and just ahead of the line produced damaging wind gusts and large hail, consistent with reports of numerous downed trees and isolated quarter-to-golf ball sized hail (1-1.75 inches).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and utility wires near East Berlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trof combined with an unseasonably warm air mass to develop a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon of the 27th. While the thunderstorms started to weaken as they approached Philadelphia and the Delaware River, their gust fronts still produced damaging winds. About 20,000 homes and businesses lost power, with the greatest impact in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees within Reading. One large tree was snapped at its base and landed on a vehicle on Oley Street. Several wires were also knocked down. Another nearby utility pole was snapped in half. Wind gusts of 54 mph and 53 mph were recorded at the Reading Regional Airport and Lenhartsville respectively.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nocturnal convective complex brought training thunderstorms to north-central Pennsylvania during the early morning hours. A localized 3-5 inches of rain fell over a 8-10 hour period which resulted in significant flash flooding in Elk and northern Clearfield Counties. Flash flooding, to a lesser extent, also occurred in adjacent Warren and Cameron Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain and flash flooding caused several road closures and flooded basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southerly flow transported a plume of seasonably high moisture into the region along a slow-moving, north-south oriented cold front. This favorable heavy rain pattern produced a stripe of 2.5-5.0 inches of rainfall from the northern Blue Ridge into the north-central mountains of Pennsylvania. Widespread small stream flooding and multiple road closures were reported with five river forecast points in the Juniata and Lower Main Stem Susquehanna (Tributaries) basins exceeding minor to moderate flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall between 2 and 4 inches produced multiple reports of flooding throughout the county. Four water rescues were performed. Numerous roads were impacted, including a section of US30 which was closed due to flooding. Over 100 people were evacuated from the Gettysburg Campground due to flooding along Marsh Creek, which borders the grounds.

Widespread small stream flooding and multiple road closures were reported with five river forecast points in the Juniata and Lower Main Stem Susquehanna (Tributaries) basins exceeding minor to moderate flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall between 2 and 4 inches produced multiple reports of flooding throughout the county. The flooding prompted evacuations for the Lincoln Dell Trailer Park at Routes 30 and 997 in Greene Township, and Baumgarner Lane in Washington Township. The following road closures were reported in Washington Township: 13500 Block Welty Road, Country Club/Fish and Game Road, Amsterdam Road, West Commerce Street, Chambersburg Borough and Stone Bridge Road, Fannett Township. The following road were closed in Greene Township: Coldspring Road between Main Street and Newman Road, Houser Road at Nelson Lane, Cook Road, Woodstock Road at Main Street, Fayetteville, Sycamore Road and Mt. Pleasant Road. The East Branch of the Antietam Creek flooded Renfrew Park. Red Run Creek came out of its banks and flooded nearby areas in Rounzerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The onshore flow that started on April 29th caused minor tidal flooding along tidal sections of the Delaware River with both high tide cycles on April 30th. But, the combination of the onshore flow and the freshwater runoff caused moderate tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River with the high tide cycle early on May 1st. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 9.94 feet above mean lower low water at 306 a.m. EDT on May 1st. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water. This was the 7th highest tide on record in Philadelphia. | |The tidal flooding was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of April 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of April 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on April 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of April 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on April 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on April 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning.  The formation of the second low pressure system and the passage of the warm front helped veer the winds from the east to the southeast and enhanced the surface flow that produced the moderate tidal flooding.

The Brown Township Sewer Authority reported a system inundation with storm water but was able to maintain operations. A water rescue was performed on Jacks Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front coupled with a strong low level jet brought severe winds to northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst brought peak winds between 70 and 90 MPH to Montrose during the morning hours. The storm peeled a roof off of a barn, tore flashing off of a house and resulted in large tree branch damage throughout the city. The width of damage was estimated to be one-half mile with the worst damage on the southeast side of Lake Montrose along Lake Shore Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving cold front continued to move across the region on the 28th. A few strong to severe storms developed in the afternoon over Ohio, but the primary threat was heavy rain which produced flooding over already saturated areas of western PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported flooded roads and basements in Sutersiville, Lowber, and in Sewickley, Rostraver, and South Huntingdon Townships.

Heavy rain preceding a warm front during the early morning of the 13th caused flooding along a couple of creeks in Chester and Montgomery Counties and made the region vulnerable to additional heavier rain later that day. While the rain started to fall during the afternoon of the 12th, it fell at its heaviest during the early morning of the 13th and ended before 8 a.m. EDT on the 13th.  Heavy rain fell again later on the 13th.  Entire event (including the afternoon and evening rain on the 13th) precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 2.0 inches in Chester County and 1.0 to 3.5 inches in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding along the upper reaches of the Perkiomen Creek. The Perkiomen Creek at East Greenville was above its 4 foot flood stage from 650 a.m. EDT through 1227 p.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 5.16 feet at 1100 a.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 2.95 inches in East Greenville and 2.61 inches in Perkiomenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A semi-organized band of convection extending southward from the Buffalo, NY vicinity to near Pittsburgh, PA and Charleston, WV moved eastward during the late afternoon and evening hours. The band evolved into a well-defined linear feature with enhanced bowing segments, increasing damaging wind potential as it moved into a moderately unstable air mass located across central and eastern PA. Widespread damaging straight-line winds with embedded microburst damage accompanied the line as it crossed the Mid-State, knocking down numerous trees and utility wires. The damaging winds also caused localized structural damage and thousands of power outages. A few discrete cells ahead of and embedded in the main convective line tapped into strong low-level shear/helicity, which proved to be a favorable environment for tornadogenesis. Following official NWS storm surveys, an EF2 tornado was confirmed near Dushore in Sullivan County along with a 90-100mph microburst in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires in Dillsburg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined upper level short wave trough moved from OH into PA during the afternoon. An associated surface low over western OH and trailing cold front moved across this area and promoted thunderstorm development over the upper OH valley into western PA. An isolated severe storm produced wind damage in Somerset County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees along Listie Road in Somerset Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nocturnal convective complex brought training thunderstorms to north-central Pennsylvania during the early morning hours. A localized 3-5 inches of rain fell over a 8-10 hour period which resulted in significant flash flooding in Elk and northern Clearfield Counties. Flash flooding, to a lesser extent, also occurred in adjacent Warren and Cameron Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused significant life-threatening flash flooding in central Elk County focused on the Borough of Ridgway. Major flooding occurred on the Clarion River at Ridgway, where the river crested at 21.67 feet - second highest crest on record. The flooding was life-threatening with numerous water rescues and evacuations in Elk County. A flash flood emergency was declared for Elk County during the height of the flash flooding. Elk County was declared a disaster area after flooding caused significant damage to homes and businesses. Multiple roads were closed due to high water including portions of Routes 120, 219, 255, 948, 1009, 2003, 2007 and 3002. Approximately 25 homes and up to 10 commercial buildings sustained first floor flooding. Ridgway Borough evacuated approximately 100 people from a high rise apartment at 101 North Mill Avenue to Central Fire Hall. Saint Leos Church School was evacuated.  Other schools throughout the county conducted early dismissals. Other areas impacted by the flooding included St. Mary's, Johnsonburg, Byrnedale, James City, Wilcox, Weedville, Kersey and Elbon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds followed a strong cold frontal passage in Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon of the 15th and persisted into the night over the Poconos. Peak wind gusts averaged around 40 mph, but reached closer to 50 mph in the Poconos and around the immediate Philadelphia area. The strong winds, coupled with the precipitation, knocked down some weak tree limbs and wires causing isolated power outages. Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in Port Richmond (Philadelphia County), 49 mph at Newtown Square (Delaware County) and 48 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County). The strong gusty winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between the cold front and the approaching high pressure system over the lower Ohio Valley. When the high pressure system reached the upper Ohio Valley on the morning of the 16th, the strong winds ceased in all areas.

A lee side trough and an unstable air mass produced scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey during the late afternoon to evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped hail ranging from nickel to quarter size in the town of Bath.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trough and an unstable air mass produced scattered strong to severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey during the late afternoon to evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped half dollar size hail in Moore Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first back-to-back days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher occurred across southeastern Pennsylvania on the 17th and 18th.  For many places, these were the first 90s of the year. High temperatures were in the lower to mid 90s both days with the hottest weather on the 18th. Afternoon heat index values were in the mid 90s both days. Because of the heat,  Philadelphia schools dismissed children early on the 18th. Highest temperatures included 94 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and Langhorne (Bucks County) and  93 degrees in Worcester (Montgomery County), Marcus Hook (Delaware County) and West Chester (Chester County). A slow moving cold front ended the heat as it moved through on the 19th.

An approaching cold front helped trigger some strong to locally severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 13th. Hardest hit was Berks and the Lehigh Valley where the combination of damaging winds, lightning and flooding caused more than 6,800 homes and businesses to lose power. In addition, a couple of homes were struck and damaged by lightning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down trees and wires in eastern Bern Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lee side trof combined with an unseasonably warm air mass to develop a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon of the 27th. While the thunderstorms started to weaken as they approached Philadelphia and the Delaware River, their gust fronts still produced damaging winds. About 20,000 homes and businesses lost power, with the greatest impact in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm also knocked down a few trees and wires in Northeast Philadelphia and caused power outages. A wind gust of 49 mph was measured at nearby Northeast Philadelphia Airport.

A slow moving cold front dropped between 1 and 3 inches of precipitation across Eastern Pennsylvania on the 15th. While some urban and poor drainage flooding occurred, because of the recent dry weather, only isolated creek flooding was reported.  The steady rain began during the morning and fell heavy at times from the late morning into the middle of the afternoon. The precipitation ended during the evening. It ended as snow, with the longest duration over the higher terrain locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage and some creek flooding in Chester County. The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek below Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 455 p.m. EDT through 955 p.m. EDT on the 15th. It crested at 8.91 feet at 730 p.m. EDT.  Event precipitation totals included 2.50 inches in Spring City, 2.37 inches in West Chester, 2.20 inches in West Grove, 2.10 inches in Nottingham and 2.00 inches in Chadds Ford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving low pressure system and a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico and then the Atlantic Ocean dropped heavy rain across Eastern Pennsylvania centered on the 30th. Event precipitation totals averaged from 3 to 6 inches, with the highest amounts in the Philadelphia suburbs. This caused poor drainage and creek and river flooding that reached major levels around the Philadelphia Metropolitan Area. Over 1,000 people had to be evacuated from flooded homes and apartments. The American Red Cross opened several shelters and comfort stations in Delaware and Chester Counties. Numerous water rescues and road closures occurred. the evening commute on the 30th and the morning commute on May 1st were greatly impacted. Regional commuter rail lines in and around Philadelphia had major delays. The Schuylkill River had its highest crest since Tropical Storm Floyd in September of 1999. Other creeks and rivers had their highest crest since Tropical Storm Irene in August of 2011. The steady rain began around noon EDT on the 29th. The rain fell at a fairly steady light to moderate pace until the 30th. Then, the rain fell heavy at times from the morning into the evening of the 30th. Rainfall amounts of around 2 inches fell in the six hour period from 2 p.m. EDT through 8 p.m. EDT on the 30th in the local Philadelphia area.  The rain ended from south to north between Midnight EDT and 6 a.m. EDT on May 1st. |  |The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of the 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of the 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on the 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of the 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on the 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on the 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage as well as some creek flooding in Lehigh County. The Little Lehigh Creek near Allentown had minor flooding and was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1025 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 815 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 6.31 feet at 445 a.m. EDT on May 1st.||Event precipitation totals included 4.29 inches in Schnecksville, 4.26 inches in Ancient Oaks, 4.01 inches in Trexlertown, 3.55 inches at the Allentown Airport, 3.54 inches in Slatington and 3.50 inches in Heidelberg Township.
