EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in Northampton County as well as flooding along the Bushkill Creek. West Douglasville Road was flooded in Nazareth and Bushkill Township because of the creek flooding on the night of the 30th.  Pennsylvania State Route 378 was closed in Northampton. Event precipitation totals included 5.37 inches in Fork Township, 4.91 inches in Martins Creek, 4.62 inches in Glendon and 4.52 inches in Nazareth. and 3.98 inches in Bushkill Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The onshore flow that started on the 29th caused minor tidal flooding along tidal sections of the Delaware River with both high tide cycles on the 30th.  But, the combination of the onshore flow and the freshwater runoff caused moderate tidal flooding along the tidal Delaware River with the high tide cycle early on May 1st. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 9.94 feet above mean lower low water at 306 a.m. EDT on May 1st. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water. This was the 7th highest tide on record in Philadelphia. | |The tidal flooding was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. The low pressure system was located in northwest Missouri on the morning of the 29th. It moved northeast to near Chicago during the afternoon of the 29th. The low pressure system then became nearly stationary in southern Wisconsin as its cold front reached Ohio early on the 30th. Then a new low pressure system developed on the frontal boundary and was over Eastern Tennessee on the morning of the 30th. The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on the 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on the 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning.  The formation of the second low pressure system and the passage of the warm front helped veer the winds from the east to the southeast and enhanced the surface flow that produced the moderate tidal flooding.

An approaching cold front triggered thunderstorms with heavy downpours across Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 13th. This was in addition to the heavy rain that fell across the Philadelphia northwest suburbs from the afternoon of the 12th into the early morning of the 13th preceding the same system's warm front. Total event precipitation amounts averaged between 2.0 and 3.5 inches. The initial shot of heavy rain left the ground nearly saturated and vulnerable to flash flooding which occurred in parts of Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and Montgomery County that afternoon and early evening. In addition, the combination of the preceding onshore flow, fresh water runoff and spring tides associated with the full moon caused minor tidal flooding during the overnight high tide cycle on the 13th along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 8.46 feet above mean lower low water early on the 14th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy downpours caused flash flooding of roadways and smaller creeks in Muhlenberg Township. Two vehicles were trapped in flood waters on North Fifth Street Highway near the Plaza 222 Shopping Center.

There were twenty water rescues and over one hundred stranded vehicles. Numerous roads were closed. Creek and river flooding reached major category. Flooding along the Schuylkill River was the worst since Tropical Storm Floyd on September 16,1999 and flooding along the Perkiomen Creek was the worst since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011. In Conshohocken Borough, flooding from the Schuylkill River flooded apartments and a hotel on Washington Street. Residents were asked to voluntarily evacuate. In Skippack Township, vehicles were stuck on Pennsylvania State Route 73. The Skippack Creek also flooded. For about an hour during the evening rush on the 30th in Lower Merion Township, Interstate 76 (Schuylkill Expressway) eastbound was closed due to flooding between State Route 3050 (exit 337) and U.S. Route 1 southbound (exit 339). Rainfall amounts of around 2 inches fell in the six hour period from 2 p.m. EDT through 8 p.m. EDT on the 30th in the local Philadelphia area.  The rain ended from south to north between Midnight EDT and 6 a.m. EDT on May 1st. |  |The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a strong high pressure system that built over the Canadian Maritimes that initiated the onshore flow and a strong, but slow moving low pressure system. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused widespread poor drainage, moderate to major creek and river flooding in Philadelphia. Flooding along the Schuylkill River was the worst since Tropical Storm Floyd on September 16, 1999.  Even moderate tidal flooding occurred on the night of April 30th. The flooding also affected regional rail lines as SEPTA service was suspended through May 1st between Manayunk and Norristown. Air travel was no better at Philadelphia International Airport as flight delays reached around two hours at the height of the storm and one third of all arriving and departing flights were cancelled on April 30th. In Manayunk, three people were rescued from the top of a SEPTA bus as Main Street was severely flooded. Residents were evacuated from an apartment complex on the river. Road closures included Martin Luther King, Kelly and Lincoln Drives.   ||The worst damage to city structures occurred at the Water Works facility and to the Gorgas Bridge on Forbidden Drive. About thirty residents on Venice Island Lofts suffered heavy apartment flooding and about thirty vehicles were towed. The paper factory on Venice Island was also damaged. The Manayunk Brewery suffered significant damage and was closed for several weeks as were some of its neighboring businesses. The Green Towne Montessori School was also heavily flooded. The water department responded to over 100 reports of residential basement flooding.| |The Frankford Creek at Castor Avenue had moderate flooding and twice rose above its 7 foot flood stage, first from 257 p.m. EDT through 323 p.m. EDT on April 30th with a crest of 7.28 feet at 315 p.m. EDT. The Frankford Creek at Castor Avenue again rose above its flood stage from 453 p.m. EDT through 837 p.m. EDT on April 30th, with moderate flooding occurring and a higher crest of 9.50 feet at 615 p.m. EDT. The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge had major flooding and was above its 7 foot flood stage from 307 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 530 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 12.03 feet at Midnight EDT on May 1st. It was the 6th highest crest on record and the highest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  |  |The Wissahickon Creek at its mouth had moderate flooding and was above its 5 foot flood stage from 405 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 837 a.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 8.30 feet at 1200 a.m. EDT on May 1st. This was the highest crest recorded since September 8, 2011.  The Schuylkill River at Philadelphia had moderate flooding and was above its 11 foot flood stage from 624 p.m. EDT on April 30th through 626 p.m. EDT on May 1st. It crested at 13.91 feet at 400 a.m. EDT on May 1st. This was historically the 7th highest crest on record for the river and the highest crest since September 17, 1999 associated with Tropical Storm Floyd.  | |The onshore flow that started on April 29th caused minor tidal flooding in Philadelphia with both high tide cycles on April 30th.  But, the combination of the residual onshore flow and the freshwater runoff caused moderate tidal flooding with the high tide cycle early on May 1st. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 9.94 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water. This was the 7th highest tide on record in Philadelphia.||Event precipitation totals included 5.45 inches in Rockledge, 4.91 inches at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport, 4.87 inches in Somerton and Philadelphia and 4.81 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first back-to-back days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher occurred across southeastern Pennsylvania on the 17th and 18th.  For many places, these were the first 90s of the year. High temperatures were in the lower to mid 90s both days with the hottest weather on the 18th. Afternoon heat index values were in the mid 90s both days. Because of the heat,  Philadelphia schools dismissed children early on the 18th. Highest temperatures included 94 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and Langhorne (Bucks County) and  93 degrees in Worcester (Montgomery County), Marcus Hook (Delaware County) and West Chester (Chester County). A slow moving cold front ended the heat as it moved through on the 19th.

A semi-organized band of convection extending southward from the Buffalo, NY vicinity to near Pittsburgh, PA and Charleston, WV moved eastward during the late afternoon and evening hours. The band evolved into a well-defined linear feature with enhanced bowing segments, increasing damaging wind potential as it moved into a moderately unstable air mass located across central and eastern PA. Widespread damaging straight-line winds with embedded microburst damage accompanied the line as it crossed the Mid-State, knocking down numerous trees and utility wires. The damaging winds also caused localized structural damage and thousands of power outages. A few discrete cells ahead of and embedded in the main convective line tapped into strong low-level shear/helicity, which proved to be a favorable environment for tornadogenesis. Following official NWS storm surveys, an EF2 tornado was confirmed near Dushore in Sullivan County along with a 90-100mph microburst in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires near New Salem.

The low pressure system reached West Virginia during the late afternoon on the 30th as its warm front started to move through Delmarva. The low pressure system reached north central Maryland at 11 p.m. EDT on the 30th as its warm front reached extreme southeast Pennsylvania and central New Jersey. The low pressure system reached the Allentown, Pennsylvania area at 5 a.m. EDT on May 1st and then was absorbed again into the frontal boundary associated with the original low as it moved into New York State that morning. The steady rain ended as the low pressure system passed north of Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy rain caused widespread poor drainage, moderate to major creek flooding in Bucks County. Flooding along the Neshaminy Creek was the worst since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011.  Even moderate tidal flooding occurred on the night of the 30th along the Delaware River. An apartment building along the East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek in Perkasie was evacuated because of flooding. Vehicles were stuck in flood waters in Bensalem Township. Townships reporting road closures due to flooding included Milford and New Britain.| |The Neshaminy Creek at Langhorne had major flooding and was above its 9 foot flood stage from 518 p.m. EDT on the 30th through 1232 p.m. EDT on May 1st.  It crested at 14.64 feet at 215 a.m. EDT on May 1st and was the highest crest since Tropical Storm Irene on August 28, 2011. The Little Neshaminy Creek at Neshaminy had minor flooding and was above its 8 foot flood stage from 507 p.m. EDT through 1147 p.m. EDT on the 30th. It crested at 9.07 feet at 900 p.m. EDT. The East Branch of the Perkiomen Creek at Dublin had moderate flooding and was above its 5 foot flood stage from 339 p.m. EDT through 740 p.m. EDT on the 30th. It crested at 6.82 feet at 545 p.m. EDT. ||Event precipitation totals included 4.90 inches in Quakertown and Richlandtown, 4.82 inches in Warrington, 4.79 inches in Furlong, 4.57 inches in Warwick Township and 4.42 inches in Doylestown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A semi-organized band of convection extending southward from the Buffalo, NY vicinity to near Pittsburgh, PA and Charleston, WV moved eastward during the late afternoon and evening hours. The band evolved into a well-defined linear feature with enhanced bowing segments, increasing damaging wind potential as it moved into a moderately unstable air mass located across central and eastern PA. Widespread damaging straight-line winds with embedded microburst damage accompanied the line as it crossed the Mid-State, knocking down numerous trees and utility wires. The damaging winds also caused localized structural damage and thousands of power outages. A few discrete cells ahead of and embedded in the main convective line tapped into strong low-level shear/helicity, which proved to be a favorable environment for tornadogenesis. Following official NWS storm surveys, an EF2 tornado was confirmed near Dushore in Sullivan County along with a 90-100mph microburst in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees and wires near Rockville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front triggered thunderstorms with heavy downpours across Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon of the 13th. This was in addition to the heavy rain that fell across the Philadelphia northwest suburbs from the afternoon of the 12th into the early morning of the 13th preceding the same system's warm front. Total event precipitation amounts averaged between 2.0 and 3.5 inches. The initial shot of heavy rain left the ground nearly saturated and vulnerable to flash flooding which occurred in parts of Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and Montgomery County that afternoon and early evening. In addition, the combination of the preceding onshore flow, fresh water runoff and spring tides associated with the full moon caused minor tidal flooding during the overnight high tide cycle on the 13th along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries. The high tide in Philadelphia reached 8.46 feet above mean lower low water early on the 14th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy downpours caused flash flooding of roadways and smaller creeks in and around Wyomissing. At least one vehicle was stranded in flood waters on State Hill Road near Bern Road in Wyomissing. Event precipitation total was 2.20 inches in Wyomissing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow fell across Eastern Pennsylvania from the middle of the afternoon through the middle of the evening on the 9th. A steady light to moderate rain event over approximately twenty-four hours coupled with some snow melt caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some creeks in Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos on the 11th. Rain began during the evening of the 10th, fell at its heaviest during the morning and early afternoon on the 11th and ended during the evening of the 11th. Storm totals averaged around 1.5 inches, except reached up to 2.5 inches in the Poconos. The rain was caused by a milder and moist southwest flow of air preceding a cold front. The cold front's low pressure system moved through the Great Lakes and the Saint Lawrence River Valley on the 11th and dragged its cold front through Pennsylvania during the middle of the evening on the 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The combination of snow melt and heavy rain caused flooding along The Bushkill Creek  in Bushkill Township. The creek flooded West Douglassville and Clearfield Roads. Event precipitation totals included 1.51 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and 1.27 inches in Forks Township.

A developing cold front triggered snow showers in Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 26th. The snow showers and snowfall were greatest in Berks County, Philadelphia and its suburbs averaging 1 to 2 inches. Less than one inch of snow on average fell elsewhere in Eastern Pennsylvania. The snow showers started between 5 a.m. and 6 a.m. EST on the 26th, fell at their heaviest intensity during the middle of the morning and finished moving through between 10 a.m. EST and 1130 a.m. EST that day. During the heavier snow bursts, untreated and less traveled roadways became snow covered and slippery.  ||Representative snowfall included 1.9 inches in West Grove (Chester County), 1.8 inches in Kutztown (Berks County),1.3 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County) and Norwood (Delaware County), 1.2 inches in Warwick (Chester County) and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 1.1 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.0 inch in Nottingham (Chester County) and Fleetwood (Berks County). A cold front developed over Western Pennsylvania early on the 26th and progressed southeast. It moved through Eastern Pennsylvania  between 9 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST on the 26th and coincided with the end of the heavier snow showers.

A winter storm dropped 5 to 9 inches of snow across Eastern Pennsylvania from the middle of afternoon of the 2nd into the morning of the 3rd. This caused hazardous traveling conditions for the evening commute on the 2nd and the morning commute on the 3rd.  Three storm related deaths occurred, all in Bucks County.  After one day of classes after the winter break, many schools were closed again on the 3rd.||In Bucks County, just as the snow was starting, a 51-year-old man was crushed to death in Falls Township when a 100 foot tall salt pile toppled and collapsed on him as he was operating a front end loader. An 83-year-old man collapsed and died as he was clearing the snow off his vehicle in Southampton Township. A 70-year-old Langhorne woman was found dead outside. AAA Mid-Atlantic reported over 200 calls for assistance from stuck motorists in Pennsylvania and New Jersey. The heavy snow caused numerous flight cancellations at Philadelphia International Airport and many travelers were stranded at the airport overnight. In addition to school closings, many government offices were closed on the 3rd. Many shopping malls opened late.||The snow started between 3 p.m. EST and 6 p.m. EST on the 2nd. It started first in Berks County and the Poconos and the latest in Montgomery and Bucks Counties. The snow fell at a fairly steady rate with the heaviest rate occurring during the evening hours. The snow ended from west to east between 6 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST on the 3rd. It exited lower Bucks County last. Strong northwest winds and the powdery nature of the snow caused blowing and drifting even after it ended on the 3rd.||Representative snowfall totals included 9.0 inches in Bensalem (Bucks County) and the Philadelphia International Airport, 8.7 inches in Newtown Square (Delaware County), 8.0 inches in East Nantmeal (Chester County), 7.5 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County) and Wynnewood (Montgomery County),  7.4 inches in King of Prussia (Montgomery County), 7.2 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, Springtown (Bucks County) and Drexel Hill (Delaware County), 7.0 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County), Fox Chase (Philadelphia), Nazareth and Forks Township (Northampton County) and Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 6.3 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County),  6.2 inches in Wyomissing (Berks County), Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), Palmerton (Carbon County) and Doylestown (Bucks County), 6.0 inches in Slatington (Lehigh County), 5.8 inches in Pocono Summit (Monroe County) and 5.5 inches in Honey Brook (Chester County).| |The winter storm was caused by a rapidly intensifying low pressure system. The low was moving through the Ohio River Valley on the morning of the 2nd. Meanwhile an arctic high pressure system near Montreal supplied an ample supply of fresh cold air. At 1 p.m. EST that afternoon, the low pressure system reached West Virginia. From there, it reformed farther to the southeast and was over northern North Carolina at 7 p.m. EST that evening and passed east of Elizabeth City, North Carolina around 9 p.m. EST. Once it reached the coast, it started to intensify rapidly as it moved eastnortheast. The low pressure system went from 998 millibars at 10 p.m. EST on the 2nd to 981 millibars located at 40 degrees north latitude and 65 degrees west longitude at 7 a.m. EST on the 3rd.

Strong gusty west to northwest winds occurred in Eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 27th following an arctic cold frontal passage. Peak wind gusts averaged around 40 mph, but some peak gusts reached around 45 mph just after the front passed during the afternoon in southeast Pennsylvania. Peak wind gusts for the day included 46 mph at the Northeast Philadelphia Airport and Lenhartsville (Berks County), 44 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and 38 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport and Mount Pocono (Monroe County).

The very persistent and strong west to northwest winds that started during the afternoon of the 27th coupled with the greater tidal fluctuations associated with the spring tides with the approaching new moon caused blowout tides during the morning low tide cycle on the 28th along tidal sections of the Delaware River in Pennsylvania. In Marcus Hook (Delaware County), the low tide reached 2.05 feet below mean lower low water. In Philadelphia, the low tide reached 2.13 feet below mean lower low water and on Newbold Island (Bucks County) the low tide reached 2.24 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water. The northwest winds decreased during the day on the 28th and while tidal levels were below normal astronomical levels, no further blowout conditions occurred.

The very persistent and strong west to northwest winds that started during the afternoon of the 27th coupled with the greater tidal fluctuations associated with the spring tides with the approaching new moon caused blowout tides during the morning low tide cycle on the 28th along tidal sections of the Delaware River in Pennsylvania. In Marcus Hook (Delaware County), the low tide reached 2.05 feet below mean lower low water. In Philadelphia, the low tide reached 2.13 feet below mean lower low water and on Newbold Island (Bucks County) the low tide reached 2.24 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.80 feet below mean lower low water. The northwest winds decreased during the day on the 28th and while tidal levels were below normal astronomical levels, no further blowout conditions occurred.

A developing cold front triggered snow showers in Eastern Pennsylvania during the morning of the 26th. The snow showers and snowfall were greatest in Berks County, Philadelphia and its suburbs averaging 1 to 2 inches. Less than one inch of snow on average fell elsewhere in Eastern Pennsylvania. The snow showers started between 5 a.m. and 6 a.m. EST on the 26th, fell at their heaviest intensity during the middle of the morning and finished moving through between 10 a.m. EST and 1130 a.m. EST that day. During the heavier snow bursts, untreated and less traveled roadways became snow covered and slippery.  ||Representative snowfall included 1.9 inches in West Grove (Chester County), 1.8 inches in Kutztown (Berks County),1.3 inches in Langhorne (Bucks County) and Norwood (Delaware County), 1.2 inches in Warwick (Chester County) and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County), 1.1 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport and 1.0 inch in Nottingham (Chester County) and Fleetwood (Berks County). A cold front developed over Western Pennsylvania early on the 26th and progressed southeast. It moved through Eastern Pennsylvania  between 9 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST on the 26th and coincided with the end of the heavier snow showers.

A strong cold front produced strong gusty west winds as it moved through Eastern Pennsylvania between 6 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST on the 6th. The cold frontal passage initiated the onslaught of a brutally cold arctic air mass that peaked the next day. Peak wind gusts were around 45 mph and knocked over weak tree limbs and wires. These included 51 mph in Forks Township (Northampton County), 49 mph in Newtown Square (Delaware County), 48 mph in Lenhartsville (Berks County), 46 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 45 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County) and 41 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County).

The unseasonably cold arctic air mass resulted in ice jam flooding along the Delaware River north of the jam in Bucks County and along the Lehigh River in Northampton County before its confluence with the Delaware River on January 8th and 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam along the Lehigh River caused flooding along Lehigh Drive (State Route 2026) from the Old Glendon Bridge to its confluence with the Delaware River. In West Easton Borough from the afternoon of the 8th into the morning of the 9th. A vehicle became stuck in four feet of icy water on Lehigh Drive after driving around a barricade. Temperatures that rose above freezing on the 9th and 10th helped ease the ice jam.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across east central and southeast Pennsylvania on the 21st and caused scores of accidents and tremendous traffic tie-ups on the afternoon of the 21st as well as least one storm related death.  Hundreds of flights were cancelled at the Philadelphia International Airport and many civic events were cancelled. An 87-year-old man in Wallingford (Delaware County) died after shoveling snow.||The heavy snow was caused by a quick moving clipper system tracked out of the Great Lakes region and began transferring its energy to the developing coastal low, resulting in rapid intensification. This strengthening low pressure moved northeast along the frontal boundary through the afternoon of the 21st, allowing multiple bands of heavy snow to traverse across portions of southeastern Pennsylvania. By late that day, the coastal low emerged east of the Delmarva Peninsula and quickly moved off towards New England, bringing an end to the snow from west to east across the region during the evening to overnight of the 21st to 22nd. ||Along and near the Interstate 95 corridor, temperatures were cold enough for rapid accumulation, and a narrow band of heavy snow developed in this vicinity.  Within this general area, snowfall rates reached 2 to 3 inches per hour for a time during the midday hours of the 21st. Another band of heavy snow developed during the evening of the 21st over the northwestern Philadelphia suburbs. Snowfall totals ranged from around 12 inches  in the local Philadelphia area, 6 to 10 inches in the Philadelphia northwest suburbs, around 6 inches in the Lehigh Valley and Berks County and 1 to 4 inches in the Poconos. ||In the greater Philadelphia area, the heavy banding snow led to the early dismissal of businesses, closing of malls and government offices and any schools that were open. This led to tremendous delays on roadways as traffic congestion complicated snow removal activities. Two hour or long commutes were common. The snow caused delays on SEPTA commuter rail service and forced the suspension or re-routing of about 30 bus routes.  Philadelphia International Airport managed to keep one runway open, but hundreds of flights were cancelled nonetheless. About 155 passengers spent the night at the airport. The Philadelphia Flyers-Carolina Hurricanes NHL hockey game was postponed. The effects of the heavy snow in southeast Pennsylvania continued the next day. Schools were closed. About 100 flights were cancelled at the Philadelphia International Airport. Major tourist attractions in Philadelphia were closed.  ||Representative snowfall totals included 13.9 inches in Clifton Heights (Delaware County), 13.5 inches at the Philadelphia International A single cloud to ground lightning strike in the Manayunk section of Philadelphia damaged a transformer and nearby windows.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A single cloud-to-ground lightning strike struck and caused a transformer explosion on Hermit Street in the Manayunk section of Philadelphia. The concussive force of the transformer explosion damaged the windows of at least six homes in the area. One home suffered damage to a dozen windows. The vibrations from the explosion were felt up to one mile away. No serious injuries were reported.

Strengthening surface low pressure south of the region, advancing eastward from Kentucky to the North Carolina coast, coupled with an upper air disturbance moving overhead produced accumulating snow across portions of eastern Pennsylvania during the day on the 15th.  Snowfall totals ranged from 6 inches across the higher elevations in Carbon and Monroe Counties to between 1 and 4 inches southward into the Lehigh Valley, Berks County, and the western Philadelphia suburbs, with mainly an inch or less falling closer to the city.  The snow was ongoing throughout the day across the southern Poconos, from around 330 a.m. EST to about 9 p.m. EST, with a period of moderate snow between 9 a.m. EST and 3 p.m. EST that contributed to higher accumulations.  Elsewhere, the expanding precipitation shield from the strengthening surface low pressure yielded snow beginning between 8 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST across the Lehigh Valley, Berks County, and into southeastern Pennsylvania.  Snow totals were somewhat higher into the Lehigh Valley and Berks County as air temperatures were a little colder through the event, and increased snowfall rates with a period of moderate snow in the early afternoon allowed for higher accumulations.  Meanwhile, closer to the city of Philadelphia, the precipitation began as rain around 830 a.m. EST, with some mixing with sleet and a changeover to snow occurring during the afternoon of the 15th as air temperatures cooled somewhat.  As low pressure quickly advanced east and off the coast by late-day, snow generally ended across much of eastern Pennsylvania between 5 p.m. EST and 7 p.m. EST, with lingering snow in the Poconos until about 9 p.m. EST.||The weight of the snow on roof tops continued to cause concerns. In Lehigh County, in South Whitehall Township the snow damaged a building which housed four tennis courts. In Upper Macungie Township,  an Amazon warehouse was evacuated because of roofing concerns. In Northampton County, in Wilson Borough, a building on Butler Street which housed a laundromat was evacuated because the structure was buckling. The roadway was closed. In Hanover Township, a Wegman's was closed because of concern over the structural integrity of its roof.||Representative snowfall totals included 6.0 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County), 5.8 inches at Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 4.7 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County) and in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 4.0 inches in Tobyhanna (Monroe County) and in Danielsville (Northampton County), 3.8 inches in Slatington (Lehigh County) and in Germansville (Lehigh County), 3.4 inches in Huffs Church (Berks County), 3.0 inches in Heidelberg Township (Lehigh County) and in Kutztown (Berks County) and also in Pennsburg (Montgomery County), 2.8 inches in Bushkill Township (Northampton County) and in Hellertown (Northampton County), 2.7 inches in Glenmoore (Chester County), 2.3 inches in Birdsboro (Berks County), 1.8 inches in Warwick (Chester County) and in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County) and also in Graterford (Montgomery County), 1.7 inches in West Rockhill Township (Bucks County) and in Devault (Chester County), 1.3 inches in Wayne (Delaware County) and in Langhorne (Bucks County), 1.1 inches in Furlong (Bucks County), 0.6 inches at the Philadelphia International Airport (Philadelphia County), and 0.5 inches in Fox Chase (Philadelphia County) and in Upper Darby (Delaware County).

Low pressure advancing across the region brought a wintry mix of precipitation across eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania late on the 17th through the 18th. Light freezing rain and sleet impacted much of the region south of Pittsburgh before changing to snow, while areas further north experienced mainly snow. Freezing rain and sleet amounts were generally a trace across the region. Snowfall amounts north of Pittsburgh ranged from 3-5 inches with isolated higher amounts of up to 6 inches across Lawrence and Butler counties in PA. An enhanced area of snow produced 6-8.5 inches across Jefferson county, PA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 3-6 inches across the county. Thundersnow was reported.

Snowfall totals ranged from 6 inches across the higher elevations in Carbon and Monroe Counties to between 1 and 4 inches southward into the Lehigh Valley, Berks County, and the western Philadelphia suburbs, with mainly an inch or less falling closer to the city. A major winter storm brought heavy snow, sleet and freezing rain to the Poconos, a heavy wintry mixture to the Lehigh Valley and Berks County and a crippling ice storm to Philadelphia and in particular its western suburbs. Snowfall reached as high as ten inches in the Poconos and ice accumulations were as high as half an inch. The combination of new ice and snow still on the trees from the just concluded winter storm caused a record breaking 715,000 customer power outages in the PECO Energy service area in and around Philadelphia (849.000 throughout Pennsylvania). This was the worst power outage event ever for the winter season, represented about 45 percent of all its customers and the second worst behind Sandy for the utility. It took about one week to have power fully restored. The weight of the snow, then sleet and freezing rain on limbs all collaborated to cause more tree damage then would have occurred if trees were bare at the start of this event.  Governor Tom Corbett declared a State of Emergency. President Barack Obama also declared a state of emergency in Pennsylvania. Many counties and municipalities also declared snow emergencies or states of emergency. Government offices, schools and businesses were closed. Some schools as well as universities, such as Villanova and West Chester, remained closed for the rest of the week. More than a dozen hospitals in the Philadelphia area ran on generators. Red Cross shelters and warming centers were opened. Some boil water advisories were also in effect because water treatment plants lost power.||In Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, precipitation started as sleet (and a bit of snow) during the late evening on the 4th. Precipitation changed over to freezing rain within a couple of hours of the start. Precipitation then changed to plain rain from south to north between 6 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 5th. The rain ended that afternoon. In Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, precipitation started as snow right around Midnight EST on the 5th. The snow fell heavy at times before sunrise in the Poconos. In the Lehigh Valley and Berks County, precipitation changed to sleet between 1 a.m. EST and 2 a.m. EST on the 5th and to freezing rain between 2 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the 5th. Precipitation changed to plain rain between 8 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST that morning and ended during that afternoon. In the Poconos, precipitation changed to sleet around 7 a.m. EST on the 5th, then freezing rain around 9 a.m. EST that morning. Some valley locations changed to plain rain, but over the higher terrain freezing rain persisted until precipitation ended during the middle of the afternoon.  A very light wintry mix then occurred in the Poconos during the early evening of the 5th. |  |The greatest impact from the winter and ice storm occurred in the Philadelphia suburbs as power was lost for up to one week. Repair crews from throughout North America assisted. About 5,100 workers were involved in the restoration process. Sections of the Schuylkill Expressway and U.S. Routes 202 and 322 were closed because of downed trees. Downed trees also littered the Pennsylvania Turnpike, the Pennsylvania Turnpike Northeast Extension and U.S. Routes 30 and 1. There were posted speed reductions on the Pennsylvania Turnpike and an empty tractor-trailer ban. Commuter rail and bus service was also affected. Amtrak rail service between Philadelphia and Harrisburg was suspended until the 8th. SEPTA suspended four regional rail lines until the 6th. Others ran with delays of at least 30 minutes. ||In Chester County, whole municipalities lost power as did 87 percent of the county overall. There were about 400 tree blocked roadways in the county.  Hardest hit municipalities included Highland, Charlestown, East Nantmeal, Parkesburg and West Vincent. West Chester University was used as a shelter. About 265 Verizon telephone customers lost service in West Fallowfield Township. In Montgomery County, there were 340 calls for electrical fires, 1,001 road obstructions, 153 crashes, and more than 4,000 calls for police assistance. Fifty major roadways were blocked by fallen trees. In Philadelphia, there were 139 cancelled flights at the Philadelphia International Airport on the 5th. A fallen tree limb injured one man. In Bucks County, over 90 percent of Doylestown, New Hope and Yardley lost power. Eighty state maintained highways were blocked by fallen trees. Power outages in Delaware County were the worst along its border with Chester County. Throughout the Philadelphia area, dozens of people suffered carbon monoxide poisoning because of improper ventilation of generators. ||In the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, power outages were less as more precipitation fell as snow and sleet. The total number of customers without power were around 1,000. In Lynn Township (Lehigh County), a collision between a pick-up truck and van resulted in the death of a 52-year-old man and a 58-year-old woman and one additional injury. In Northampton County,  Pennsylvania State Route 33 Northbound was closed near Freemansburg Avenue in Bethlehem Township due to a crash that involved a tractor trailer and a bus.||The number of PECO Energy customer outages peaked at 715,000 on the 5th. It dropped to 499,000 on the morning of the 6th, 440,000 on the evening of the 6th, 275,000 on the afternoon of the 7th, 182,000 on the evening of the 7th, 160,000 on the morning of the 8th, 82,000 on the evening of the 8th, 23,000 on the afternoon of the 9th and 5,600 on the 10th. This ice storm also made it the worst winter for outages in the utility's history: 1,097,900 outages. The previous winter season record occurred in 1993-1994 with 1,011,377 outages. ||Representative ice accumulations included 0.50 inches in West Chester (Chester County) and Wayne (Delaware County), 0.40 inches in Northeast Philadelphia, Allentown (Lehigh County), Blandon (Berks County) and Royersford (Montgomery County), 0.30 inches in Upper Darby (Delaware County), Bushkill Township (Northampton County) and Eaglesville (Montgomery County), 0.25 inches in Furlong (Bucks County) and Gilbertsville (Montgomery County) and 0.20 inches in Mount Pocono (Monroe County) and Forks Township (Northampton County). ||Representative snowfall and sleet accumulations included 10.0 inches in Lake Harmony (Carbon County), 9.5 inches in Albrightsville (Carbon County), 9.0 inches in Tobyhanna and Pocono Summit (Monroe County), 7.0 inches in Kidder Township (Carbon County) and Bushkill Township (Northampton County), 6.5 inches in Jim Thorpe (Carbon County), 6.2 inches in Saylorsburg (Monroe County), 6.0 inches in East Stroudsburg (Monroe County), 4.0 inches in Palmerton (Carbon County) and Forks Township (Northampton County), 3.7 inches in Lehighton (Carbon County), 3.1 inches in Schnecksville (Lehigh County), 3.0 inches in Martins Creek (Northampton County), 2.4 inches at the Lehigh Valley International Airport, 2.0 inches in Kutztown (Berks County), 1.2 inches in Gilbertsville (Montgomery County) and 0.8 inches in Chester Springs (Chester County).  ||The winter storm was caused by a low pressure system that formed in the western Gulf of Mexico on the morning of the 4th and a surface high pressure system that passed over the region and with the assistance of the snow cover locked in cold air near the surface. This low pressure system moved northeast and reached Mississippi during the early evening of the 4th and the Tennessee River Valley around Midnight EST on the 5th. About the same time, a secondary low pressure system was forming on the primary low's warm front off the North Carolina coast. Both low pressure systems in tandem proceeded to move northeast and at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th, the primary low pressure system was over southeast Ohio with the secondary low pressure system off the Delaware coast. As is typically the case, the primary low pressure system weakened and reached western Pennsylvania during the early afternoon on the 5th while the stronger secondary low pressure system was just off of Long Beach Island on the New Jersey coast. A major winter storm brought heavy snow, sleet and freezing rain to the Poconos, a heavy wintry mixture to the Lehigh Valley and Berks County and a crippling ice storm to Philadelphia and in particular its western suburbs. Snowfall reached as high as ten inches in the Poconos and ice accumulations were as high as half an inch. The combination of new ice and snow still on the trees from the just concluded winter storm caused a record breaking 715,000 customer power outages in the PECO Energy service area in and around Philadelphia (849.000 throughout Pennsylvania). This was the worst power outage event ever for the winter season, represented about 45 percent of all its customers and the second worst behind Sandy for the utility. It took about one week to have power fully restored. The weight of the snow, then sleet and freezing rain on limbs all collaborated to cause more tree damage then would have occurred if trees were bare at the start of this event.  Governor Tom Corbett declared a State of Emergency. President Barack Obama also declared a state of emergency in Pennsylvania. Many counties and municipalities also declared snow emergencies or states of emergency. Government offices, schools and businesses were closed. Some schools as well as universities, such as Villanova and West Chester, remained closed for the rest of the week. More than a dozen hospitals in the Philadelphia area ran on generators. Red Cross shelters and warming centers were opened. Some boil water advisories were also in effect because water treatment plants lost power.||In Philadelphia and the nearby suburbs, precipitation started as sleet (and a bit of snow) during the late evening on the 4th. Precipitation changed over to freezing rain within a couple of hours of the start. Precipitation then changed to plain rain from south to north between 6 a.m. EST and 9 a.m. EST on the 5th. The rain ended that afternoon. In Berks County, the Lehigh Valley and the Poconos, precipitation started as snow right around Midnight EST on the 5th. The snow fell heavy at times before sunrise in the Poconos. In the Lehigh Valley and Berks County, precipitation changed to sleet between 1 a.m. EST and 2 a.m. EST on the 5th and to freezing rain between 2 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the 5th. Precipitation changed to plain rain between 8 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST that morning and ended during that afternoon. In the Poconos, precipitation changed to sleet around 7 a.m. EST on the 5th, then freezing rain around 9 a.m. EST that morning. Some valley locations changed to plain rain, but over the higher terrain freezing rain persisted until precipitation ended during the middle of the afternoon.  A very light wintry mix then occurred in the Poconos during the early evening of the 5th. |  |The greatest impact from the winter and ice storm occurred in the Philadelphia suburbs as power was lost for up to one week. Repair crews from throughout North America assisted. About 5,100 workers were involved in the restoration process. Sections of the Schuylkill Expressway and U.S. Routes 202 and 322 were closed because of downed trees. Downed trees also littered the Pennsylvania Turnpike, the Pennsylvania Turnpike Northeast Extension and U.S. Routes 30 and 1. There were posted speed reductions on the Pennsylvania Turnpike and an empty tractor-trailer ban. Commuter rail and bus service was also affected. Amtrak rail service between Philadelphia and Harrisburg was suspended until the 8th. SEPTA suspended four regional rail lines until the 6th. About 265 Verizon telephone customers lost service in West Fallowfield Township. A winter weather event that fell mainly as snow in the Poconos and Lehigh Valley and a wintry mix in the rest of Eastern Pennsylvania became predominately freezing rain in and around Philadelphia. This caused numerous accidents during the morning commute on the 10th especially in the greater Philadelphia Metropolitan Area. Snowfall from the far northern Philadelphia suburbs through the Poconos averaged 1 to 2 inches. But it was the around one tenth of an inch of ice that fell around Philadelphia that had the greatest impact. ||In the Philadelphia northern suburbs, the Lehigh Valley, Berks County and the Poconos, precipitation started as snow from the Poconos southward between 2 a.m. EST and 5 a.m. EST on the 10th. Precipitation changed to sleet in Berks County and the northern Philadelphia suburbs between 9 a.m. EST and 10 a.m. EST. All precipitation ended from north to south between 10 a.m. EST and 11 a.m. EST that morning. A significant ice jam flood was observed along Losey Creek in Middlebury, PA (Tioga County) on March 12th. The flood waters along with some ice chunks flowed across Rt. 287 and other secondary roads, affecting homes located in low-lying and poor drainage areas near the creek. The water eventually froze and contributed to the flooding impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ice jam flooding along Losey Creek impacted the Middlebury area along Rt. 287. Many low-lying and poor drainage areas experienced flooding. Several homes were threatened due to flood waters and ice chunks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex weather system brought 2-4 inches of rainfall over a 24-48 hour period across the Lower Susquehanna Valley. The heavy rain combined with cold-season hydrology (frozen ground contributing to excessive runoff) produced several areas of flooding, particularly in Adams and York Counties. The only river forecast point to rise above flood stage was near Camp Hill on Yellow Breeches Creek, which crested at 8.1 feet or about one foot above minor flood stage on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall and excessive runoff produced flooding of several road intersections and some small streams.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall and excessive runoff produced flooding of several road intersections and some small streams.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent, narrow band of rain, wet snow and sleet on the backside of a cut-off upper low centered near the Delmarva/NJ coast produced significant travel impacts across portions of the Susquehanna Valley. Locally heavy snow (6 to as much as 12 inches) was observed from the Middle Susquehanna Valley (Shamokin area) into the Poconos/Endless Mountain region northeast of Williamsport, with the highest amount reported atop the North Mountain at Ricketts Glen State Park (2449 FT elevation) in southeastern Sullivan County. Lesser amounts (1-3) were reported closer to the Mason-Dixon line, but still managed to cause serious travel conditions. Numerous traffic accidents were reported due to slick, icy and snow/slush covered roads. The travel difficulties impacted most of the major and secondary roads from York County northward through Columbia and Sullivan Counties. The poor road conditions were blamed for at least one confirmed fatality.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Poor road conditions caused by rain, snow and ice were blamed for numerous crashes in York County, including one that was fatal. A 52-year-old woman was pronounced dead at the scene of a two-vehicle crash on Carlisle Road in Dover Township around 1650 EDT. Northern York County Regional Police believe the crash was caused when a 16-year-old driver of a Jeep Cherokee, that the 52-year-old woman was a passenger in, lost control on the icy road and went into the northbound lane, colliding with a Dodge Durango SUV. The 44-year-old man driving the Dodge SUV suffered minor injuries, and the teen suffered life-threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front that moved in from the Great Lakes region allowed for strong to severe thunderstorms to develop across portions of northeast Pennsylvania. Strong and organized low level wind shear resulted in damaging winds from one severe storm during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down on Route 84.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary resulted in moist and unstable air present over northeast Pennsylvania. An approaching upper level system provided the forcing needed to generate severe thunderstorms across northeast Pennsylvania. Organized shear and lift resulted in large hail along with severe wind reports.

A weak frontal boundary and a strong upper level disturbance helped develop numerous severe thunderstorms. Favorable and strong wind shear in the lower and middle layers of the atmosphere resulted in damaging winds across northeast Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on a house. Trees were either downed or uprooted, a boat on a lift was turned over and a flagpole was blown down on Page Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moving across the region led to the development of numerous severe thunderstorms across northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of July 8th. With a tremendous favorable atmospheric wind pattern present, severe thunderstorms resulted in widespread wind damage along with several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down. Time was estimated using radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary and a strong upper level disturbance helped develop numerous severe thunderstorms. Favorable and strong wind shear in the lower and middle layers of the atmosphere resulted in damaging winds across northeast Pennsylvania.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moving across the region led to the development of numerous severe thunderstorms across northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of July 8th. With a tremendous favorable atmospheric wind pattern present, severe thunderstorms resulted in widespread wind damage along with several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with estimated maximum wind speeds of 90 MPH...path width of 150 yards and a path length of 1.4 miles touched down just south of the Borough of Kingsley. Numerous large trees and power lines were knocked down with several homes being struck by large trees. A chimney was partially damaged on one home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep southerly flow transported a plume of seasonably high moisture into the region along a slow-moving, north-south oriented cold front. This favorable heavy rain pattern produced a stripe of 2.5-5.0 inches of rainfall from the northern Blue Ridge into the north-central mountains of Pennsylvania. Widespread small stream flooding and multiple road closures were reported with five river forecast points in the Juniata and Lower Main Stem Susquehanna (Tributaries) basins exceeding minor to moderate flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in excess of 3 inches produced widespread flooding. Route 287 was closed between Route 249 and Catlin Hollow Road in Middlebury Township. A temporary Road for Route 4017 at bridge project in Osceola Township was closed between Red House Hollow Road and Bulkley Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system located over southern Ontario along with surface boundaries in the vicinity of northeast Pennsylvania resulted in severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening hours.  These storms developed in an unstable airmass with severe thunderstorms producing large hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree was blown down on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed well ahead of a slow moving cold front. Some of the thunderstorms were severe, and also produced flash flooding across much of northern Venango county in Pennsylvania, with some flooding in Tuscarawas county Ohio.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over the Great Lakes/Ohio Valley progressed slowly east across the Mid-Atlantic and New England states during the afternoon and evening hours. Meanwhile at lower levels, a secondary cold frontal surge crossed the central Appalachians triggering another round of diurnal storms. Strong heating of a very moist, high PW airmass along with moderate deep shear and sufficient large scale forcing promoted organized thunderstorm clusters/bands which produced numerous reports of damaging winds and isolated, marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Spruce Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over the Great Lakes/Ohio Valley progressed slowly east across the Mid-Atlantic and New England states during the afternoon and evening hours. Meanwhile at lower levels, a secondary cold frontal surge crossed the central Appalachians triggering another round of diurnal storms. Strong heating of a very moist, high PW airmass along with moderate deep shear and sufficient large scale forcing promoted organized thunderstorm clusters/bands which produced numerous reports of damaging winds and isolated, marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half-dollar size hail (1.25 inch diameter) was reported in Mount Carmel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough over the Great Lakes/Ohio Valley progressed slowly east across the Mid-Atlantic and New England states during the afternoon and evening hours. Meanwhile at lower levels, a secondary cold frontal surge crossed the central Appalachians triggering another round of diurnal storms. Strong heating of a very moist, high PW airmass along with moderate deep shear and sufficient large scale forcing promoted organized thunderstorm clusters/bands which produced numerous reports of damaging winds and isolated, marginally severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Beavertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A semi-organized convective line and accompanying cool pool progressed eastward from the Upper Ohio Valley across central and southern PA during the late evening hours between 2000 and 2300 EDT. Thunderstorms along and just ahead of the line produced damaging wind gusts and large hail, consistent with reports of numerous downed trees and isolated quarter-to-golf ball sized hail (1-1.75 inches).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a few trees in Lottsville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with very heavy downpours produced flash flooding in the Lehigh Valley and far southeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. There were a couple of corridors of very heavy rain: one across the lower Lehigh Valley, a second across central Montgomery and Bucks Counties and the third and heaviest along the Delaware River in Philadelphia and Bucks Counties. In this last band, Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached 4 inches, while in the other two bands, Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 1.5 and 3.0 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding in south central Lehigh County. Several roadways were flooded in Allentown, South Whitehall Township, Emmaus and Upper Saucon Township.  Vehicles stalled trying to cross the flood waters.  In Allentown, this included one vehicle that became stuck in flood waters under a railroad bridge on South 8th Street. Flooded roadways included Walbert Avenue and Cedar Crest Boulevard in South Whitehall Township and Lehigh Street in Emmaus.  Event precipitation totals included 1.57 inches in Breinigsville, 1.39 inches in Macungie and 1.31 inches at the Allentown Queen City Airport. Most of this rain fell within an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary moved north across eastern Ohio and Western Pennsylvania producing scattered severe thunderstorms the evening of the 17th into the morning hours of the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front the afternoon of August 12th with scattered severe thunderstorms over eastern Ohio and western Pennsylvania. Minor road flooding also occurred across portions of Allegheny county in Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported one tree and wires down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved along a stalled boundary across eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia panhandle, and into western Pennsylvania producing numerous severe thunderstorms. An EF0 tornado also occurred over Clarion county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported some creeks out of their banks, roads covered with water, and some basement flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly flow and a surface low riding along a stalled cold front over Ohio, provided for the development of strong to severe thunderstorms in the afternoon and evening on May 14th. With strengthening shear, several storms maintained strong updrafts, producing quarter to baseball sized hail over Ohio and the northern West Virginia panhandle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State official reported one tree down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved along a stalled boundary across eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia panhandle, and into western Pennsylvania producing numerous severe thunderstorms. An EF0 tornado also occurred over Clarion county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported numerous trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm moist air ahead of a slow moving cold front supported storms with torrential rain June 23rd through midday on the 25th. Numerous thunderstorms produced rainfall amounts around 1 to 2 inches during that time. For areas that saw training thunderstorms rainfall amounts exceeded 4 inches. Heavy rain and floods the week before had kept grounds saturated in many areas, so this additional rainfall was sufficient to cause flash floods.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A series of storms moved over the Titusville area in southeast Crawford County PA on the night of the 25th. At the Titusville Water Works 3.28 inches of rain was measured. |Route #27 was being closed due to high water right outside the city limits. Basement flooding was reported throughout Titusville and the surrounding area.  Debris in the Oil Creek caused a three inch natural gas pipeline to rupture along Superior Street at the bridge and was repaired by National Fuel Gas Company. Several residents were in need of being rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain preceding a warm front during the early morning of the 13th caused flooding along a couple of creeks in Chester and Montgomery Counties and made the region vulnerable to additional heavier rain later that day. While the rain started to fall during the afternoon of the 12th, it fell at its heaviest during the early morning of the 13th and ended before 8 a.m. EDT on the 13th.  Heavy rain fell again later on the 13th.  Entire event (including the afternoon and evening rain on the 13th) precipitation totals averaged 1.5 to 2.0 inches in Chester County and 1.0 to 3.5 inches in Montgomery County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused minor flooding along the East Branch of the Brandywinre Creek. The East Branch of the Brandywine Creek below Downingtown was above its 7 foot flood stage from 624 a.m. EDT through 918 a.m. EDT on the morning of the 13th. It crested at 7.46 feet at 730 a.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 1.75 inches at East Nantmeal, 1.39 inches in Unionville and 1.30 inches in Spring City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a warm and humid air mass produced scattered severe thunderstorms across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For the second day in a row, an approaching cold front triggered a cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms across Eastern Pennsylvania mainly during the early evening of the 3rd.  PECO Energy reported about 150,000 of its customers lost power in southeast Pennsylvania. Hardest hit was Chester County with 59,000 outages.  Approximately 35,000 homes and businesses were still without power at Noon EDT on the 4th. All power was restored by the 6th. Many planned firework events on the 3rd were postponed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees in Glen Mills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold fronts triggered scattered thunderstorms over southeast PA during the afternoon on 5-6 September 2014. Deep moisture combined with strong instability and relatively slow storm motions/training to produce torrential rains and localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential thunderstorm rains and flash flooding closed SR 441 in Washington Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved along a stalled boundary across eastern Ohio, the northern West Virginia panhandle, and into western Pennsylvania producing numerous severe thunderstorms. An EF0 tornado also occurred over Clarion county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported numerous trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance combined with a warm and humid air mass produced scattered severe thunderstorms across eastern Ohio, northern West Virginia, western Pennsylvania, and Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported a tree blocking Burnsville Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level wave moving along a stalled front initiated thunderstorms that produced flash flooding and 2 weak tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 Call Center reported a vehicle caught in flash flooding with a driver rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Milder temperatures ahead of a cold front combined with moderate rainfall produced ice jam flooding on numerous streams and creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported an ice jam quickly formed on streams and creeks then broke near Bolivar and Fairfield Townships. Water was into 5 homes but quickly receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low-amplitude mid/upper level trough coupled with moderate instability allowed for thunderstorm development over the higher terrain of central PA during the late morning. Storms encountered a more favorable convective environment as they moved east during the afternoon, becoming more organized near edge of stronger northwesterly flow aloft and deep-layer shear. The storms that intensified to severe levels produced sporadic wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees near Coudersport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered scattered severe thunderstorms over the middle and lower Susquehanna River Valley during the mid-afternoon. A moderately favorable combination of instability and shear yielded multiple reports of large hail (some 2+ diameter) just east of the Danville area near the Montour-Columbia county line. The very large hail produced significant property damage.

A cold front triggered scattered severe thunderstorms over the middle and lower Susquehanna River Valley during the mid-afternoon. A moderately favorable combination of instability and shear yielded multiple reports of large hail (some 2+ diameter) just east of the Danville area near the Montour-Columbia county line. The very large hail produced significant property damage.

A cluster of slow moving thunderstorms with very heavy rain caused drainage and smaller creek flash flooding in southeastern Pennsylvania during the late afternoon and early evening of the 9th. Event totals where flash flooding occurred reached between two and three inches, most of which fell within an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused a water rescue from a trapped vehicle along the Mill Creek at the intersection of Old Gulph and Williamson Roads in Lower Merion Township.  The single occupant was safely rescued by fire department personnel. The precipitation event total in Lower Merion Township was 2.13 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system moving across the region led to the development of numerous severe thunderstorms across northeast Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening hours of July 8th. With a tremendous favorable atmospheric wind pattern present, severe thunderstorms resulted in widespread wind damage along with several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado with maximum wind speeds of 70 MPH was confirmed near this location. The tornado touched down in a rural area along State Route 4041 in Fairmount Township. The tornado destroyed a storage shed, a nearby garden and numerous trees along this road before moving through a wooded area and striking a dairy farm on Ridge Road. A roof was torn off of a barn and a grain silo was shifted off its foundation. The tornado crossed over corn and hay fields before lifting above a narrow valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Great Lakes and interacted with a warm and humid air mass over northeast Pennsylvania being drawn northward from Hurricane Arthur. Large hail fell with the severe thunderstorms that were able to develop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary resulted in moist and unstable air present over northeast Pennsylvania. An approaching upper level system provided the forcing needed to generate severe thunderstorms across northeast Pennsylvania. Organized shear and lift resulted in large hail along with severe wind reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down with wind gusts estimated close to 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey found damage from a micro-burst along a 1.4 mile long path along U.S. Highway 62 in Jackson Center. The maximum winds were estimated between 80 and 85 MPH, and the width of the damage path was around 50 yards. Damage consisted of numerous soft and hardwood trees snapped or uprooted, with roof damage to several family homes and garages.

A strong micro-burst also occurred in Mercer county Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey found damage from an EF1 tornado near Hoagland in Mercer county with estimated winds of 90 MPH. A sporadic damage path of almost 2.5 miles was found with a maximum path width of 150 yards. Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted, with roof damage to 2 barns and 1 home, as well as a metal fence blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved across Erie County, Pennsylvania as a cold front moved across the area. The thunderstorm caused many downed trees and was accompanied by quarter size hail on Old Frisbeetown Rd east of Mill Village.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported numerous trees and powerlines brought down from strong thunderstorm winds on Old Frisbeetown Road near Route 6. Quarter size hail was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of thunderstorms moved across northwest Pennsylvania during the afternoon of July 8 producing severe weather. These storms developed in the warm and unstable air ahead of an approaching cold front.  Strong thunderstorm winds brought down trees on houses and power lines in Millcreek Township with additional trees down on powerlines in the city of Erie.  Thunderstorm winds also brought down trees in Corry, with numerous power lines downed by the tree damage.  Trees were reported down both in the center of town and on Sciota Street.  Power was reportedly out for more than 24 hours in some areas.  Additional tree damage occurred in Titusville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported thunderstorm winds blew a tree down on a house in Millcreek Township along with trees down on power lines in the city of Erie.
